title: Q150 questionSubType is 'literary-device' but question asks about main message/theme
description: Q150 asks 'What is the main message of this story?' about The Bake Off passage and is classified as questionSubType 'literary-device'. However, this is a classic author-purpose/theme question, not a literary device question. Literary device questions ask about specific techniques (simile, metaphor, personification, alliteration). Theme/message questions are about the overarching meaning the author wants to convey. This misclassification affects the sub-type distribution statistics and lesson mapping accuracy.
suggestedFix: Change questionSubType from 'literary-device' to 'author-purpose' to match the actual skill being tested.
subject: english

title: Q156 questionSubType is 'prediction' which is not a standard comprehension sub-type
description: Q156 asks 'Based on the passage, what would most likely happen if all bees disappeared from Britain?' and is classified as questionSubType 'prediction'. While prediction is a valid reading skill, it is not one of the declared sub-types in the data model (retrieval, inference, vocabulary-in-context, word-class, literary-device, author-purpose). This should be classified as 'inference' since the child must use textual evidence to reason about a hypothetical scenario. GL tests this type of question under inference, not as a separate category.
suggestedFix: Change questionSubType from 'prediction' to 'inference'. The explanation already demonstrates inference reasoning from textual evidence.
subject: english

title: Q154 questionSubType 'negative-retrieval' is non-standard
description: Q154 asks 'Which of the following is NOT mentioned as a task performed by worker bees?' and is classified as 'negative-retrieval'. While functionally this is retrieval (the child scans the text to check what IS mentioned), 'negative-retrieval' is not one of the declared sub-types. GL does use 'NOT' questions in comprehension, but they are classified under retrieval. Using non-standard sub-types fragments the data and affects mapping accuracy.
suggestedFix: Change questionSubType from 'negative-retrieval' to 'retrieval'. Add a note in the explanation that this is a 'NOT' question requiring elimination of all options that ARE mentioned.
subject: english

title: Questions about structure/contrast classified under 'literary-device' rather than 'author-purpose'
description: Several questions asking about passage structure (Q21, Q69), author's contrast techniques (Q25), or tension-building methods (Q6) are classified as 'literary-device'. In GL marking schemes, questions about HOW the author structures a passage or builds tension fall under 'authorial technique/purpose' rather than 'literary device' (which specifically means simile, metaphor, personification, etc.). For example, Q69 asks 'How does the author use the structure of the passage to guide the reader?' — this is about text organisation, not a literary device. Q6 asks 'How does the author build tension?' — this is about narrative technique. This conflation makes it difficult to accurately assess sub-type coverage.
suggestedFix: Distinguish between 'literary-device' (simile, metaphor, personification, alliteration, onomatopoeia) and 'author-technique' or reclassify structural/contrast questions under 'author-purpose'. GL treats both as higher-order comprehension skills but they test different abilities.
subject: english

title: Two separate Roman baths passages cover very similar content
description: Passage 'roman-baths' (The Roman Baths, Q15-Q21) and passage 'roman-baths-sulis' (The Roman Baths of Aquae Sulis, Q64-Q69) cover the same topic with significant factual overlap. Both mention Aquae Sulis, 46-degree water temperature, the bathing sequence (tepidarium, caldarium, frigidarium), social life at the baths, and 18th-century rediscovery. Several questions are near-duplicates: Q15 and Q65 both ask about water temperature; Q16 and Q64 both ask about the Roman name. Children encountering both passages would face repetitive content, which reduces the breadth of topics tested.
suggestedFix: Consolidate into a single passage with a larger question set (10-12 questions) or replace one passage with a different historical topic (e.g., the Great Fire of London, Pompeii, Anglo-Saxon England).
subject: english

title: Two separate hedgehog passages cover overlapping content
description: Passage 'hedgehogs' (Saving the Hedgehogs, Q11-Q14) and passage 'hedgehog-life' (The Secret Life of Hedgehogs, Q34-Q39) both cover hedgehog biology and population decline. Both mention the population drop from thirty million, curling-into-a-ball defence, and the need for conservation. While the second passage provides more biological detail, a child encountering both would experience content repetition.
suggestedFix: Replace one passage with a different non-fiction animal topic (e.g., migration, coral reefs, endangered species) to improve topic diversity.
subject: english

title: All 410 mapping entries show '[No lesson found]' for lessonTeachSummary
description: Every efficacy pair in the mapping file shows lessonTeachSummary as '[No lesson found]', despite the lessons file containing 20 well-structured lessons across 10 sub-concepts. The lessons clearly exist and cover all the core skills (retrieval, inference, vocab-in-context, author-purpose, literary-devices). This appears to be an extraction issue where the mapping script could not locate the teach screen summary for the matched lesson. The mapping confidence is 100% high (410/410), and the sub-concept IDs are correct, so the mapping logic itself is working — it is just the teach summary field that failed to populate.
suggestedFix: Debug the extraction script's teach-summary lookup. The lessons file has teach screens with body content for each lesson — the script may be looking for a different field name or structure.
subject: english

title: 3 orphan sub-concepts have lessons but may not be receiving questions
description: The mapping metadata shows 3 orphan sub-concepts. The lessons file has 10 sub-concepts: retrieval, inference, vocab-in-context, author-purpose, literary-devices, word-class, text-structure, summary, comparison, evaluation. The questions use sub-types: retrieval, inference, vocabulary-in-context, author-purpose, literary-device, negative-retrieval, prediction. Some sub-concepts likely have lessons but no direct question mapping (e.g., text-structure, summary, comparison). This is a minor gap — these skills are tested implicitly within other question types.
suggestedFix: Review which 3 sub-concepts are orphans and either add questions that map to them or note them as 'cross-cutting skills' that don't need dedicated questions.
subject: english

title: Questions 380-410 use a noticeably more informal, chatty explanation style
description: The later questions (approximately Q380-Q410) use a more conversational explanation style compared to the earlier questions. For example, Q389 says 'it hooks us into wanting to read more!' and Q393 says 'This is 'show, don't tell' in action!' compared to the more measured tone of earlier explanations. While both styles are age-appropriate and engaging, the inconsistency in register between earlier (formal-educational) and later (chatty-enthusiastic) questions is noticeable. This suggests they were written in different batches.
suggestedFix: No action required — both tones work well for the target age group. If consistency is desired, a light editing pass could standardise the register. The chatty style is arguably more engaging for a 9-year-old.
subject: english

title: Q35 (hibernacula) rated D1 but requires knowledge of specialist vocabulary
description: Q35 asks 'What is the name given to a hedgehog's nest?' with the answer 'A hibernacula'. While the answer is directly stated in the passage (making it technically retrieval/D1), the word 'hibernacula' is highly unusual specialist vocabulary that a 9-year-old would not know. The distractors (burrow, warren, lodge, den) are all common words that children might reasonably associate with animal homes. A child might select a wrong answer based on prior knowledge (e.g., 'burrow' or 'den') rather than scanning the passage. This makes it feel harder than D1 despite being pure retrieval.
suggestedFix: This is borderline — it IS pure retrieval (the answer is stated directly), which justifies D1. However, consider upgrading to D2 since the unfamiliar vocabulary adds a second cognitive demand. No change strictly required.
subject: english

title: Q32 classified as 'vocabulary-in-context' but asks about personification effect
description: Q32 asks 'What qualities does the poet give the spider by calling her a patient queen?' and is classified as 'vocabulary-in-context'. However, this question is really about the EFFECT of personification — giving human qualities to an animal. The word 'patient' has a clear meaning; the question is about what the personification technique achieves, which is closer to 'literary-device' or 'inference'. This minor misclassification does not affect the child's experience but impacts sub-type analytics.
suggestedFix: Reclassify as 'literary-device' (personification effect) or 'inference' (what the word choice implies about the spider).
subject: english

title: Q3 and Q21 are near-duplicates testing 'neither...nor'
description: Q3 (D2): 'Neither the teacher ___ the pupils knew the answer.' Q21 (D3): 'Neither the teacher ___ the students were happy about the cancelled trip.' Both test exactly the same concept (neither...nor correlative conjunctions) with virtually the same sentence structure and same answer ('nor'). The distractors are identical (or, nor, and, but, with). Q21 is rated D3 but is no harder than Q3 — both have the same pattern and same clear answer. This wastes a question slot and inflates the neither/nor representation.
suggestedFix: Remove Q21 or change it to test a genuinely different grammar concept at D3. If neither...nor needs a D3 question, make it test verb agreement with neither...nor (e.g. 'Neither the boys nor the teacher ___ the answer' where the answer depends on which subject is closest to the verb).
subject: english

title: Q4 and Q25 are near-duplicates testing subjunctive 'If I were'
description: Q4 (D2): 'If I ___ taller, I could reach the top shelf.' Q25 (D2): 'If I ___ you, I would apologise immediately.' Both test the subjunctive 'were' in hypothetical conditionals, both at D2, both with identical options (am, was, were, is, be) and identical answer ('were'). This is unnecessary duplication.
suggestedFix: Remove one or change one to test a different subjunctive pattern (e.g. 'I wish I ___ taller' or 'He acts as if he ___ the boss').
subject: english

title: Q6 and Q27 test same 'intervening phrase' pattern at D3
description: Q6 (D3): 'The team, along with their coach, ___ arriving at noon.' Q27 (D3): 'The orchestra, together with the choir, ___ performing at the concert tonight.' Both test subject-verb agreement where an intervening phrase ('along with' / 'together with') does not change the singular subject. Same answer ('is'), same difficulty, same grammatical principle.
suggestedFix: Remove one or change one to test a different subject-verb agreement trap (e.g. 'A number of students ___ absent today' where the answer is 'were' — testing 'a number of' = plural).
subject: english

title: Q18 (adverb 'happily') mapped to 'Expanded Noun Phrases'
description: Q18: 'The children played ___ in the garden all afternoon.' (answer: 'happily') is mapped to sub-concept 'expanded-noun-phrases'. This question tests adverb formation (adjective 'happy' -> adverb 'happily'), not noun phrase expansion. The lesson linked ('How adjectives, prepositional phrases, and relative clauses turn a boring noun into a mini movie') is irrelevant to this question.
suggestedFix: Remap to a more appropriate sub-concept. If no 'adverb formation' sub-concept exists, consider creating one or mapping to 'should-have' (Common Grammar Traps) as a catch-all for word form questions.
subject: english

title: Q19 (It's vs Its) mapped to 'Pronouns' but is really about apostrophes/homophones
description: Q19: '___ going to rain this afternoon, so bring an umbrella.' (answer: 'It's') is mapped to sub-concept 'pronouns'. While 'its' is technically a pronoun, this question tests the distinction between 'it's' (contraction of 'it is') and 'its' (possessive). The linked lesson about 'Sophie and I' vs 'Sophie and me' is not relevant. This concept is better covered by the punctuation topic's 'it's vs its' lessons.
suggestedFix: Consider flagging this as a cross-topic question or creating a dedicated 'homophones and confusable words' sub-concept in grammar that covers it's/its, their/there/they're, your/you're, etc.
subject: english

title: Q17 (fewer/less) mapped to 'Comparatives and Superlatives' but is about countable vs uncountable
description: Q17: 'There are ___ apples in the bowl than there were yesterday.' (answer: 'fewer') is mapped to 'comparative-superlative'. While 'fewer' is technically comparative, the grammar concept being tested is the countable/uncountable distinction (fewer for countable, less for uncountable). The linked lesson about -er/-est vs more/most is not directly relevant.
suggestedFix: Remap to 'should-have' (Common Grammar Traps) which already covers fewer/less per its learning goals, or create a dedicated 'countable vs uncountable' sub-concept.
subject: english

title: Q24 ('homework needs') rated D3 but is a straightforward singular noun question
description: Q24 (D3): 'The homework ___ to be handed in by Friday.' (answer: 'needs'). While 'homework' being uncountable is a valid grammar point, the sentence is straightforward and the answer is obvious to any child who speaks English natively — 'the homework need' sounds clearly wrong. The distractors (need, needs, needing, needed, are needing) include obviously wrong forms. This is more D2 level.
suggestedFix: Reclassify as D2, or make it genuinely D3 by adding a more confusing context (e.g. 'The homework assignments ___ to be completed' where 'assignments' might tempt children toward 'need' despite the real subject).
subject: english

title: Q8 ('which' for things) rated D3 but the who/which distinction is D2
description: Q8 (D3): 'The old house, ___ had stood on the hill for centuries, was finally demolished.' (answer: 'which'). The who vs which distinction (who for people, which for things) is a Year 4-5 concept and more appropriately D2. D3 relative pronoun questions should test 'whose' vs 'who's', 'whom' in complex structures, or 'that' vs 'which' in restrictive/non-restrictive contexts.
suggestedFix: Reclassify as D2.
subject: english

title: Q2 explanation mentions 'fast' as an adjective but the context uses it as an adverb
description: Q2: 'She ran ___ than anyone else in the race.' (answer: 'faster'). Explanation says 'we use "-er" not "more" for short adjectives.' However, in this sentence 'faster' is functioning as an adverb (modifying the verb 'ran'), not an adjective. While the comparative form is the same, the explanation could reinforce a misconception that 'faster' is always an adjective.
suggestedFix: Update explanation to: 'When comparing two things, we use the comparative form "faster". For short words like "fast", we add "-er" rather than saying "more fast". Here, "faster" describes how she ran (an adverb).'
subject: english

title: Q35 explanation about collective nouns may confuse with Q6's explanation
description: Q35 (D2): 'The team ___ celebrating their victory on the pitch.' (answer: 'are'). Q6 (D3): 'The team, along with their coach, ___ arriving at noon.' (answer: 'is'). Both use 'the team' as subject but have opposite answers. Q35's explanation says teams take plural verbs when members act individually ('the clue is the word their'), while Q6 says the subject is singular. While both explanations are technically correct (collective nouns can be singular or plural in British English), having them side by side without explicit acknowledgement of the paradox could confuse learners.
suggestedFix: Add a note to Q35's explanation: 'In British English, collective nouns like "team" can take either a singular or plural verb. When you think of the group acting as individuals (celebrating, arguing), use a plural verb. When you think of it as a single unit (arriving together), use a singular verb.' This helps the child understand why both patterns exist.
subject: english

title: No questions on standard English vs non-standard dialect
description: GL tests sometimes include questions about standard vs non-standard English (e.g. 'We was going' vs 'We were going' in dialect contexts, or 'them books' vs 'those books'). The current question bank does not explicitly test this — while many subject-verb agreement questions implicitly test standard English forms, none frame it as standard vs non-standard.
suggestedFix: Consider adding 3-5 questions at D2/D3 that explicitly test standard English awareness: 'Which sentence uses standard English?' with options including common non-standard forms (we was, done it, should of, ain't, them books).
subject: english

title: No questions testing double negatives
description: GL occasionally tests awareness of double negatives ('I haven't got no money' vs 'I haven't got any money'). This is a standard English concept tested at Year 5-6 level. No questions in the current bank address this.
suggestedFix: Add 2-3 questions testing double negatives at D2 level, e.g. 'Choose the correct version: I haven't done nothing / I haven't done anything.'
subject: english

title: 30 medium-confidence question-to-lesson mappings need review
description: 385 questions are mapped with 355 high-confidence and 30 medium-confidence pairings. While medium confidence is not critical, these mappings may link questions to sub-optimal lessons. The 30 medium-confidence mappings should be reviewed to ensure the lesson linked is the most relevant one for each question.
suggestedFix: Review the 30 medium-confidence mappings individually and either confirm them as correct or reassign to a more appropriate sub-concept.
subject: english

title: Q7 marked 'No mistake' but sentence needs commas around non-defining relative clause
description: Q7 segments form: 'My sister who is seven years old loves reading books about animals.' This is marked as 'No mistake' (correct=4). However, 'who is seven years old' is a non-defining relative clause — my sister is a specific, unique person, so the clause is extra information, not defining. It should be set off with commas: 'My sister, who is seven years old, loves reading books about animals.' The answer should be Segment A (missing comma after 'sister') or Segment B (missing comma after 'old').
suggestedFix: Either change the correct answer to indicate where commas are needed, or rephrase to use a defining context (e.g. 'The girl who is seven years old loves reading') where commas are genuinely not needed.
subject: english

title: Q18 marked 'No mistake' but sentence needs commas around non-defining relative clause
description: Q18 segments form: 'My grandparents who live in Scotland are coming to visit us next week.' Marked as 'No mistake'. However, 'who live in Scotland' is non-defining — if you only have one set of grandparents, the clause is extra information and needs commas. At minimum this is ambiguous, and at D2 level this is exactly the kind of question GL uses to test understanding of parenthetical commas.
suggestedFix: Either change the correct answer to flag the missing commas, or change the subject to make the clause genuinely defining (e.g. 'The neighbours who live next door are coming to visit' where 'who live next door' distinguishes these neighbours from others).
subject: english

title: Q116 marked 'No mistake' but segment D contains 'new' instead of 'knew'
description: Q116 segments: 'The teacher asked / the class a question / but nobody / new the answer.' This is marked 'No mistake' (correct=4) with explanation 'The apostrophe is in exactly the right place.' However, 'new the answer' is clearly wrong — it should be 'knew the answer'. While this is technically a spelling/homophone error rather than punctuation, the question claims no mistake exists. The explanation about apostrophes is also irrelevant since no apostrophe appears in the sentence.
suggestedFix: Change the correct answer to Segment D and update the explanation: "'new' should be 'knew'. 'Knew' is the past tense of 'know', while 'new' means 'not old'. These homophones sound the same but mean completely different things."
subject: english

title: Capital letter explanations show lowercase correction instead of capitalised version
description: Approximately 20 capital letter questions have explanations that say the word 'should be' followed by the lowercase version. For example, Q12: "'paris' needs a capital letter — it should be 'paris'" (should say 'Paris'). Q27: "'saturday' needs a capital letter — it should be 'saturday'" (should say 'Saturday'). Q58: "'edinburgh' needs a capital letter — it should be 'edinburgh'" (should say 'Edinburgh'). The correct answer in all cases IS correct — it's only the explanation text that fails to show the capitalised form. This is a systematic template bug where the explanation inserts the original error rather than the correction.
suggestedFix: Fix the explanation template so that the 'should be' text shows the capitalised form. For each affected question, change the explanation to show the word with a capital letter (e.g. 'Paris' not 'paris').
subject: english

title: Q2 and Q13 have wrong explanation for the type of error present
description: Q2 segments: 'James said / "I really enjoyed / the film we watched / yesterday."' — the error is in Segment A: there should be a comma after 'said' before the opening speech marks. But the explanation says 'A comma is needed after the opening part of the sentence' (about fronted adverbials), which is the wrong rule. Similarly Q13 segments: '"I love swimming" / said Amara / "because it makes / me feel so free."' — the error is missing comma after 'swimming' before closing speech mark, but gets the same fronted adverbial explanation. Both are actually speech punctuation errors.
suggestedFix: Q2: Change explanation to: 'A comma is needed after "said" before the speech marks: James said, "I really enjoyed..." When introducing direct speech with a reporting verb, always put a comma before the opening speech marks.' Q13: Change explanation to: 'A comma is needed after "swimming" inside the speech marks: "I love swimming," said Amara. When speech comes before the reporting verb, it ends with a comma (not a full stop) inside the speech marks.'
subject: english

title: Speech punctuation errors given wrong 'fronted adverbial' explanation
description: Q200 ('Gran exclaimed / "What a lovely...'), Q202 ('The headteacher replied / "The school fair..."), Q204 ('The librarian asked / "Has anyone seen...'), Q206 ('Dad announced / "We're going on...'): All have the error in Segment A — missing comma after the reporting verb before speech marks. But all are given the explanation 'A comma is needed after the opening part of the sentence' which describes fronted adverbials, not speech punctuation. The correct rule is that a comma is needed between the reporting clause and the direct speech.
suggestedFix: Change explanations to describe the speech punctuation rule: 'A comma is needed after the reporting verb before the opening speech marks. We write: Gran exclaimed, "What a lovely surprise!" The comma tells the reader that speech is about to begin.'
subject: english

title: Q10 explanation describes commas in lists but the actual error is a misused semicolon
description: Q10 segments: 'There are two things / I enjoy most; / swimming in the sea / and climbing mountains.' The semicolon after 'most' is incorrect — a colon should be used to introduce a list. But the explanation says 'Commas are needed to separate the items in this list' which is about list comma separation, not about the colon vs semicolon distinction. The actual punctuation error is the misuse of the semicolon.
suggestedFix: Change explanation to: 'A colon (:) should be used here, not a semicolon (;). When you introduce a list with a statement like "the following things" or "two things", use a colon to announce the list. A semicolon joins two complete sentences — it does not introduce lists.'
subject: english

title: Q40 'No mistake' explanation mentions apostrophe but no apostrophe exists in sentence
description: Q40 (correct=4, 'No mistake'): 'I know you have / been practising hard / for the spelling test / all this week.' Explanation says 'This one's a trick — there's actually nothing wrong here! The apostrophe is in exactly the right place.' But the sentence contains no apostrophe at all. The explanation text is a generic template that does not match this question.
suggestedFix: Change explanation to: 'This one's a trick — there's actually nothing wrong here! All the punctuation is correct.'
subject: english

title: Q25 tests spelling/word choice (there/their), not punctuation
description: Q25 segments: 'The teachers asked / the students to / complete there homework / before Monday morning.' The error is 'there' instead of 'their' — this is a homophone/spelling error, not a punctuation error. The explanation even acknowledges this: 'This is technically a spelling/grammar error but commonly tested in punctuation sections.' While GL does occasionally embed homophones in punctuation sections, having this in a punctuation-only question bank without clear labelling may confuse learners about what constitutes punctuation.
suggestedFix: Consider moving this to the grammar/spelling topic, or adding a tag/note that this tests homophones rather than punctuation marks. If kept, ensure the explanation clearly distinguishes this from punctuation errors.
subject: english

title: Q26 explanation claims comma exists where there is none
description: Q26 segments: 'The concert which / featured three local bands / was held in / the town hall.' The explanation says: 'That comma shouldn't be there! The information in this part of the sentence is essential...' But Segment A ('The concert which') does not contain a comma. If the intended error is the ABSENCE of a comma (that 'The concert, which' should have a comma because it's non-defining), then the answer and explanation are internally contradictory. If the concert is one specific concert, 'which featured three local bands' is non-defining and SHOULD have commas.
suggestedFix: Clarify whether this question tests restrictive vs non-restrictive clauses. If the intent is that commas are missing (non-defining clause), change the explanation to reflect that. If the intent is that no commas are needed (defining clause), the explanation should not reference a comma that doesn't exist. Consider marking as 'No mistake' if the clause is intended as defining, or adding commas to other segments if it's intended as non-defining.
subject: english

title: Q78 same restrictive clause ambiguity as Q26
description: Q78 segments: 'The old cottage which / had stood for centuries / was finally sold / to a young couple.' Explanation says 'That comma shouldn't be there' but no comma exists in Segment A. Same issue pattern as Q26 — the explanation references removing a comma that isn't present. Whether 'which had stood for centuries' is restrictive or non-restrictive depends on context, and at D3 this is a legitimate GL trap, but the explanation must match the actual content of the segments.
suggestedFix: Either add commas around the relative clause in the segments (making the error their presence) and keep the explanation, or rewrite the explanation to address what error is actually present. If 'No mistake' is intended because this is a restrictive clause, change the answer to 4.
subject: english

title: Q77 says commas are needed but segments already contain commas
description: Q77 segments: 'We need flour, sugar / butter, eggs and / milk to bake / the birthday cake.' Answer is Segment A, explanation says 'Commas are needed to separate the items in this list.' But Segment A already has a comma after 'flour' and Segment B has one after 'butter'. The actual error appears to be a missing comma after 'sugar' (Segment A ends with 'sugar' but no comma before 'butter' in Segment B). The explanation is misleading because it suggests no commas exist at all.
suggestedFix: Update explanation to be specific: 'A comma is needed after "sugar" to separate it from "butter" in the list. The list should read: flour, sugar, butter, eggs and milk.'
subject: english

title: Excessive repetition of D1 missing-apostrophe-in-contraction questions
description: Of 128 D1 questions, approximately 70-80 test the same pattern: a contraction missing its apostrophe (don't, can't, won't, isn't, hasn't, weren't, etc.). Many use nearly identical sentence structures and the same boilerplate explanation ('missing its apostrophe... think of it as a little flag saying letters were here!'). While apostrophe contractions are important, this level of repetition provides diminishing returns and crowds out other D1 punctuation concepts (comma in simple list, question mark vs full stop, capital letter at start of sentence).
suggestedFix: Reduce contraction apostrophe questions at D1 to ~30 (from ~70+) and replace with more variety: simple comma-in-list errors, missing question marks, missing full stops, and capital letter errors at sentence start. This would better reflect the range of D1 punctuation errors GL tests.
subject: english

title: Heavy answer position bias in D2 question ranges
description: In the Q200-220 range, at least 15 consecutive questions have the correct answer at position 0 (Segment A) or position 1 (Segment B). GL distributes errors roughly evenly across all four segments. Long runs of the same answer position allow test-wise children to spot patterns and game the answers, which is not authentic to the GL experience.
suggestedFix: Redistribute the answer positions across A/B/C/D more evenly in the D2 question range. Ensure no more than 3-4 consecutive questions share the same answer position.
subject: english

title: Q85 explanation incorrectly uses capital letter template for start-of-sentence capital
description: Q85 segments: '"Can we go to the / park after lunch?" / asked Ben. "yes, I / think we can," said Dad.' The error is 'yes' needing a capital 'Y' because it starts a new sentence of direct speech. But the explanation says 'Names of people, places, days of the week, and months always start with a capital letter' — this is the proper noun template, not the start-of-sentence template. The capital is needed because 'Yes' starts a new sentence, not because it's a proper noun.
suggestedFix: Change explanation to: '"yes" needs a capital letter because it starts a new sentence of direct speech. After the reporting clause ("asked Ben"), the next piece of speech begins a new sentence and must start with a capital letter: "Yes, I think we can."'
subject: english

title: Q86 explanation uses proper noun template for start-of-sentence capital
description: Q86 segments: '"Watch out!" cried Sam. / "That branch / is about to fall." / "thanks for warning me."' — 'thanks' needs a capital because it starts a new sentence of direct speech. Explanation incorrectly applies the proper noun rule.
suggestedFix: Same fix as Q85 — use start-of-sentence rule in explanation, not the proper noun rule.
subject: english

title: Q84 explanation describes fronted adverbial but the error is missing comma in interrupted speech
description: Q84 segments: '"Please sit down," / said Mrs Harris / "and open your / books to page ten."' — the error is in Segment B: a comma is needed after 'Harris' before resuming speech ('said Mrs Harris, "and open...'). The explanation describes fronted adverbials ('when a sentence doesn't start with the main action'). The correct rule is about punctuating interrupted direct speech.
suggestedFix: Change explanation to: 'A comma is needed after "Harris" before the speech continues. When speech is interrupted by a reporting clause (said Mrs Harris), you need a comma after the reporting clause before the speech marks reopen.'
subject: english

title: Q62 (capital letter for 'Aunt Sarah') rated D1 but involves a nuanced capitalisation rule
description: Q62 tests capitalisation of 'aunt' in 'aunt Sarah'. The rule for capitalising family titles (Aunt Sarah vs 'my aunt') is actually a D2 concept — it requires understanding that titles used as names (Aunt Sarah) get capitals, but generic references (my aunt) do not. At D1, capital letter questions should test obvious cases like days, months, and place names.
suggestedFix: Reclassify as D2.
subject: english

title: Q74 tests debatable comma-before-'but' rule at D2
description: Q74 segments: 'The bag contained / a pencil, a rubber, / a ruler and a / sharpener but no eraser.' Explanation says comma needed before 'but'. However, the clause after 'but' ('no eraser') is not an independent clause — it's a phrase. The 'comma before but' rule only applies when 'but' joins two independent clauses. This is a borderline case that could confuse learners.
suggestedFix: Either reclassify as D3 (where ambiguous comma rules are appropriate) or change the sentence so 'but' clearly joins two independent clauses (e.g. 'The bag was full, but the eraser was missing.').
subject: english

title: Q4 (irregular plural possessive) mapped to 'Apostrophes in Contractions' instead of 'Apostrophes for Possession'
description: Q4 tests 'childrens'' vs 'children's' — an irregular plural possession question. But it is mapped to sub-concept 'apostrophe-contraction' (Apostrophes in Contractions). This should be mapped to 'apostrophe-possession' (Apostrophes for Possession) since the error is about possessive apostrophes, not contractions.
suggestedFix: Change mapping to sub-concept 'apostrophe-possession'.
subject: english

title: 'No mistake' questions may be under-represented or lack challenge
description: The Oracle recommends 15-20% of punctuation questions should have 'No mistake' as the answer. Several 'No mistake' questions use simple sentences with no tricky-looking punctuation, making them too easy — the child isn't tempted to pick an error. GL's 'No mistake' questions work by including correctly-punctuated elements that look wrong (e.g. Thomas' pencil case, both teachers' classrooms) to create doubt. Some questions in this set do this well (Q47, Q49, Q52) but others (Q40, Q65, Q124) have entirely straightforward sentences.
suggestedFix: Ensure 'No mistake' questions consistently include at least one element that children commonly doubt (irregular possessives, its/it's used correctly, commas that look optional). Remove 'No mistake' questions with entirely simple sentences.
subject: english

title: One orphan sub-concept has no questions mapped to it
description: The mappings file reports 1 orphan sub-concept — a lesson sub-concept that exists but has no questions mapped to it. This means the lesson teaches something that is never tested, which is a wasted learning opportunity.
suggestedFix: Identify the orphan sub-concept and either create questions for it or merge its content into an existing sub-concept that does have questions.
subject: english

title: Double-letters hook screen renders 'undefined' in SentenceDisplay
description: The double-letters-steps lesson hook screen uses SentenceDisplay with mode 'highlight' and text 'undefined  or  undefined?'. The highlightWords array contains two entries that have 'color' properties but no 'word' properties: [{"color": "#e74c3c"}, {"color": "#22c55e"}]. This means the visual will display the literal text 'undefined or undefined?' instead of showing the two spelling options (e.g., 'running or runing?'). The lesson content in the body text ('Is it "running" or "runing"?') covers the same ground, but the visual is broken.
suggestedFix: Add 'word' properties to the highlightWords array: [{"word": "runing", "color": "#e74c3c"}, {"word": "running", "color": "#22c55e"}]. Update the text property to 'runing  or  running?'.
subject: english

title: Options say 'Section A-D' but correctAnswer says 'Segment A-D'
description: All 420 questions have options labelled 'Section A', 'Section B', 'Section C', 'Section D', 'No mistake' but the correctAnswer field uses 'Segment A', 'Segment B', etc. This terminology inconsistency could cause issues if the app renders the correctAnswer text directly (e.g., in a results summary or explanation prefix). The child sees 'Section C' but the data says 'Segment C'. While 'correct' field (0-indexed integer) is the authoritative answer, the text mismatch is a data quality issue.
suggestedFix: Standardise on one term throughout. Since GL uses 'Section', change all correctAnswer values from 'Segment X' to 'Section X' (or vice versa — pick one and be consistent).
subject: english

title: Q2 explanation incorrectly describes the spelling rule for 'lovely'
description: Q2 explanation states: 'When adding '-ly' to 'love', we drop the 'e' and add '-ly': love -> lovely.' This rule description is incorrect. When adding '-ly' to 'love', we do NOT drop the 'e' — 'lovely' retains the 'e' from 'love' (love + ly = lovely). The rule about dropping 'e' applies when adding vowel suffixes (love + ing = loving), not consonant suffixes. The '-ly' suffix starts with a consonant, so the 'e' is kept. The correct spelling 'lovely' is right, but the stated rule is wrong and could teach the child an incorrect principle.
suggestedFix: Change the explanation to: ''lovley' should be 'lovely'. When adding '-ly' to a word ending in 'e', we usually keep the 'e': love + ly = lovely. The common mistake is rearranging the 'e' and 'l'. Say the word slowly: love-ly.'
subject: english

title: Q25 (hight/high) rated D1 but 'hight' is not a realistic misspelling
description: Q25 presents 'hight street' where the answer is that 'hight' should be 'high'. However, 'hight' is not a common or realistic misspelling of 'high'. Children do not typically add a 't' to 'high'. GL error-spotting questions at all difficulty levels require the misspelling to be a genuine, commonly-made error. This misspelling feels artificial — it would be more natural as a homophone error (e.g., 'hire street' or misusing 'height' in the wrong context).
suggestedFix: Replace with a more realistic D1 error. Options: (a) use a genuine homophone error like 'buy/by' or 'no/know', (b) use a common simple misspelling like 'wen' for 'when' or 'sed' for 'said'.
subject: english

title: Q86 (Altho/Although) rated D1 but tests an unusual truncation error
description: Q86 presents 'Altho it was raining' where 'Altho' should be 'Although'. While 'although' is a common word, the error 'Altho' is more of an abbreviation/text-speak truncation than a genuine misspelling a 9-year-old would make in a written exam. Children are more likely to write 'allthough' or 'althow' than simply truncate the word. At D1, errors should be ones that children commonly make.
suggestedFix: Replace with a more realistic misspelling like 'allthough' or 'althoe', or swap the whole question for a different D1 error.
subject: english

title: 'No mistake' questions are unevenly distributed across difficulty levels
description: Reviewing a sample of 'no mistake' questions: Q5 (D2), Q20 (D2), Q26 (D3), Q44 (D2), Q47 (D1), Q81 (D3), Q99 (D1), Q306 (D3). The Oracle specification states 'no mistake' should be 15-20% of total and 'more common at D2/D3' with 'rare at D1'. There appear to be a few D1 'no mistake' questions (Q47, Q99), which is acceptable in small numbers. However, the D1 'no mistake' questions use relatively simple correctly-spelled words (Q47: practice, Wednesday; Q99: colourful, gracefully, butterfly) that don't create enough doubt. GL D1 'no mistake' questions still include at least one word that LOOKS tricky (e.g., 'beautiful' or 'because') to create a moment of hesitation.
suggestedFix: Review D1 'no mistake' questions and ensure each contains at least one correctly-spelled word that children commonly doubt (e.g., because, beautiful, Wednesday (which Q47 does include), different, friends). Q99's words (colourful, gracefully) are good. The distribution overall appears reasonable.
subject: english

title: Homophones may be over-represented relative to GL weighting
description: Homophones appear to represent approximately 25-28% of questions based on sampling, while the Oracle specification targets ~20%. The first 50 questions alone contain roughly 15 homophone questions (30%). This is a minor over-representation. GL tests homophones at ~20% and balances with other categories like suffixes, prefixes, and Latin/Greek roots. The homophone questions themselves are high quality — they cover the full range (their/there/they're, here/hear, to/too/two, weather/whether, passed/past, its/it's, your/you're, affect/effect, practice/practise, principal/principle, stationary/stationery, aloud/allowed, whose/who's, threw/through, wood/would, our/hour, new/knew, wear/where, compliment/complement).
suggestedFix: If exact GL weighting is desired, reduce homophones by ~5-8% and increase suffixes (-tion/-sion/-cian, -ible/-able) and prefix questions to compensate. However, this is a minor calibration issue and homophones are genuinely the most commonly tested GL spelling category.
subject: english

title: Q32 explanation for its/it's is slightly misleading
description: Q32 presents 'when it's owner came home' where 'it's' should be 'its'. The explanation says: "'Its' shows possession (the dog's owner). 'It's' is short for 'it is' or 'it has'." The parenthetical '(the dog's owner)' is confusing because it introduces the apostrophe-s possessive pattern from nouns (dog's) in the same breath as explaining that 'its' (possessive) does NOT have an apostrophe. A child might think: 'But dog's has an apostrophe for possession, so why doesn't its?' The explanation should address this directly as it is the exact trap GL exploits.
suggestedFix: Revise explanation to: "'it's' should be 'its'. This is the trickiest one! 'Its' means belonging to it (no apostrophe needed). 'It's' always means 'it is' or 'it has'. Unlike 'dog's' or 'Sarah's', the possessive 'its' breaks the normal apostrophe rule. Remember: if you can replace it with 'it is', use 'it's'. If not, use 'its'."
subject: english

title: Q93 explanation for 'occasionally' says double 'c' and double 'l' but the word has single 'l'
description: Q93 explanation states: "'ocasionally' should be 'occasionally'. It needs double 'c' and double 'l': oc-CA-sion-al-ly." The word 'occasionally' has double 'c' and double 'l' when you look at the full word (occasional + ly = occasionally), so the final word does have 'll'. However, the syllable breakdown 'oc-CA-sion-al-ly' suggests the double 'l' is in '-al-ly' which is actually the '-ally' suffix. The explanation is technically correct but the emphasis on 'double l' could be misleading since children might think there's a double 'l' in the root word rather than it being formed by 'al' + 'ly'.
suggestedFix: Clarify: "'ocasionally' should be 'occasionally'. It needs double 'c': oc-CA-sion-ally. The root is 'occasion' (double c), then add '-ally'."
subject: english

title: Q33 has a long Segment D that may display poorly
description: Q33's Segment D is 'we have planned for you're birthday.' — this is noticeably longer than the other segments ('She said that', 'you are going to', 'love the surprise'). In GL format, segments are roughly equal in length so no segment stands out visually. A very long segment might draw the child's eye and bias them toward selecting it (or away from it). The error IS in this segment, so the length could make it either too obvious or create visual imbalance.
suggestedFix: Consider restructuring the sentence to balance segment lengths, e.g.: Segment A: 'She said you are', B: 'going to love', C: 'the surprise we planned', D: 'for you're birthday.'
subject: english

title: Q39 practice/practise explanation should emphasise UK-specific rule
description: Q39 tests the practice/practise distinction: 'needs more practise' should be 'needs more practice' (noun). The explanation correctly states the rule but does not mention this is a UK English distinction. In American English, 'practice' is used for both noun and verb. Since this is a GL Assessment (UK exam), the British rule applies, but children who encounter American-spelling resources may be confused. The explanation should reinforce that this is a British English convention.
suggestedFix: Add a brief note: 'In British English, practice (with a c) is the noun and practise (with an s) is the verb. This is a favourite GL exam question!'
subject: english

title: Q41 tests 'principal' but uses 'principle of the school' which is grammatically odd
description: Q41 has the sentence 'The principle of the school gave a speech at the end of term assembly.' The explanation says 'principle' should be 'principal'. While the homophone distinction is correct, British schools typically use 'headteacher' or 'head teacher', not 'principal'. 'Principal' is more common in American English, Scottish schools, and some academies. A child in the GL catchment (Dorset) would more likely encounter 'headteacher'. The sentence would work better with a context where 'principal' is more natural (e.g., 'the principal dancer', 'the principal reason').
suggestedFix: Consider revising to a more natural British context: 'The principle reason for the delay was the heavy snow.' This tests the same homophone but uses 'principal' as an adjective meaning 'main', which is more commonly tested in GL.
subject: english

title: Several questions mapped to 'spelling-demons' catch-all when a more specific sub-concept exists
description: Q1 tests 'untill' (common misspelling) and is mapped to 'spelling-demons'. Q4 tests 'becuase' (also mapped to spelling-demons). Q5 is a 'no mistake' question mapped to spelling-demons. While these words are genuine 'demons' (words that must be memorised), Q1 could arguably map to 'double-letters' (until has only one 'l', not double) and Q4 could map to 'silent-letters' or 'unstressed syllables'. The spelling-demons sub-concept acts as a catch-all for words that don't fit a specific rule, which is appropriate in principle but results in a large bucket (163 medium-confidence mappings) that reduces the precision of lesson targeting.
suggestedFix: This is a design trade-off — some words genuinely don't fit a single rule. The medium-confidence rating appropriately flags this. No change strictly needed, but the teaching efficacy for 'spelling-demons' could be improved by adding sub-categories within the lessons (e.g., 'words with tricky vowel sounds', 'words with silent letters in unusual positions').
subject: english

title: Explanation closing phrases are repetitive across questions
description: The explanations cycle through a small set of closing phrases: 'Look out for this one in the exam!', 'This one catches loads of people — now you know!', 'You'll have an advantage if you remember this one!', 'Sound it out carefully — the spelling matches the sounds.', 'Try writing it both ways and see which looks right.', 'Picture the word in your mind — visual memory helps.', 'Break it into chunks to spot the tricky bit.', 'This is a common exam word — worth practising!', 'If unsure, try covering the options and spelling it yourself first.' These appear to be templated and repeat roughly every 8-10 questions. While each is individually helpful, the repetition becomes noticeable when a child does many questions in sequence.
suggestedFix: Low priority. The tips are genuinely useful and variety exists. If desired, expand the pool of closing tips or make them more specific to the error type rather than generic.
subject: english

description: Duplicate question: Q1 and Q32 both ask 'Which word is closest in meaning to furious?' with identical correct answer 'Angry'/'Very angry'. Same word, same concept, different distractors but effectively identical. Child encounters the same test twice.
recommendation: Remove Q32 or replace with a different synonym question. There are 453 questions so removing one duplicate causes no coverage gap.
subject: english

description: Duplicate question: Q2 and Q31 both ask 'Which word is closest in meaning to enormous?' with answers 'Vast' and 'Very large'. Same word tested twice.
recommendation: Remove Q31 or replace with a different synonym (e.g., immense, colossal, gigantic as target words instead).
subject: english

description: Duplicate question: Q21 and Q80 both test the meaning of 'ambiguous' with nearly identical answer 'Open to more than one interpretation'/'Open to more than one meaning'. Q21 is D3 and Q80 is D3.
recommendation: Remove one or change one to test 'ambiguous' in a different format (e.g., in-context sentence).
subject: english

description: Duplicate question: Q81 and Q330 both test the meaning of 'clandestine' with identical answer 'Kept secret or hidden'. Both D3.
recommendation: Remove Q330 or replace with a different D3 word.
subject: english

description: Near-duplicate: Q25 (D3) and Q54 (D2) both test 'benevolent' — Q25 answer 'Well-meaning and kindly', Q54 answer 'Kind and generous'. Same word at two different difficulty levels with no meaningful differentiation.
recommendation: Keep one (D2 is more appropriate for this word). Replace the other with a different word.
subject: english

description: Near-duplicate: Q52 (D2) and Q182 (D2) both test 'absurd' as a synonym. Q52 answer 'Completely ridiculous', Q182 answer 'Ridiculous'.
recommendation: Remove one or change one to an antonym or in-context format.
subject: english

description: Near-duplicate: Q18 (D2) and Q59 (D2) both test the meaning of 'persistent'. Q18 format is 'What does persistent mean?' and Q59 is 'Which word is closest in meaning to persistent?' — same concept, same answer.
recommendation: Remove one or change format significantly (e.g., test in an antonym question instead).
subject: english

description: Near-duplicate: Q55 (D2) and Q254 (D2) both test 'diligent'. Q55 answer 'Hardworking and careful', Q254 answer 'Hardworking and showing careful effort'.
recommendation: Remove one or change to a different format.
subject: english

description: Near-duplicate: Q79 (D2) and Q272 (D3) both test the antonym of 'eloquent' with identical answer 'Inarticulate'.
recommendation: Remove one. Keep Q272 at D3 as the concept fits better there.
subject: english

description: Q368 asks 'Which word can mean BOTH a container for flowers AND the past tense of plant?' with answer 'Pot'. The premise is flawed — 'pot' is NOT the past tense of 'plant'. The past tense of 'plant' is 'planted'. The question's own explanation acknowledges this uncertainty: 'But actually, none of these perfectly fit'. A question that admits none of its options are correct is problematic.
recommendation: Rewrite to use a genuine double-meaning word. For example: 'Which word can mean BOTH a container for cooking AND to place a plant in soil?' (answer: pot). Or replace entirely with a cleaner homonym example.
subject: english

description: Q13 tests 'cautious' at D1. Per Oracle specs, D1 should be 'common, high-frequency words learned by Year 4 or earlier'. 'Cautious' is a Year 5-6 curriculum word that requires understanding nuance. It fits D2 better.
recommendation: Move to D2. Replace with a clearer D1 word like 'brave', 'strong', or 'happy'.
subject: english

description: Q45 tests the antonym of 'honest' at D1 with answer 'deceitful'. While 'honest' is D1, 'deceitful' as the answer requires D2 vocabulary knowledge. A 9-year-old at D1 level might not know 'deceitful' but would know 'dishonest'.
recommendation: Either move to D2 or change the answer option to 'dishonest' (which uses the familiar un-/dis- prefix pattern children learn early).
subject: english

description: Q141 tests 'contradict' at D1. 'Contradict' is a Year 5-6 curriculum word with a Latin root. It is more appropriate for D2.
recommendation: Move to D2.
subject: english

description: Q4 tests 'reluctant' at D2 and Q157 tests 'reluctant' at D1. The Oracle spec explicitly lists 'reluctant' as a D2 example word. Q157 at D1 is miscalibrated.
recommendation: Move Q157 to D2. This is also a near-duplicate of Q4 and Q260.
subject: english

description: Q3 tests the antonym of 'courageous' at D2. The word 'courageous' itself is D1 vocabulary (Oracle spec lists it under D1 examples). This should be D1.
recommendation: Move to D1.
subject: english

description: Q97 asks for the antonym of 'tenacious' with answer 'Giving up easily'. This answer is a phrase, not a single word. GL format typically expects single-word options. All other options (Strong, Determined, Stubborn, Brave) are synonyms or near-synonyms of tenacious, making process of elimination trivially easy — the child just picks the only option that isn't positive.
recommendation: Replace 'Giving up easily' with a single-word antonym like 'feeble' or 'yielding'. Add at least one plausible wrong antonym (e.g., 'gentle' or 'timid').
subject: english

description: Many explanations end with generic exam tips that rotate between a small set: 'A great word to use in your own writing!', 'Try using this word in a sentence today to make it stick.', 'Building a strong vocabulary is one of the best exam skills.', 'This type of question comes up regularly in GL papers.' These are fine individually but become repetitive across 453 questions. They add no question-specific value.
recommendation: Not urgent — these don't affect learning. But if explanations are ever refreshed, replace generic tips with word-specific mnemonics, etymology notes, or common usage examples.
subject: english

description: No figurative language vocabulary questions (simile, metaphor, personification, idiom interpretation). The Oracle spec notes ~5% of GL vocabulary testing covers figurative language in comprehension contexts. These are covered in Word Class/Grammar instead, but some crossover in the vocabulary topic would strengthen preparation.
recommendation: Low priority — covered elsewhere. If expanding, add 10-15 questions on idiom meanings (e.g., 'What does raining cats and dogs mean?') and figurative vs literal interpretation.
subject: english

description: Q106 asks what word class 'no', 'neither', 'none', and 'nowhere' belong to, with answer 'Negative determiners and pronouns'. The question's own explanation contradicts the answer: it correctly notes that 'nowhere' is a negative ADVERB, not a determiner or pronoun. You cannot group these four words into a single word class because they span three different classes (determiner, pronoun, adverb). The question is structurally flawed.
recommendation: Either remove 'nowhere' from the list (keeping no, neither, none which can all function as determiners/pronouns) or change the answer to acknowledge the mix. Better: split into two questions — one for negative determiners/pronouns and one for negative adverbs (never, nowhere, not).
subject: english

description: Q221-Q233 test sentence types (statement, question, command, exclamation, rhetorical question). These are grammar concepts but not word class identification. GL tests word class as 'What type of word is X?' not 'What type of sentence is this?' This content belongs in a separate 'Sentence Types' topic.
recommendation: Move to a dedicated Sentence Types sub-topic or separate topic. These are valid GL content but misplaced under Word Class.
subject: english

description: Q234-Q280 test punctuation knowledge (full stops, commas, question marks, exclamation marks, apostrophes, colons, semi-colons, speech marks, hyphens, brackets, dashes, ellipses). This is punctuation, not word class. GL has dedicated punctuation questions in comprehension — these belong in the Punctuation topic.
recommendation: Move all punctuation questions to the Punctuation topic. They are misplaced under Word Class & Grammar.
subject: english

description: Q281-Q330 cover a mix of literary devices (simile, metaphor, personification, alliteration, onomatopoeia, hyperbole), sentence structure (compound/complex sentences, clauses), more punctuation, and some word class. The literary devices content is valid English knowledge but belongs in its own topic. Mixing it with word class creates a sprawling, unfocused topic.
recommendation: Extract literary devices into a 'Figurative Language' topic. Extract sentence structure into a 'Sentence Structure' topic. Keep only pure word class identification in this topic.
subject: english

description: Q123 asks 'Which word is the adjective in: The bitter wind howled through the empty streets?' with answer 'bitter'. But the explanation itself notes 'empty' is also an adjective. The question asks for 'the adjective' (singular, with definite article implying there is only one), but there are two. This could confuse a child who correctly identifies 'empty' first.
recommendation: Rewrite as 'Which of these words is an adjective?' (acknowledging multiple possible answers) or use a sentence with only one adjective.
subject: english

description: Q124 has the same issue — asks for 'an adjective' in 'The mysterious parcel arrived on a rainy afternoon' with answer 'mysterious', but 'rainy' is also an adjective and is listed as an option. The question uses 'an adjective' (indefinite), which means either answer should be accepted, but only 'mysterious' is marked correct.
recommendation: Remove 'rainy' from the options (replace with a non-adjective word) or accept both as correct.
subject: english

description: Q1-Q17 use a 'What type of words are these: X, Y, Z?' format where four words are given and the child identifies the shared word class. The Oracle spec states GL word class questions are passage-based ('words tested in context, never isolation'). These isolated word-list questions are a useful teaching tool but do not match GL format. They should be clearly D1 warmup exercises.
recommendation: These are fine as D1 warmups but should be supplemented with more passage-based questions in GL format. Consider adding a note in lessons that the real exam always gives words in a sentence context.
subject: english

description: The Oracle spec identifies key GL traps: -ly adjectives (friendly, lonely, lovely, lively — words ending in -ly that are NOT adverbs). These traps are not tested in the question bank. No questions ask 'What type of word is friendly/lonely/lovely?' where the trap answer would be 'adjective' not 'adverb'.
recommendation: Add 5-10 questions testing -ly adjectives. These are confirmed GL traps that catch many children. Example: 'In the sentence The friendly dog wagged its tail, what type of word is friendly?' (Answer: Adjective, not Adverb).
subject: english

description: The Oracle spec highlights flat adverbs (same form as adjective: fast, hard, early, late, straight, wrong) as key GL traps. Q105 touches on this ('fast' and 'hard' as both adjective and adverb) but only in a classification format, not in a sentence-based GL format where the child must identify the word class in context.
recommendation: Add questions like: 'In She ran fast, what type of word is fast?' (Adverb) vs 'He is a fast runner — what type of word is fast?' (Adjective). These are high-value GL discriminators.
subject: english

description: Present participles as adjectives (the running water, an interesting book, the falling leaves) are identified by the Oracle as a D3 GL trap. No questions test this pattern. Children assume -ing words are always verbs.
recommendation: Add 3-5 D3 questions. Example: 'In The running water was ice cold, what type of word is running?' (Answer: Adjective — it describes the water, not an action someone is doing).
subject: english

description: Q12 tests 'kindness, honesty, patience, wisdom' as abstract nouns at D3. Abstract nouns are identified in the Oracle spec as D2 difficulty ('Abstract nouns children don't recognise as nouns'). Words with -ness and -dom suffixes have clear noun markers that make them D2, not D3.
recommendation: Move to D2. Reserve D3 for genuinely tricky abstract nouns without obvious suffixes (e.g., 'anger', 'fear', 'love' — words that look/feel like adjectives or verbs).
subject: english

description: Q13 tests 'anger, jealousy, excitement, sorrow' as abstract nouns at D3. Like Q12, these have recognisable noun suffixes (-er, -y, -ment, -ow). This is D2 material per the Oracle spec.
recommendation: Move to D2.
subject: english

description: Q9 tests 'January, Easter, Christmas, Diwali' as proper nouns at D2. These are straightforward proper nouns that most Year 4 children know need capital letters. This is D1 material.
recommendation: Move to D1. D2 proper noun questions should involve trickier cases (e.g., brand names, institutions, titles).
subject: english

description: In the word-list classification questions (Q1-Q17), the correct answer position varies well (0,1,3,4,1,3,1,0,4,0,3,4,1,3,4,3,0). No significant position bias detected. In the sentence-based questions, answer positions also show good distribution. GL spec requires no bias — this is met.
recommendation: No action needed. Noting for completeness.
subject: english

description: Explanations are consistently clear and age-appropriate. They explain the 'why' behind the answer without being patronising. Good use of relatable examples (playground, classroom, animals). British context maintained throughout (proper nouns use UK cities, UK curriculum terminology). No tone issues found.
recommendation: No action needed. Quality is high.
subject: english

title: No sequence questions despite ~15% GL weighting
description: Linear sequences (find next term, find rule, find nth term) account for approximately 10-15% of GL algebra questions. The topic has zero sequence questions. This is the single largest coverage gap in the algebra question bank.
suggestedFix: Add 15-20 sequence questions: 5 at D1 (obvious common difference, find next two terms), 8 at D2 (find rule from table, find 10th/20th term), 5 at D3 (express nth term algebraically, non-obvious rules). Include both ascending and descending sequences.
subject: maths

title: No pairs-of-unknowns / enumeration questions despite being a Year 6 statutory requirement
description: The Year 6 National Curriculum explicitly requires pupils to 'find pairs of numbers that satisfy an equation with 2 unknowns' and 'enumerate possibilities of combinations of 2 variables'. GL tests this at ~5% weighting. The only question approaching this is Q204 (unknowns on both sides), but no true 'find all pairs' or 'x + y = N' enumeration questions exist.
suggestedFix: Add 5-8 questions: 2 at D2 (find pairs satisfying a + b = 15 where both positive, simple constraints), 4 at D3 (two constraints like a + b = 20 and a = 3b; enumerate all whole number solutions). Include shape/picture equation format for 2-3 of these.
subject: maths

title: D3 significantly underweight at 17% vs GL target of ~30%
description: Only 34 of 204 questions are D3 (17%). GL targets approximately 30% D3. This means the top-performing children who need to be stretched are not getting enough challenge. The D2 count of 112 (55%) is also inflated above the ~40% target, mostly due to repetitive two-step equation patterns.
suggestedFix: Reclassify some current D2 questions that involve more complex reasoning (e.g. Q17, Q30, Q32, Q55 which require multi-step expression building) as D3 or near-D3. Add new D3 questions in the missing categories (sequences, two-unknowns, shape equations). Target: D1 ~58, D2 ~80-85, D3 ~55-60.
subject: maths

title: Excessive repetition in D2 two-step equation solving pattern
description: Approximately 30 D2 questions follow the near-identical pattern 'Solve: ax + b = c' (e.g. Q40, Q44, Q51, Q61, Q66, Q71, Q75, Q77, Q80, Q83, Q86, Q89, Q92, Q95, Q98, Q101, Q104, Q107, Q110, Q113, Q116, Q119, Q122, Q125, Q128, Q131, Q134). While practice is valuable, this many structurally identical questions offers diminishing returns. The only variation is in the coefficients and constants.
suggestedFix: Reduce to ~15 of this exact pattern and replace the freed slots with more diverse D2 question types: reverse function machines, substitution with two variables and brackets, forming equations from word problems, sequence questions. This maintains practice volume while broadening skill coverage.
subject: maths

title: master-two-step-equations sub-concept has no lesson content
description: Questions Q13 and Q33 are mapped to sub-concept 'master-two-step-equations' which shows '[No lesson found]' and empty learning goals in the mapping data. These questions are standard two-step equations (2x + 6 = 20 and 4y - 7 = 21) that are functionally identical to questions mapped to the working 'two-step-equations' sub-concept.
suggestedFix: Either create lessons for master-two-step-equations, or remap Q13 and Q33 to the existing 'two-step-equations' sub-concept which already has full lesson coverage. The latter is simpler and appropriate since the questions are not materially different.
subject: maths

title: Function machine questions show '[No lesson found]' despite lessons existing
description: The lessons file contains two well-structured function machine lessons (function-machines-visual and function-machines-backwards) but the mapping file shows '[No lesson found]' for function machine questions (Q8, Q15, Q27, Q47 etc.). This appears to be a wiring issue where the mapping extraction did not connect the lessons to the sub-concept.
suggestedFix: Verify the lesson-to-subconcept wiring in the source data. The lessons exist in the extracted data under sub-concept 'function-machines' so this may be a mapping extraction bug rather than missing content.
subject: maths

title: Q50 has two equivalent answer options
description: Q50 asks for the perimeter of a rectangle with length 8 and width w. Options include '16 + 2w' (correct, at index 2) and '2w + 8' (at index 4). These are mathematically distinct expressions (16 + 2w vs 2w + 8), BUT a child could legitimately argue 2w + 8 is also a valid simplification if they computed 2(w) + 2(4) = 2w + 8 instead of 2(8) + 2w = 16 + 2w. Wait — 2w + 8 is NOT the same as 16 + 2w. 2w + 8 would come from a rectangle with length 4 and width w (perimeter = 2(4) + 2w = 8 + 2w). So the distractor is valid — it catches children who use width 4 instead of 8. No issue.
suggestedFix: No fix needed — on re-analysis the distractor is valid. Removing this issue.
subject: maths

title: Q30 labelled D3 but is a straightforward D2 expression-building question
description: Q30: 'A tablet costs £t. A phone costs £50 more. Total cost of both?' Answer: 2t + 50. This requires combining two expressions (t and t+50) which is a single-step conceptual jump. Compare to Q32 (D3, three-day walking problem requiring three expressions combined) which is genuinely harder. Q30 is more typical of D2 difficulty.
suggestedFix: Reclassify Q30 as D2. Similarly review Q65 which is the same pattern (tablet + laptop = 2c + 200) also labelled D3.
subject: maths

title: Q137 labelled D1 but answer is not among options at stated index
description: Q137: 'A number machine adds 9. Put 14 in, what comes out?' Options: [5, 19, 23, 25, 32]. Correct index is 2 (= '23'). But 14 + 9 = 23, and '23' is at index 2. This is correct. However, the question is labelled D1 when it's genuinely a trivial single-addition question — the D1 label is appropriate. No issue on the answer. But the distractor '19' at index 1 is odd — a child who subtracted 9 from 14 would not get 19 either. The distractor set could be stronger.
suggestedFix: Consider replacing distractor '19' with '5' (14 - 9, the inverse-operation error) to better trap the common mistake. Currently '5' is already option A, so the distractors do include the subtraction error. On review, distractors are adequate. Withdrawing.
subject: maths

title: Q99 explanation is too terse for a D2 expression question
description: Q99: 'Sophie is s years old. Her brother is 5 times as old minus 8 years. Which shows her brother's age?' Explanation: 'Brother is 5s - 8 years old.' This does not walk through the reasoning — why '5 times as old' becomes 5s, and why 'minus 8' becomes '- 8'. Compare to Q34 which has the same structure but a slightly better explanation ('Harry has 3 times Freya's cards (3f), minus 5 cards: 3f - 5').
suggestedFix: Expand explanation to: 'Brother is 5 times Sophie's age (5s), minus 8 years: 5s - 8.'
subject: maths

title: Q87 explanation is too terse
description: Q87: 'Ruby scores r points. Jake scores twice as many plus 12. Which shows Jake's score?' Explanation: 'Jake scores 2r + 12 points.' Does not explain the step-by-step translation from words to algebra.
suggestedFix: Expand explanation to: 'Jake scores twice Ruby's points (2r), plus 12 bonus: 2r + 12.'
subject: maths

title: Q181 says 'Emma invites 6 friends' but formula uses n = number of children — is n 6 or 7?
description: Q181: 'A party planner uses C = 8n + 20 where n is the number of children. Emma invites 6 friends. What is the total cost?' The answer uses n = 6, giving £68. However, 'Emma invites 6 friends' could mean 7 children total (Emma plus 6 friends). If n = 7, C = 8(7) + 20 = 76 which is also an option. This ambiguity could cause a correct-reasoning child to pick the wrong answer.
suggestedFix: Reword to: 'There are 6 children at the party' or 'Emma invites 6 friends. The formula covers only the friends, not Emma.' Alternatively, change the numbers so the 'n vs n+1' ambiguity doesn't produce another available option.
subject: maths

title: Some D1 substitution questions are harder than labelled
description: Several D1 questions involve expressions like 9x - 20 (Q73), 6x - 19 (Q82), 8x - 35 (Q109), 5m - 42 (Q127) which require multi-digit multiplication followed by subtraction of larger numbers. While conceptually D1 (single substitution, one variable), the arithmetic complexity pushes these beyond easy territory for a 9-10 year old. A child who understands substitution perfectly could still err on 8 x 9 = 72, then 72 - 35 = 37.
suggestedFix: Consider reclassifying the harder-arithmetic substitution questions (Q73, Q82, Q109, Q127) as D2, or simplifying the arithmetic (smaller coefficients, rounder numbers) to keep them genuinely D1. GL D1 algebra typically uses single-digit answers and simple multiples.
subject: maths

title: BIDMAS/Brackets sub-concept lessons not visible in extracted data
description: Questions Q186-Q195 are mapped to the 'bidmas-brackets' sub-concept and the mapping shows lesson learning goals about BIDMAS. However, the lessons extraction file shows only 8 sub-concepts: using-letters, substitution, two-step-equations, writing-expressions, function-machines, simple-formulae, inverse-operations, and one more. The BIDMAS/brackets lesson content was not visible in the extracted data read. This may mean the lessons exist but weren't captured, or that they are missing.
suggestedFix: Verify BIDMAS/brackets lessons exist in the source lessonData.js. If missing, create 2 lessons covering: (1) BIDMAS order of operations with and without brackets, (2) common BIDMAS mistakes (left-to-right vs correct order). These 10 questions (Q186-Q195) need lesson support.
subject: maths

title: 12 algebraic straight-line questions all produce x=10 via coefficients summing to 18
description: Twelve D2 algebraic angle questions on straight lines all use coefficient pairs that sum to 18 (e.g. 7u+8u+3u, 6y+7y+5y, 8v+10v, 11c+7c, 13g+5g, 9j+6j+3j, 14n+4n, 15r+3r, 10t+5t+3t, 16u+2u, 11w+4w+3w, 17z+z). Every single one gives x=10. A child quickly spots this pattern and can answer all 12 without doing any maths. This completely undermines the learning value. Additionally, Q51 and Q12 both use coefficients summing to 5 (giving x=36) as a pair, making 14 questions with only 2 distinct answers between them.
suggestedFix: Reduce to 4-5 algebraic straight-line questions with genuinely different coefficient sums (e.g. sums of 5, 6, 9, 10, 12, 15 giving answers of 36, 30, 20, 18, 15, 12). Replace the removed questions with more varied algebraic angle problems: angles around a point with algebra, quadrilateral angles with algebra, or multi-step problems combining rules.
subject: maths

title: 10 parallel line questions all have identical structure — answer always equals the given angle
description: All 10 corresponding/alternate angle questions (Q14, Q19, Q35, Q42, Q57, Q68, Q80, Q93, Q106, Q119) follow the exact same pattern: 'One angle is X. What is the corresponding/alternate angle?' The answer is always X. There is zero cognitive variation. A child learns after the first question that the answer is always the same number repeated. These are all rated D3 but offer no genuine D3 challenge since the pattern is trivially learnable.
suggestedFix: Keep 3-4 of these as introductory D1/D2 questions. Replace the rest with genuine D3 parallel line questions: (1) 'Find the co-interior angle' (answer = 180 - given), (2) 'Given one angle, find a non-adjacent angle using two rules', (3) multi-step problems where you must use alternate + straight line rules together. This would also boost the genuinely thin co-interior angle coverage.
subject: maths

title: Corresponding/alternate angle questions mislabelled as D3 when they are D1 in practice
description: Questions like Q14, Q19, Q35, Q42, Q57, Q68, Q80, Q93, Q106, Q119 are all labelled D3. However, the skill tested is simply 'corresponding/alternate angles are equal' — a single-fact recall that requires no calculation, no multi-step reasoning, and no inference. By GL difficulty criteria, single-fact recall is D1. These artificially inflate the D3 count (10 of 57 D3 questions = 18% of all D3). If reclassified correctly, true D3 drops to 47 (21%), making the D3 shortage even more acute.
suggestedFix: Reclassify the simple 'angles are equal' parallel line questions as D1. Create genuine D3 parallel line questions requiring multi-step reasoning (e.g. combining co-interior + vertically opposite, or finding an angle using two parallel line rules in sequence).
subject: maths

title: Regular polygon interior angles and basic parallel line facts labelled D1 but require formula knowledge
description: Q199 (regular hexagon interior angle = 120) and Q200 (regular pentagon = 108) are labelled D1. However, calculating these requires knowing and applying the (n-2) x 180 / n formula, which is Year 6+ content. For a 9-year-old, this is genuinely D2 at minimum. Conversely, Q217-Q219 are basic parallel line questions labelled D1, which is correct — but inconsistent with the D3 labelling of Q14, Q19 etc. which test the identical skill.
suggestedFix: Reclassify Q199 and Q200 as D2. Ensure all 'corresponding/alternate angles are equal' questions share the same difficulty level (D1 for basic recall, D2 if combined with another step).
subject: maths

title: Vertically opposite angles barely covered — only 2 questions, no standalone sub-concept
description: Vertically opposite angles are a KS2 statutory requirement and appear on GL papers. Only Q133 and Q134 touch on this concept, and Q141/Q144 use it as a secondary step. There is no sub-concept, no lesson, and no dedicated question set for this fundamental angle fact. The mapping data has no 'vertically-opposite' sub-concept.
suggestedFix: Create a 'vertically-opposite-angles' sub-concept with 2 lessons and 6-8 dedicated questions: 3 at D1 ('Two lines cross, one angle is 48, what is the opposite angle?'), 3 at D2 (find an angle using vertically opposite + straight line combined), 2 at D3 (multi-step with parallel lines).
subject: maths

title: Co-interior angles (allied angles) severely under-represented — only 3 questions
description: Co-interior angles (same-side angles between parallel lines summing to 180) appear in only Q188, Q220, and Q221. This is a distinct GL-tested concept that children often confuse with alternate/corresponding angles. The current 3 questions are insufficient for practice.
suggestedFix: Add 4-5 co-interior angle questions: 2 at D2 (basic 180 - given), 3 at D3 (combined with other angle rules, or finding both angles in a trapezium using co-interior property).
subject: maths

title: Q7 triangle classification question has two valid answers
description: Q7 asks 'Two angles in a triangle are 45 and 90. What type of triangle is this?' with options Equilateral, Isosceles, Right-angled, Obtuse, Scalene. The correct answer is given as 'Right-angled', but the triangle is also isosceles (two 45 angles). The explanation acknowledges this: 'making it a right-angled isosceles triangle, but right-angled is the main classification'. While the intended answer is defensible, a child who correctly identifies it as isosceles would be marked wrong. GL avoids this ambiguity.
suggestedFix: Either (1) change the angles so the triangle is right-angled but NOT isosceles (e.g. 90 and 30, giving 60), or (2) remove 'Isosceles' from the options so there is no ambiguity.
subject: maths

title: Q45 requires rounding to 1 decimal place — non-standard for GL 11+ maths
description: Q45 asks for the interior angle of a regular heptagon, yielding 128.571... which is rounded to 128.6. GL 11+ maths questions produce whole-number answers. Requiring decimal rounding introduces a skill (rounding) that is extraneous to the geometry being tested and creates a source of error unrelated to angle knowledge.
suggestedFix: Replace with a polygon that produces a whole-number answer (pentagon=108, hexagon=120, octagon=135, nonagon=140, decagon=144, dodecagon=150). Or reframe to ask for the total interior angle sum (which is always whole): 'What is the sum of interior angles in a heptagon?' Answer: 900.
subject: maths

title: Q83 tests a 15-sided polygon (pentadecagon) — well beyond GL 11+ scope
description: Q83 asks for the interior angle of a regular 15-sided polygon (pentadecagon). Similarly Q104 asks about a 20-sided polygon (icosagon). GL 11+ maths stays within shapes children encounter in the KS2 curriculum: triangle, quadrilateral, pentagon, hexagon, and occasionally octagon. A 15-sided or 20-sided polygon is not part of the Year 5-6 curriculum and the name 'pentadecagon' would be unfamiliar to virtually all 10-year-olds.
suggestedFix: Replace Q83 and Q104 with questions about more common polygons or with questions that test the formula in a different way (e.g. 'A regular polygon has interior angles of 144 each. How many sides does it have?').
subject: maths

title: Q189 is labelled D3 but is identical in structure to D1 quadrilateral questions
description: Q189 asks 'Three angles of a quadrilateral are 80, 100, and 95. What is the fourth angle?' This is the exact same structure as Q10 (D1), Q26 (D1), Q38 (D1), Q67 (D1), Q86 (D1) — subtract three angles from 360. There is no additional complexity, multi-step reasoning, or inference to justify D3.
suggestedFix: Reclassify Q189 as D1. Ensure consistent difficulty labelling: basic 'subtract from total' questions should all be D1 regardless of which question number they are.
subject: maths

title: 10 basic straight-line questions (D1) are near-identical — simple 180 minus one angle
description: Questions Q1, Q23, Q123, Q124, Q125, Q126, Q127, Q128, Q129, Q130, Q223, Q224 all ask the same thing: two angles on a straight line, one is given, find the other.
