That is 12 questions testing a single-step subtraction from 180. While some repetition aids fluency, 12 is excessive for a single pattern. Combined with Q131/Q132 (three angles on a straight line, still D1-level), the straight-line sub-concept has ~25 questions at the most basic level.
suggestedFix: Reduce basic two-angle straight-line D1 questions to 5-6. Use the freed slots for more varied angle problems (vertically opposite, multi-rule combinations, real-world contexts).
subject: maths

title: Q210 and Q117 are identical questions — isosceles with two 70-degree angles
description: Q117 (D2): 'An isosceles triangle has two equal angles of 70 each. What is the third angle?' Answer: 40. Q210 (D1): 'An isosceles triangle has two equal angles of 70. What is the third angle?' Answer: 40. These are the exact same question with different difficulty labels.
suggestedFix: Remove one of the duplicates. If keeping Q210 at D1, remove Q117 (or vice versa). Ensure the remaining question has the correct difficulty label — this is a single-step subtraction, so D1 is appropriate.
subject: maths

title: master-missing-angle-triangle sub-concept has no learning goals and no lesson content
description: Questions Q3, Q5, Q24, Q25, Q85, Q96, Q113, Q159-Q163 map to sub-concept 'master-missing-angle-triangle' which shows empty lessonLearningGoal arrays and '[No lesson found]' for teach summaries. This sub-concept appears to be a catch-all for basic triangle angle questions that don't fit isosceles, right-angled, or algebraic categories. These children get no targeted lesson before attempting these questions.
suggestedFix: Either (1) create a proper 'Missing Angle in a Triangle' sub-concept with 2 lessons covering the 180 rule for general triangles, or (2) merge these questions into an existing sub-concept like straight-line (since the method is identical: subtract known from total).
subject: maths

title: All lesson teach summaries show '[No lesson found]' in mappings — possible extraction bug
description: Every single mapping entry shows lessonTeachSummary as '[No lesson found]', even for sub-concepts that clearly have lessons (e.g. straight-line has 2 lessons with full teach screens). This is likely an extraction/wiring issue in the mapping generation script rather than actually missing lessons. However, it means the mapping data cannot confirm whether lessons properly address their mapped questions.
suggestedFix: Investigate the mapping extraction script to determine why lessonTeachSummary is not being populated. The lesson data file confirms teach screens exist with interaction content, so this appears to be a data pipeline issue.
subject: maths

title: Q137 is identical to Q17 — both ask 'one angle is twice the other on a straight line'
description: Q17 (D2): 'Sarah draws two angles on a straight line. One angle is twice the size of the other. What is the smaller angle?' Answer: 60. Q137 (D2): 'Jack draws two angles on a straight line. One angle is twice the size of the other. What is the smaller angle?' Answer: 60. Only the character name differs.
suggestedFix: Remove Q137 or change it to a different ratio (e.g. one angle is 4 times the other: x + 4x = 180, x = 36).
subject: maths

title: Questions 123-225 lack character names and real-world context compared to Q1-Q122
description: The first 122 questions consistently use character names (Tom, Sophie, Emma, Jake, etc.) and sometimes real-world scenarios. Questions 123-225 are more bare and abstract: 'Two angles on a straight line are X and Y. What is Y?' While not incorrect, the shift in style is noticeable. GL papers typically embed maths in light contexts. The warmth and engagement drops noticeably in the second half.
suggestedFix: Add brief character names and contexts to the more abstract questions in the 123-225 range. E.g. 'Sophie measures two angles on a straight line. One is 130. What is the other?' This maintains engagement consistency.
subject: maths

title: Algebraic angle question distractors follow a predictable +/-5 pattern
description: Nearly all D2 algebraic angle questions use distractors spaced exactly 5 apart (e.g. 8, 10, 12, 15, 18 or similar patterns). While evenly-spaced distractors are standard, the consistency of the 5-apart pattern means a child familiar with the format can sometimes eliminate options by recognising the spacing pattern rather than doing the maths.
suggestedFix: Vary distractor spacing: include some with uneven spacing, include common wrong answers (e.g. for x + 2x = 180, include 90 as a distractor since children might divide 180 by 2 instead of 3). Make distractors diagnostic of specific errors.
subject: maths

title: Q190 states kites have 'two pairs of equal adjacent angles' — this is not always how kites are defined at KS2
description: Q190 states 'A kite has two pairs of equal adjacent angles.' At KS2 level, a kite is typically defined as having 2 pairs of equal adjacent sides and exactly 1 pair of equal opposite angles (the angles between the unequal sides). The question's phrasing could confuse children who have learned a different definition. The maths is correct (110 + 110 + x + x = 360, x = 70), but the framing may not match what children have been taught.
suggestedFix: Rephrase to avoid the definitional claim: 'A kite has angles of 110, 110, x, and x. What is x?' or 'A kite has two angles of 110 each. The other two angles are equal. What is each one?'
subject: maths

title: 42 questions mapped to sub-concept with no lesson
description: The sub-concept 'master-area-rectangles' has 42 questions mapped to it (Q2, Q4, Q7, Q9, Q15, Q17, Q26, Q32, Q33, Q39, Q44, Q50, Q54, Q55, Q57, Q63, Q65, Q66, Q69, Q76, Q81, Q83, Q85, Q87, Q91, Q99, Q101, Q105, Q107, Q110, Q117, Q122, Q129, and more). Every single one shows '[No lesson found]' in the mapping. This means a student entering Focused Learning for basic area of rectangles/squares will never see a teaching lesson before being tested. This is the largest teaching gap in the topic.
suggestedFix: Create 2 lessons for 'master-area-rectangles': a step-by-step lesson teaching Area = length x width with worked examples, and a spot-the-mistake lesson addressing the common error of confusing area with perimeter (adding instead of multiplying). Use RectangleDiagram visual component.
subject: maths

title: D3 questions significantly underweight (15.8% vs 30% target)
description: Current distribution is D1:85 (40.7%), D2:91 (43.5%), D3:33 (15.8%). The GL target is approximately 30:40:30. D3 is almost half what it should be. This means students are not being sufficiently challenged at the higher end and may be underprepared for the harder questions on the actual GL paper. Areas where D3 could be expanded: algebraic dimensions with more complex expressions, multi-step path/border problems with non-integer widths, problems requiring unit conversion mid-calculation, questions where area and perimeter are combined in the same problem, and problems involving scaling/enlargement of shapes.
suggestedFix: Add approximately 30 new D3 questions across these categories: algebraic area/perimeter (10), complex path/border calculations (5), multi-step combined area+perimeter problems (5), scaling and proportion with area (5), real-world cost/coverage problems with rounding (5). This would bring D3 to ~30%.
subject: maths

title: Q24 and Q68 are identical questions
description: Q24: 'A square has a perimeter of P cm. What is its area in terms of P?' with answer P²/16. Q68: 'A square has a perimeter of P metres. What is its area in terms of P?' with answer P²/16. These are the same question with the same options in the same order, differing only in the unit (cm vs metres) which is irrelevant since the answer is in terms of P. Both are rated D3. A student seeing both would recognise it immediately.
suggestedFix: Replace Q68 with a different algebraic question, e.g. 'A rectangle has a perimeter of P cm and a width of 5 cm. What is its area in terms of P?' (Answer: 5(P/2 - 5) = 5P/2 - 25). Or change to: 'A square has an area of A cm². What is its perimeter in terms of A?' (Answer: 4 x sqrt(A)).
subject: maths

title: Option E uses wrong units (square metres for a perimeter question)
description: Q126 asks for the perimeter of a rectangle 6x as long as wide (width 5m). Options are: 35m, 55m, 70m, 105m, '150 square metres'. Option E gives the area (30 x 5 = 150) with 'square metres' unit. While this is a plausible distractor trapping the area/perimeter confusion, using 'square metres' in a perimeter question is inconsistent with the other options which all use 'metres'. A child who knows perimeter is measured in linear units can eliminate it immediately without calculating, which defeats the purpose of the distractor.
suggestedFix: Change option E to '150 metres' (keeps the area-confusion trap but with consistent units, so the child must actually think about which calculation is correct rather than eliminating on units alone). Alternatively, change to '140 metres' as a plausible arithmetic error.
subject: maths

title: Option E uses wrong units (square metres for a perimeter question)
description: Q116 asks for perimeter of a rectangle with algebraic dimensions (6w by 8w, w=2). Options include '192 square metres' as option E. This is the same issue as Q126 — mixing square unit into a perimeter question allows elimination by unit inspection alone.
suggestedFix: Change '192 square metres' to '192 metres' to maintain consistent units across all options.
subject: maths

title: Comparing area and perimeter with different units is conceptually questionable
description: Q5 asks 'What is the difference between its perimeter and its area?' for a 9x4 rectangle. The answer is 36 - 26 = 10. The explanation notes 'we're comparing numbers, not units.' While mathematically valid, this is conceptually problematic — you are subtracting cm from cm², which has no physical meaning. Q43, Q97, Q184, Q187, Q188 all have the same pattern. GL Assessment does occasionally use these 'compare the numbers' questions, but the explanation should be clearer about why this works (pure number comparison) so children don't think subtracting area from perimeter is meaningful in real life.
suggestedFix: No change to the questions themselves (GL does test this pattern), but improve explanations to explicitly state: 'We compare the numerical values only — area and perimeter use different units so this is a number comparison, not a measurement comparison.' The current explanation on Q5 does note this but later questions (Q43, Q184, Q187, Q188) do not.
subject: maths

title: Q9 rated D2 but is effectively D1 complexity
description: Q9: 'Oliver is tiling his bathroom floor which is 3m long and 2m wide. Each tile covers 1 square metre. How many tiles does he need?' This is simply 3 x 2 = 6. The tile covering 1m² adds no complexity — it's a direct area calculation. Rated D2 but the cognitive demand is identical to D1 rectangle area questions.
suggestedFix: Either downgrade to D1, or make it genuinely D2 by using a non-unit tile size (e.g. 'Each tile covers 0.5 square metres. How many tiles does he need?' Answer: 12).
subject: maths

title: Q50 rated D2 but is effectively D1 complexity
description: Q50: 'Harper is tiling a rectangular floor that is 7m long and 5m wide. Each tile covers 1 square metre. How many tiles are needed?' Answer: 35. Same issue as Q9 — tiling with 1m² tiles is simply calculating area, which is D1.
suggestedFix: Downgrade to D1 or increase complexity by using non-unit tiles.
subject: maths

title: Q186 rated D1 but involves perimeter-to-area conversion (D2 skill)
description: Q186: 'A square has a perimeter of 20 cm. What is its area?' requires two steps: find side (20/4=5), then square it (25). This is the same multi-step process as Q42, Q48, Q58 etc., all rated D2. Q186 should be D2 for consistency.
suggestedFix: Upgrade Q186 from D1 to D2 to match the rating of identical question patterns in the bank.
subject: maths

title: Q198-Q209 all rated D1 but some involve multi-step inverse operations
description: Questions Q198-Q209 are all rated D1. Q198 (area 24, length 6, find width = 4) and similar missing-side-from-area questions are simple division. Q205-Q209 (perimeter given, find missing side) require halving the perimeter then subtracting — a two-step process typically rated D2 elsewhere in the bank (Q8, Q34, Q38 etc.). However, the numbers used are deliberately small and simple (perimeter 18/20/22/24/26), making them accessible at D1. This is borderline — the small numbers justify D1 but the pattern is D2 elsewhere.
suggestedFix: Consider upgrading Q205-Q209 to D2 for consistency with the rest of the bank where perimeter-to-missing-side is always D2. Or keep as D1 but note these are 'easy D2' bridging questions. No urgent action needed.
subject: maths

title: area-vs-perimeter lesson doesn't cover numeric comparison questions
description: The 'area-vs-perimeter' sub-concept lesson teaches that same area doesn't mean same perimeter, and vice versa. But several questions mapped to this sub-concept actually ask for the numeric difference or sum of area and perimeter (Q5, Q43, Q97, Q184, Q187, Q188). The lesson doesn't teach this 'compare the numbers' skill — it teaches the conceptual difference. The mapping is reasonable (they are area-vs-perimeter questions) but the lesson doesn't prepare students for the specific calculation pattern.
suggestedFix: Add a worked example to the consolidate screen showing a 'find the difference between area and perimeter' calculation, clarifying that you compare the numbers only. Or create a separate sub-concept for 'numeric comparison of area and perimeter'.
subject: maths

title: L-shape perimeter questions mapped to area lesson
description: Q147 and Q148 ask for the perimeter of an L-shape, but are mapped to 'compound-shapes' sub-concept whose lessons only teach area (splitting into rectangles and adding areas). The perimeter of an L-shape requires tracing around the outside and counting all 6 edges — a fundamentally different skill from area calculation. Students who get these questions after the compound shapes lesson may be confused because the lesson never mentions perimeter at all.
suggestedFix: Either: (a) create a separate sub-concept 'compound-shape-perimeter' with a lesson teaching how to trace around an L-shape counting all 6 sides, or (b) add perimeter content to the existing compound-shapes lesson consolidation screen. Option (a) is preferred for cleaner mapping.
subject: maths

title: D1 severely overweight at 47% vs GL target of ~30%
description: 103 of 220 questions are D1 (47%), well above the ~30% GL target. The excess comes from highly repetitive calculation questions in the Q26-Q125 range. For example, there are 20+ 'What is the mean of X, Y, Z, W, V?' questions, 15+ 'What is the mode?' questions, and 15+ 'What is the range?' questions, all following identical formats with different numbers. While some repetition builds fluency, this volume crowds out more challenging content.
suggestedFix: Remove or reclassify approximately 35-40 of the most repetitive D1 questions. Keep a representative sample of ~8 mean, ~6 mode, ~6 range, ~6 median D1 questions. Replace freed slots with D2 questions requiring multi-step reasoning or interpretation.
subject: maths

title: D2 underweight at 29% vs GL target of ~40%
description: Only 64 of 220 questions are D2 (29%), below the ~40% GL target. The D2 questions that exist are good quality — even-count medians, combined measures, bar chart interpretation, table cross-referencing. But there aren't enough of them. The gap is most noticeable in graph/chart interpretation where D2 should test multi-step reading (e.g. 'which month showed the biggest increase?', 'what fraction of total does X represent?').
suggestedFix: Add 20-25 D2 questions: (1) Multi-step chart interpretation requiring comparison/calculation, (2) Data from frequency tables requiring weighted mean calculation, (3) Two-way table completion (find missing values), (4) Questions requiring children to choose between mean/median/mode as the best average for a given context.
subject: maths

title: master-mean-average sub-concept has no lesson content
description: Questions Q2, Q7, Q22, Q26, Q30, Q37, Q42, Q49, Q54, Q60, Q66, Q72, Q77, Q84, Q89, Q96, Q101, Q108, Q113, Q120, Q125 (21 questions) are mapped to 'master-mean-average' which shows '[No lesson found]' and has empty learning goals []. The mean is the most fundamental data handling calculation — add all values, divide by count — and currently has no micro-lesson teaching support.
suggestedFix: Create dedicated lessons for this sub-concept with: (1) step-by-step lesson teaching sum ÷ count, (2) spot-the-mistake lesson covering common errors (dividing by wrong count, forgetting a value in the sum). Alternatively, remap these questions to an existing mean-related sub-concept.
subject: maths

title: Excessive repetition of D1 mode questions with identical structure
description: Questions Q27, Q33, Q38, Q43, Q50, Q61, Q68, Q74, Q81, Q93, Q98, Q105, Q116, Q122 all follow the identical pattern: 'What is the mode? X, Y, X, Z, X, W' where X appears 3 times and all others appear once. The only variation is the numbers used. This is 14 structurally identical D1 questions testing the exact same skill.
suggestedFix: Keep 4-5 representative mode questions at D1. Replace the rest with mode questions that add variety: data with two modes (bimodal), data with no mode (all unique), mode from a frequency table, mode from a bar chart. Some of these already exist at D2/D3 (Q203, Q204) but D1 needs variety too.
subject: maths

title: Excessive repetition of D1 range questions with identical structure
description: Questions Q29, Q32, Q39, Q45, Q51, Q58, Q64, Q70, Q76, Q82, Q88, Q94, Q100, Q106, Q112, Q118, Q124 all follow the identical pattern: 'What is the range? A, B, C, D, E' — a set of 5 numbers requiring highest minus lowest. This is 17 structurally identical D1 questions.
suggestedFix: Keep 4-5 representative range questions at D1. Replace the rest with range questions testing slightly different skills: range from ordered data, range from a chart/graph, interpreting what a large/small range means in context.
subject: maths

title: Excessive repetition of D1 mean questions with identical structure
description: Questions Q26, Q37, Q42, Q49, Q54, Q60, Q66, Q72, Q77, Q84, Q89, Q96, Q101, Q108, Q113, Q120, Q125 all follow the identical pattern: 'What is the mean? X, Y, Z, W, V' — five numbers requiring sum ÷ 5. This is 17 structurally identical D1 questions. The numbers are always in an arithmetic sequence (e.g. 22,26,30,34,38 or 44,52,60,68,76), making them even more formulaic.
suggestedFix: Keep 4-5 representative mean questions at D1 with varied number sets (not all arithmetic sequences). Replace the rest with mean questions in context: mean from a table, mean from grouped data, comparing means of two groups.
subject: maths

title: combined-averages lesson covers mode+range but also maps mode+median questions
description: The 'combined-averages' sub-concept is named 'Questions Using Mode AND Range Together' and its learning goals reference mode and range. However, Q24 ('What is the mode and median?') and Q86 ('What is the mode and median?') are also mapped to this sub-concept. The lesson doesn't explicitly teach how to handle mode+median combination questions — only mode+range.
suggestedFix: Either: (1) Expand the combined-averages lesson to cover all pairwise combinations (mode+range, mode+median, mean+range, etc.), or (2) Create a separate sub-concept for mode+median questions with its own lesson.
subject: maths

title: Minor typo in median lesson: '3th' should be '3rd'
description: In the finding-median-steps lesson interact screen, the incorrect feedback reads: 'The middle value (3th out of 5) is 6.' The ordinal '3th' should be '3rd'.
suggestedFix: Change '3th' to '3rd' in the incorrectFeedback string.
subject: maths

title: No pictogram questions
description: GL Assessment occasionally includes pictogram reading questions at primary level (where each symbol represents N items and children must read/calculate values). The question bank has bar charts, line graphs, pie charts, and tables but no pictograms. This is a minor gap as pictograms are less frequently tested than other chart types.
suggestedFix: Add 3-5 pictogram questions: 2 at D1 (read value where key is given), 2 at D2 (calculate total, find difference using half-symbol values). Low priority compared to other issues.
subject: maths

title: V2 bar chart questions (Q146-Q161) reference visual components but have no SVG images
description: Questions Q146-Q161 all have hasVisualComponent: true and visualComponent: 'BarChart' but image: null. This means the questions rely entirely on the rendered BarChart component for their data. This is actually fine for the app (the component renders the chart), but it differs from earlier questions (Q5, Q15, Q23 etc.) which reference SVG files. The data values are embedded in the explanation text rather than in visual props, so if the component fails to render, the child cannot answer the question.
suggestedFix: No action needed if the BarChart component reliably renders. However, consider adding fallback SVG images for robustness, or ensure the visual component data (bar values) is included in the question's visual props for the component to render from.
subject: maths

title: V2 pie chart questions (Q176-Q186) reference visual components but have no SVG images
description: Questions Q176-Q186 all have hasVisualComponent: true and visualComponent: 'PieChart' but image: null. Same situation as the bar chart questions — relies on component rendering. The pie chart questions reference fractions and degrees in their explanations, so the visual is essential for answering.
suggestedFix: Same as MATHS-DH-VISUAL-001 — ensure reliable component rendering or add fallback SVG files.
subject: maths

title: Q111 pie chart answer uses approximate calculation instead of exact
description: Q111: 'If 180 students surveyed, how many chose Maths?' Answer: 55. Explanation: 'Maths = 110° out of 360° ≈ 0.306. So 180 × 0.306 ≈ 55.' The calculation 180 × 110/360 = 55 exactly (180 × 11/36 = 1980/36 = 55). Using '≈' (approximately) in the explanation is misleading when the answer is exact. This could confuse children into thinking approximation is required.
suggestedFix: Change the explanation to: 'Maths = 110° out of 360° = 11/36. So 180 × 11/36 = 55 students.' Remove the approximate symbol.
subject: maths

title: D3 questions significantly underweight (22.5% vs target 30%)
description: The difficulty distribution is D1:81(37.2%) D2:88(40.4%) D3:49(22.5%). D1 is 7% overweight and D3 is 7.5% underweight compared to the target 30:40:30 split. This means children who need stretching at the harder end get fewer practice opportunities. The D3 questions that exist are well-designed multi-step problems, but there are not enough of them.
suggestedFix: Convert 10-15 easier D1 questions to D3 difficulty by adding multi-step reasoning, mixed forms (fractions/decimals/percentages), context interpretation, or missing-number algebra. Good candidates for upgrade: simple addition/subtraction questions where carrying is straightforward (e.g. Q2, Q3, Q36, Q44, Q61, Q76, Q95, Q108, Q125) could be made harder by adding a second step or requiring conversion first.
subject: maths

title: No standalone number line / positioning questions
description: GL research identifies 'Number Line / Positioning' as a distinct question type (Type 8) that appears on papers. The current question bank has no questions asking children to identify a decimal value from a number line diagram. Comparison questions partially cover this skill but don't test visual number line reading.
suggestedFix: Add 5-8 number line questions across difficulties: D1 (number line marked in tenths, identify value), D2 (number line with only endpoints, identify unmarked value), D3 (estimate a position between marks). These would need SVG diagrams.
subject: maths

title: No lessons for master-column-addition-decimals sub-concept
description: The sub-concept 'master-column-addition-decimals' has approximately 20 questions mapped to it (Q2, Q12, Q16, Q25, Q28, Q36, Q44, Q52, Q61, Q71, Q76, Q89, Q95, Q98, Q108, Q125, Q132, etc.) but the mapping data shows '[No lesson found]' with empty learning goals. Unlike other '[No lesson found]' entries where lessons exist but weren't extracted, this sub-concept genuinely appears to lack lesson content. Adding decimals with carrying across the decimal point is a core GL skill.
suggestedFix: Create 2 lessons for this sub-concept: (1) a step-by-step lesson teaching column addition of decimals with emphasis on lining up decimal points and carrying, (2) a spot-the-mistake lesson addressing the misaligned column error (adding 3.4 + 0.56 by right-aligning as 3.4 + 5.6).
subject: maths

title: Options include both '6' and '6.0' which are mathematically equivalent
description: Q5 asks 'A bag of sweets costs £0.60. What do 10 bags cost?' Options are: 0.06, 6, 60, 0.6, 6.0. The correct answer is '6' (index 1). However option '6.0' (index 4) is mathematically identical to '6'. A child who correctly understands the problem could reasonably select either. This creates ambiguity.
suggestedFix: Replace '6.0' with a different distractor that exploits a specific misconception. For example: '0.006' (dividing by 10 instead of multiplying) or '600' (multiplying by 1000 instead of 10).
subject: maths

title: D1 rating too easy for dividing by 100 with a decimal resulting in thousandths
description: Q29: 'A school raises £7.50 and shares it equally among 100 pupils. How many pounds does each pupil receive?' is rated D1 but the answer is 0.075, which requires understanding thousandths and handling a decimal divided by 100. Per the GL research, D1 questions should be 'single-skill with no cross-topic integration' using 'tenths only, or familiar hundredths'. Getting 0.075 from 7.5÷100 involves understanding 3 decimal places.
suggestedFix: Reclassify as D2. The question involves dividing a decimal by 100 and interpreting a thousandths answer, which fits the D2 criteria of 'crossing a place value boundary'.
subject: maths

title: Distractor spacing too tight — rewards guessing over understanding
description: Q49: 'A rectangular label is 0.7 metres wide and 0.6 metres tall. What is its area?' Options are 0.40, 0.42, 0.44, 0.46, 4.2. The first four options are spaced 0.02 apart. A child who understands the method (0.7 × 0.6 = 0.42) will get it right, but the tight spacing means a child who makes a small arithmetic error could land on any of the nearby options. The tight spacing doesn't exploit distinct misconceptions — it just makes the question a precision test.
suggestedFix: Widen the distractor spread to test misconceptions: e.g. 0.42 (correct), 0.13 (added instead of multiplied... 0.7-0.6=0.1, or similar), 4.2 (decimal point error), 0.042 (extra decimal place), 1.3 (added 0.7+0.6). Each distractor should catch a different error pattern.
subject: maths

title: D1 rating too easy for dividing by 100 to get 0.153
description: Q53: 'A school collects £15.30 and divides it equally among 100 charity boxes' is rated D1 but produces a 3-decimal-place answer (0.153). Similar to Q29, this requires understanding that dividing by 100 shifts digits 2 places right and handling a number with initial tens digit. Per GL research, D1 should use 'tenths only, or familiar hundredths'.
suggestedFix: Reclassify as D2.
subject: maths

title: D1 rating questionable for ÷100 producing 0.091
description: Q75: '9.1 litres poured into 100 tiny pots. How many litres go into each pot?' is D1 but the answer 0.091 requires understanding that the zero in the tenths place is a placeholder. This is more aligned with D2 difficulty based on GL research criteria.
suggestedFix: Consider reclassifying as D2.
subject: maths

title: Question asks for 'largest time' but context implies fastest runner wants smallest time
description: Q40: 'Five runners record their 50-metre sprint times... Which is the largest time?' This is technically correct (asking for the largest number), but in a sprint context, children associate 'best' with fastest (smallest time). Asking for 'largest time' is slightly counterintuitive and could confuse children who think about performance rather than the numerical value.
suggestedFix: Rephrase to 'Which runner had the slowest time?' or 'Which time is the longest?' to align the question intent with the context. Alternatively, keep as-is since GL does test careful reading of what's being asked.
subject: maths

title: D2 may be too low for £1.25 × 8
description: Q85: 'Each bottle of water costs £1.25. How much do 8 bottles cost?' is rated D2. While £1.25 × 8 requires mental multiplication with hundredths, the partitioning method (1×8=8, 0.25×8=2, total=10) is quite accessible. This sits at the D1/D2 boundary. Not a significant issue — just noting it's on the easy side of D2.
suggestedFix: No change needed — D2 is defensible given the hundredths involved.
subject: maths

title: Explanation method for 0.72 ÷ 0.9 is unclear
description: Q102 explanation says '72 ÷ 90 = 0.8. Or: 7.2 ÷ 9 = 0.8.' The first method (72 ÷ 90) produces 0.8 but the division itself is not obviously easier for a child than the original. The second method (multiplying both by 10) is clearer and matches the lesson content about making the divisor a whole number.
suggestedFix: Rewrite explanation to lead with the clearer method: 'Multiply both numbers by 10 to make the divisor whole: 7.2 ÷ 9 = 0.8. Check: 0.8 × 0.9 = 0.72.'
subject: maths

title: Question requires rounding-up-in-context reasoning that isn't purely decimals
description: Q179: 'A factory makes 12,500 items packed into boxes of 1000. How many boxes are needed?' The mathematical answer is 12.5 but the correct answer is listed as 13 (you can't have half a box). While this is a valid GL-style question testing context interpretation, the question involves whole-number division more than decimals. The decimal element is the interpretation of the 0.5 remainder, but the calculation itself is 12500÷1000 which is a whole-number operation.
suggestedFix: Consider whether this question is better placed in a different topic. If kept in decimals, the question is fine but should be acknowledged as primarily testing 'interpretation of remainders in context' rather than decimal arithmetic.
subject: maths

title: Question involves no decimal arithmetic — pure whole-number division
description: Q181: 'A swimming pool holds 4,750 litres of water. The pool is drained at a rate of 100 litres per minute. How many minutes does it take to drain to exactly 250 litres remaining?' The calculation is (4750-250)÷100 = 4500÷100 = 45. This is entirely whole-number arithmetic. There are no decimals in the question, options, or working. It belongs in a different topic (e.g., long division, or a general problem-solving section).
suggestedFix: Move this question to a more appropriate topic or rework it to involve decimal arithmetic (e.g., draining at 12.5 litres per minute).
subject: maths

title: Duplicate option '6' in interact screen multiple-choice
description: In the decimal-place-value-keyfact lesson's interact screen ('What is the value of the 6 in the tenths column of 24.63?'), the options list contains '6' twice: [0.6, 6, 60, 6, 0.06]. Having the same option appear twice is confusing for children and looks like a data error.
suggestedFix: Replace the duplicate '6' with a different distractor, e.g. '0.006' (thousandths confusion) or '600'.
subject: maths

title: ~20 addition questions mapped to sub-concept with no lessons
description: Questions involving decimal addition (Q2, Q12, Q16, Q25, Q28, Q36, Q44, Q52, Q61, Q71, Q76, Q89, Q95, Q98, Q108, Q125, Q132) are mapped to 'master-column-addition-decimals' which has no lessons (lessonTeachSummary shows '[No lesson found]' and learningGoal is empty). This means children taking the focused learning path for these questions receive no pre-teaching before attempting addition-of-decimals questions.
suggestedFix: Create lessons for this sub-concept (see MATHS-DEC-META-003). Until then, consider remapping these questions to 'subtracting-decimals' which covers the column alignment skill, or create a temporary redirect to that lesson.
subject: maths

title: Whole-number questions mapped to decimal sub-concepts
description: Q179 (12500÷1000=13 boxes) and Q181 (4750-250=4500÷100=45 minutes) involve no decimal arithmetic but are in the decimals topic. Their mapping to 'multiply-by-10-100-1000' and 'divide-by-10-100-1000' sub-concepts is tangentially related but the lessons teach decimal digit-shifting, which isn't what these questions test.
suggestedFix: Either rework the questions to involve decimal numbers, or move them to a more appropriate topic.
subject: maths

title: Explanation could better connect decimal and fraction reasoning
description: Q154: 'In a class, 0.35 walk to school and 0.4 travel by car. The remaining 5 children cycle. How many children in the class?' The explanation is clear but could better bridge the connection between 'the remaining fraction is 0.25' and 'if 0.25 of the class = 5, then 1 whole class = 5÷0.25 = 20'. The step '5 ÷ 0.25 = 20' is presented without explaining WHY you divide.
suggestedFix: Add to explanation: '0.25 means 1/4 of the class. If 1/4 = 5, then the whole class = 5 × 4 = 20. Or: 5 ÷ 0.25 = 20 because we need to find how many groups of 0.25 fit into 5.'
subject: maths

title: Heavy repetition of identical question patterns in original 35 questions
description: The original Q1-Q35 set and the V2 Q36-Q135 set both follow extremely similar patterns. For example: Q1/Q15/Q40/Q48/Q67/Q86/Q100/Q113/Q128 are all 'five items measured, find largest/smallest' comparison questions with near-identical structure. Q2/Q12/Q25/Q36/Q44/Q61/Q76/Q89/Q95/Q108/Q125/Q132 are all 'add two 1dp decimals' questions. While repetition aids practice, the degree of pattern repetition means children may learn to recognise the template rather than develop flexible decimal reasoning.
suggestedFix: Diversify question stems for repeated patterns. For comparison questions, vary the format: some as ordering (already done with Q9, Q56, Q79, Q116, Q145), some as 'which is closest to X' (Q4, Q62), some as 'true or false: 0.45 > 0.5'. For addition, vary the number of decimal places between addends to test alignment skill.
subject: maths

title: Recurring decimals only lightly covered
description: GL research identifies recurring decimals (Trap 10) as a specific test area: '1/3 = 0.333... Children may write 0.3 or 0.33 as the exact equivalent.' The question bank has Q202 (7/11 recurring) and Q204 (5/6 recurring) plus Q194 (4/9 recurring) which touch this, but there are no questions specifically testing whether children understand that some fractions produce non-terminating decimals, or questions asking them to identify which fractions are recurring vs terminating.
suggestedFix: Add 2-3 D3 questions: e.g. 'Which of these fractions gives a recurring decimal? 1/4, 1/5, 1/3, 1/8, 1/10' or 'Which is the best decimal approximation for 1/3? 0.3, 0.33, 0.333, 0.3333, They are all equally good'.
subject: maths

title: 10 questions mapped to 'master-adding-fractions' sub-concept have no lesson
description: Questions Q12, Q26, Q32, Q45, Q57, Q82, Q104, Q113, Q120, Q125 are all adding-fractions-with-different-denominators questions mapped to 'master-adding-fractions', which has '[No lesson found]'. These questions require finding a common denominator where one denominator is a multiple of the other (e.g. 1/2 + 1/4, 2/3 + 1/6). The existing 'adding-diff-denom' lesson covers the full LCD case (e.g. 1/3 + 1/4) but these questions are mapped to a separate missing sub-concept instead. Children encountering these questions in Focused Learning get zero teaching preparation.
suggestedFix: Either (a) create a 'master-adding-fractions' lesson that teaches the simpler 'one is a multiple' case, or (b) remap these 10 questions to 'adding-diff-denom' which already has a lesson. Option (b) is quickest and the existing lesson would adequately prepare children.
subject: maths

title: D1:D2:D3 distribution (50:32:19) heavily skewed vs GL target (30:40:30)
description: D1 has 104 questions (49.5%) vs target ~30%. D2 has 67 (31.9%) vs target ~40%. D3 has 39 (18.6%) vs target ~30%. This means practice is too heavily weighted towards easy questions. Children get insufficient exposure to the medium and hard questions that differentiate scores in the actual GL exam.
suggestedFix: Promote ~20 D1 questions to D2 (candidates: Q14 fraction-of-amount with small round numbers, Q133 convert 0.35, Q181-183 simple different-denom subtraction where one denom is a multiple). Add ~15 new D3 questions covering: reverse/working-backwards, fraction-of-a-fraction, ordering mixed FDP representations, multi-step 'of the remainder' problems.
subject: maths

title: Zero questions on dividing fractions — a Year 6 statutory requirement and GL-tested skill
description: The GL research file lists 'Multiplying & dividing fractions' at ~10% estimated weight. The National Curriculum requires Year 6 pupils to 'Divide proper fractions by whole numbers (e.g., 1/3 / 2 = 1/6)'. The question bank has no dividing-fractions questions at all, and no corresponding lesson. This is a real gap for exam preparation.
suggestedFix: Add 10-15 dividing-fractions questions across D1-D3 (e.g. D1: '1/4 ÷ 2', D2: '3/8 ÷ 3', D3: 'How many 2/5 are in 4?'). Create a corresponding dividing-fractions lesson sub-concept.
subject: maths

title: Q55 correct answer is unsimplified '6/10' when '3/5' is also an option
description: Q55 asks '9/10 - 3/10' and marks '6/10' (option A, index 0) as correct. However option B is '3/5' which is the simplified form. The explanation even says 'Simplify: 6/10 = 3/5'. GL Assessment expects answers in simplest form unless stated otherwise. Having the unsimplified form as the 'correct' answer while the simplified form is present as a distractor is misleading — a child who correctly simplifies would pick '3/5' and be marked wrong.
suggestedFix: Change correct answer from index 0 ('6/10') to index 1 ('3/5'). Update explanation to lead with the simplified answer.
subject: maths

title: Q78 correct answer is unsimplified '4/8' when '1/2' is also an option
description: Q78 asks '1/8 + 3/8' and marks '4/8' (option B, index 1) as correct. However option C is '1/2' which is the simplified form. The explanation says 'Simplify: 4/8 = 1/2'. Same issue as Q55 — the simplified answer is present but not marked correct.
suggestedFix: Change correct answer from index 1 ('4/8') to index 2 ('1/2'). Update explanation accordingly.
subject: maths

title: Q97 correct answer is '6/12' when '1/2' is also an option — same unsimplified pattern
description: Q97 asks '11/12 - 5/12' and marks '6/12' (option A, index 0) as correct, but '1/2' (option B) is also present. The explanation says 'Simplify: 6/12 = 1/2'. Unlike Q55/Q78, this question doesn't explicitly ask for simplest form, but it follows the same problematic pattern where a child who correctly simplifies is penalised.
suggestedFix: Change correct answer to '1/2' (index 1), or remove '1/2' from options and replace with a different distractor.
subject: maths

title: Q100 correct answer is '4/8' when '1/2' is also an option — same unsimplified pattern
description: Q100 asks '3/8 + 1/8' and marks '4/8' (option B, index 1) as correct, but '1/2' (option C) is also present. Explanation says 'Simplify: 4/8 = 1/2'. Identical issue to Q55/Q78/Q97.
suggestedFix: Change correct answer to '1/2' (index 2), or remove '1/2' from options.
subject: maths

title: Q102 correct answer is '6/9' when '2/3' is also an option — same unsimplified pattern
description: Q102 asks '8/9 - 2/9' and marks '6/9' (option A, index 0) as correct, but '2/3' (option B) is also present. Explanation says 'Simplify: 6/9 = 2/3'.
suggestedFix: Change correct answer to '2/3' (index 1), or remove '2/3' from options.
subject: maths

title: Q119 correct answer is '8/12' when '2/3' is also an option
description: Q119 asks '7/12 + 1/12' and marks '8/12' (option A, index 0) as correct, but '2/3' (option B) is also present. Explanation says 'Simplify: 8/12 = 2/3'. Same systematic issue.
suggestedFix: Change correct answer to '2/3' (index 1), or remove '2/3' from options.
subject: maths

title: Q123 correct answer is '6/9' when '2/3' is also an option
description: Q123 asks '4/9 + 2/9' and marks '6/9' (option B, index 1) as correct, but '2/3' (option C, index 2) is also present. Explanation says 'Simplify: 6/9 = 2/3'. Same systematic issue.
suggestedFix: Change correct answer to '2/3' (index 2), or remove '2/3' from options.
subject: maths

title: Q127 correct answer is '9/15' when '3/5' is also an option
description: Q127 asks '13/15 - 4/15' and marks '9/15' (option A, index 0) as correct, but '3/5' (option B, index 1) is also present. Explanation says 'Simplify: 9/15 = 3/5'. Same systematic issue.
suggestedFix: Change correct answer to '3/5' (index 1), or remove '3/5' from options.
subject: maths

title: Q116 correct answer is '2/5' but '4/10' (unsimplified) is the direct calculation result
description: Q116 asks '8/10 - 4/10' and marks '2/5' (option B, index 1) as correct. This is actually fine — the simplified form IS the correct answer here. However this is inconsistent with Q55, Q78, Q97, Q100, Q102, Q119, Q123, Q127 where the unsimplified form is marked correct. The inconsistency is confusing for children practising.
suggestedFix: This question is correct as-is. The fix is to make the other 8 questions consistent with this approach (simplified = correct).
subject: maths

title: Q138 (fraction × fraction) mapped to lesson that only teaches fraction × whole number
description: Q138 asks 'Lily has 3/4 of a pizza. She gives 2/3 of what she has to her brother. What fraction of the whole pizza does her brother get?' This requires 2/3 × 3/4 (fraction times fraction). It's mapped to 'multiplying-fractions' which only teaches fraction × whole number (e.g. 2/5 × 3). The lesson doesn't cover multiplying two fractions together. Same issue affects Q146 (3/5 × 5/9), Q199 (2/3 × 3/5), Q200 (3/4 × 2/7), Q201 (2/3 × 3/4).
suggestedFix: Either expand the multiplying-fractions lesson to cover fraction × fraction (with the 'multiply tops, multiply bottoms' method), or create a separate sub-concept. The existing lesson name 'Multiplying Fractions by Whole Numbers' makes clear it doesn't cover this case.
subject: maths

title: Q67 rated D3 but is a straightforward D2 subtraction with different denominators
description: Q67 asks 'A recipe needs 2/3 cup of sugar. Sophie has 1/6 cup. How much more does she need?' This is a simple subtraction: 2/3 - 1/6 = 4/6 - 1/6 = 3/6 = 1/2. It's a two-step problem (find LCD, subtract) with friendly numbers and one denominator is a multiple of the other. This matches D2 criteria, not D3.
suggestedFix: Reclassify from D3 to D2.
subject: maths

title: Q10 rated D2 but is a simple D1 same-denominator subtraction
description: Q10 asks 'A pizza is cut into 8 equal slices. Tom eats 3 slices. What fraction of the pizza is left?' This is 8/8 - 3/8 = 5/8. Single step, same denominator, small familiar numbers. The only 'extra' step is recognising that 3 slices out of 8 = 3/8, which is basic for a 9-year-old. Matches D1 criteria.
suggestedFix: Reclassify from D2 to D1.
subject: maths

title: Q19 rated D2 but is basic simplification (D1)
description: Q19 asks 'In a bag of 60 sweets, 15 are red. What fraction are red?' This is 15/60 = 1/4. It requires forming the fraction and simplifying — but 15 and 60 are very friendly numbers (divide by 15). This is the same difficulty as other D1 simplification questions (e.g. Q13: 18/24, Q40: 15/25).
suggestedFix: Reclassify from D2 to D1.
subject: maths

title: Very few visual/diagram fraction questions despite GL emphasis
description: Only 9 questions have visual components (all SVG images for the first 35 questions). The GL research notes visual/diagram fractions as a distinct question type: 'What fraction of this shape is shaded?' with potentially irregular divisions. The question bank has no 'fraction of a shape' visual questions — all visuals are illustrative (bottle, pizza, jugs) rather than requiring the child to interpret a diagram to find the fraction.
suggestedFix: Add 5-10 visual fraction questions where children must determine what fraction of a shape is shaded, including: regular grids with partial shading, shapes divided into unequal parts, composite shapes. These test a distinct GL skill.
subject: maths

title: Q1 has both '1/2' and '2/4' as options — these are equivalent fractions
description: Q1 asks 'What fraction has Aisha drunk?' (1/4 + 1/4). Options include '1/2' (correct, index 1) and '2/4' (index 2). Since 2/4 = 1/2, there are technically two correct answers. The intended design is that 2/4 is the unsimplified distractor, but a child who picks 2/4 is mathematically correct. In a real GL exam, equivalent fractions in the options would not include both simplified and unsimplified as separate choices unless the question explicitly asks for simplest form.
suggestedFix: Either add 'in its simplest form' to the question, or replace '2/4' with a different distractor (e.g. '3/8').
subject: maths

title: Wrong answer: 250 / 12 = 20 remainder 10, but answer marked as 21
description: Q172 asks 'Chairs are arranged in rows of 12 for a school assembly. There are 250 children. How many full rows are there?' The correct answer is 20 full rows (12 x 20 = 240, remainder 10). The marked answer is 21 (option C, index 2). The explanation even states 'remainder 10' and then contradictorily says '20 full rows' but also claims the remaining '10 children sit in an incomplete 21st row, but only 20 rows are full'. Despite the explanation correctly stating 20, the correctAnswer field says '21' and correct index is 2 (pointing to 21). The correct answer should be 20 (index 1).
suggestedFix: Change correct from 2 to 1, and correctAnswer from '21' to '20'. The explanation is actually correct — just the answer index is wrong.
subject: maths

title: D3 over-represented at 41% (target 30%), D1 under-represented at 23% (target 30%)
description: Current distribution is D1:60(23%), D2:97(37%), D3:108(41%). GL target is approximately 30:40:30. The D3 block (Q46-Q130) contains 80+ questions that follow an identical pattern: large 4-5 digit dividend divided by a 2-digit divisor (80-99 range) giving an exact quotient. While the divisors are large, these are all single-step exact divisions — the only thing making them D3 is the size of the numbers. Per the GL research, D3 should involve multi-step problems, disguised division, remainder interpretation in non-obvious ways, working backwards, or decimal division — not just bigger numbers.
suggestedFix: Reclassify approximately 20-30 of the single-step exact-division D3 questions (those with divisors 60-80 that divide exactly) as D2. Add 15-20 genuinely D1 questions using 1-digit divisors with smaller dividends. Alternatively, replace some of the repetitive D3 block with genuinely harder question types (multi-step, working backwards, decimal).
subject: maths

title: No inverse/missing-number questions (GL Type 3)
description: The GL research identifies 'inverse / missing number' as a common division question type: 'What number multiplied by 23 gives 621?', '_ x 15 = 450', 'I think of a number, multiply by 8, and get 296. What was my number?' None of the 265 questions test this skill. This is a significant gap because it tests conceptual understanding of division as inverse of multiplication, which GL explicitly assesses.
suggestedFix: Add 8-10 inverse/missing-number questions across D1-D3. D1: '_ x 7 = 63', D2: 'What number times 23 gives 621?', D3: 'I think of a number, multiply by 17, then add 45. My answer is 249. What was my number?'
subject: maths

title: No working-backwards questions (GL Type 6)
description: The GL research identifies 'working backwards' as a key D3 question type: 'A number divided by 14 gives 23 remainder 8. What is the number?' (answer: 14 x 23 + 8 = 330). This requires understanding the division algorithm (dividend = divisor x quotient + remainder). None of the 265 questions test this. It is specifically listed as a GL trap (Trap 7) where children forget to add the remainder.
suggestedFix: Add 5-8 working-backwards questions at D2-D3 difficulty. E.g. 'When I divide my number by 25, I get exactly 36. What is my number?', 'A number divided by 14 gives 23 remainder 8. What is the number?'
subject: maths

title: No decimal-answer division questions (GL Type 5)
description: Every money question in the bank divides exactly to a whole-pound answer. GL tests questions like 'Four friends share a restaurant bill of £97.60 equally. How much does each pay?' (£24.40) or 'A piece of wood is 3.78m long. It is cut into 6 equal pieces. How long is each piece?' (0.63m). The absence of decimal-answer questions means children never practise this GL-tested skill.
suggestedFix: Add 5-8 questions at D2-D3 where the division produces a decimal answer, particularly in money and measurement contexts. E.g. '£45.60 shared among 8 children = £5.70', 'A 3.78m plank cut into 6 equal pieces = 0.63m'.
subject: maths

title: 85 D3 questions follow identical pattern with consecutive-integer options
description: Questions 46-130 overwhelmingly follow the same template: '[Entity] has [large number] [items] to [share/pack/divide] into [groups of large 2-digit number]. How many [groups]?' Every single one has 5 consecutive integers as options (e.g. 40,41,42,43,44 or 55,56,57,58,59). While consecutive-integer distractors are a valid GL pattern (Pattern 6 in the research), using ONLY this pattern for 85 straight questions eliminates variety. GL also uses off-by-one errors, remainder mishandling, place-value errors, and wrong-operation distractors. The correct answer is almost always at index 2 (the middle option) — see answer position issue below.
suggestedFix: Vary distractor patterns: include some options with wider spacing (e.g. 20, 25, 30, 35, 40), some with a remainder-mishandling trap, and some where the correct answer is NOT the middle option. Also vary the question structure — not all should be single-step exact divisions.
subject: maths

title: Extreme answer position bias — correct answer is option C (index 2) in ~80% of questions
description: Of the 265 questions, approximately 210+ have the correct answer at index 2 (option C, the middle of 5 options). This is a severe position bias. GL Assessment distributes answers roughly evenly across A-E. A child who simply always picks C would score anomalously high on this topic. In the original Q1-Q130 block, virtually every answer is at index 2. The v2 questions (Q131+) introduce slightly more variety but still heavily favour index 1 and 2.
suggestedFix: Redistribute correct answer positions to be roughly 20% per position (A through E). This requires adjusting the option sets so the correct answer appears at different positions while maintaining plausible distractors.
subject: maths

title: No questions testing 'zero in the quotient' — a key GL trap
description: The GL research identifies 'zero in the quotient' as Trap 2 and the single most common procedural error in long division. Example: 3024 / 14 = 216, or 2040 / 12 = 170 where the zero IS part of the answer. None of the 265 questions have a quotient containing a zero (like 102, 204, 305, etc. where the zero must be written). Questions Q110 and Q113 have answers of 101, but these involve 4-digit dividends divided by 92/96 — they don't specifically test the zero-in-quotient trap where children might skip the zero.
suggestedFix: Add 4-6 questions where the quotient contains a zero, specifically designed to expose this error. E.g. '3024 / 14 = ?' with distractors including 216, 26, 2016, 2160 (common zero-error answers). Include both D2 and D3 versions.
subject: maths

title: master-bus-stop sub-concept has no lesson content — 20 questions orphaned
description: The mapping file shows 20 D1 questions mapped to 'master-bus-stop' where lessonTeachSummary is '[No lesson found]' and learningGoal is empty. These include Q1, Q6, Q10, and many v2 D1 questions (Q131-Q154). Children answering these questions have no micro-lesson to prepare them. The 'sharing-equally' sub-concept has lessons but is only mapped to basic sharing questions, not to bus-stop-method D1 questions like '156 / 12 = 13'.
suggestedFix: Either create a lesson for the master-bus-stop sub-concept covering the bus stop (short division) method for 1-digit and friendly 2-digit divisors, OR remap these questions to the existing sharing-equally or short-division-remainders sub-concepts as appropriate.
subject: maths

title: Long division lesson teach summary missing from mapping — shows '[No lesson found]'
description: The long-division-method sub-concept has 2 lessons but the mapping shows lessonTeachSummary as '[No lesson found]' for all questions mapped to it (Q2, Q5, Q7, Q8, Q11, Q12, Q15-Q19, etc.). The lesson exists in the lesson data but the mapping isn't picking up the teach summary. This suggests a mapping extraction issue rather than a content issue. The lesson itself is good — clear step-by-step BusStopDiagram walkthrough of 432 / 18 with EMSB (Estimate, Multiply, Subtract, Bring down) cycle.
suggestedFix: Fix the mapping extraction so lessonTeachSummary populates for long-division-method. The lesson content itself is fine.
subject: maths

title: Multiple consolidate screens have no body content
description: Several consolidate screens across the lessons have null body and no bodyParts, visual, or interaction — they would render as blank screens. Affected lessons: short-division-remainders-steps (line 488), short-division-remainders-mistake (line 620), long-division-method-steps (line 883), long-division-method-mistake (line 1047), interpreting-remainders-mistake (line 1345). The consolidate screen is meant to reinforce the key takeaway — a blank screen wastes this opportunity.
suggestedFix: Add a brief summary body to each empty consolidate screen. E.g. for short-division-remainders: 'When the number doesn't divide exactly, the bit left over is the **remainder**. Always check: the remainder must be **smaller** than the number you're dividing by!'
subject: maths

title: Q155 rated D2 but uses single-digit divisor (7) — should be D1
description: Q155: 'A charity has 168 selection boxes to share equally among 7 children's homes. How many boxes does each home receive?' 168 / 7 = 24. Dividing by 7 with a 3-digit dividend is solidly within times-table recall range (7 x 24 = 168). Per the GL research, D1 includes division by single-digit numbers up to 3-digit dividends with no remainder. This is D1.
suggestedFix: Reclassify Q155 from D2 to D1.
subject: maths

title: Q156 rated D2 but uses single-digit divisor (9) — should be D1
description: Q156: 'A scout leader shares 252 badges equally among 9 scout groups.' 252 / 9 = 28. Dividing by 9 with a 3-digit dividend and no remainder is D1 per the GL criteria (single-digit divisor, straightforward sharing).
suggestedFix: Reclassify Q156 from D2 to D1.
subject: maths

title: Q157 rated D2 but uses single-digit divisor (8) — should be D1
description: Q157: 'A farmer shares 336 eggs equally among 8 shops.' 336 / 8 = 42. Same pattern — single-digit divisor, 3-digit dividend, exact division. This is D1.
suggestedFix: Reclassify Q157 from D2 to D1.
subject: maths

title: Q46 rated D3 but is a straightforward single-step division — should be D2
description: Q46: 'A school raises £1,927 at a fair and shares it equally among 47 charities. How many pounds does each charity receive?' 1927 / 47 = 41. While 47 is a non-friendly 2-digit divisor and the dividend is 4-digits, this is a single-step exact division with no remainder interpretation, no multi-step reasoning, no disguised operation. Per the GL research, D3 requires multi-step, non-obvious remainder interpretation, decimal answers, working backwards, or disguised division. This fits D2 criteria: 2-digit divisor, 4-digit dividend, requires formal long division method.
suggestedFix: Reclassify Q46 from D3 to D2. This applies to many questions in the Q46-Q70 range that share the same single-step pattern with divisors in the 40-70 range. A systematic reclassification pass would move about 20-25 of these to D2.
subject: maths

title: Checking/estimation questions have explanations that give away more than needed
description: Several estimation and checking questions (Q180, Q182, Q185, Q187, etc.) include the full correct calculation in the explanation, which is fine for review. However, some explanations reveal the correct answer to a DIFFERENT calculation than what was asked. For example, Q185 asks 'explain why 891/9 = 89 must be wrong' — the explanation says 'the correct answer is 99' which goes beyond the question. Q182 asks 'Is 576/24 = 26 correct?' and the explanation gives the full correct answer of 24. These explanations are correct and educational, but could be more focused on the checking METHOD rather than giving the full answer.
suggestedFix: Minor tone refinement: lead with the checking method ('89 x 9 = 801, not 891 — so the answer is wrong') before stating the correct answer. This reinforces the checking skill rather than just giving the answer.
subject: maths

title: No micro-lessons exist for Long Multiplication
description: All 228 question mappings show '[No lesson found]'. The topic has sub-concepts defined (master-column-method, word-problems, short-multiplication, estimation, multiply-tens, partitioning, grid-method, column-ones, column-tens, column-adding, column-full) but zero actual lessons. Children go straight into quizzes with no teaching.
suggestedFix: Build micro-lessons covering the 11 sub-concepts. Priority order: (1) master-column-method (the core skill), (2) partitioning, (3) grid-method, (4) estimation, (5) word-problems, (6) multiply-tens, (7) short-multiplication, then column-method breakdown steps.
subject: maths

title: Difficulty distribution skewed: D1:20% D2:59% D3:21% vs target 30:40:30
description: 46 D1 questions (20%), 134 D2 questions (59%), 48 D3 questions (21%). GL target is approximately 30:40:30. D2 is heavily overrepresented. Many D2 questions involve 3-digit × 2-digit calculations producing 5-digit answers (e.g. Q25: 408×37=15,096; Q64: 418×32=13,376) that are arguably D3 difficulty. Meanwhile some D3 questions (Q36: 83×46; Q46: 208×46) are comparable to standard D2.
suggestedFix: Reclassify approximately 25 D2 questions with larger numbers (3-digit × 2-digit producing 10,000+) as D3. Add approximately 20 new D1 questions or reclassify easier D2 questions (2-digit × 2-digit with friendly numbers) as D1. Target: ~68 D1, ~91 D2, ~69 D3.
subject: maths

title: Missing £ sign in answer options for money question
description: Q17: 'A sports club has 93 members, and each member pays £47 for a kit. What is the total cost of all the kits?' The answer options are plain numbers: 4,271 / 4,321 / 4,371 / 4,421 / 4,471. Since the question explicitly asks about cost in £, the options should include £ signs for consistency and to teach proper mathematical notation. Compare Q6 (£37 × 28) which correctly uses £ in options.
suggestedFix: Add £ prefix to all 5 options: £4,271 / £4,321 / £4,371 / £4,421 / £4,471. Update correctAnswer to '£4,371'.
subject: maths

title: Missing £ sign in answer options for money question
description: Q23: 'A charity walk has 176 walkers, and each walker raises £35. How much money is raised altogether?' Options lack £ signs: 6,060 / 6,110 / 6,160 / 6,210 / 6,260. This is inconsistent with other money questions that do include £.
suggestedFix: Add £ prefix to all 5 options: £6,060 / £6,110 / £6,160 / £6,210 / £6,260. Update correctAnswer to '£6,160'.
subject: maths

title: Missing £ sign in answer options for money question
description: Q58: 'A rugby club has 97 members, and each member pays £66 in annual fees. What is the total amount collected?' Options lack £ signs: 6,352 / 6,382 / 6,402 / 6,432 / 6,462. Inconsistent with other monetary questions.
suggestedFix: Add £ prefix to all 5 options: £6,352 / £6,382 / £6,402 / £6,432 / £6,462. Update correctAnswer to '£6,402'.
subject: maths

title: 18 questions are basic/short multiplication, not long multiplication
description: Questions 126-143 involve single-digit multipliers (24×3, 15×12, 4×13, 36×5, 11×14, 20×18, 43×7, 3×25, 16×11, 12×8, 32×9, 6×45, 25×16, 6×19, 14×13, 8×35, 50×9, 7×12). Many of these are times-table facts or simple mental arithmetic, not genuine long multiplication. A child who knows their times tables to 12 can answer most of these without any long multiplication method.
suggestedFix: Consider reclassifying these as a 'short multiplication' sub-topic, or replacing some with genuine long multiplication at D1 difficulty (e.g. 23×12, 34×11 -- small 2-digit × 2-digit that require the method but use friendly numbers).
subject: maths

title: Distractor pattern is too uniform -- nearly all use evenly spaced intervals
description: The vast majority of word problem questions (Q1-Q125) use distractors that are evenly spaced at fixed intervals (typically ±50 or ±25 from the correct answer). For example Q1: 302/312/322/332/342 (interval 10), Q2: 1,142/1,222/1,242/1,342/1,442 (varying but similar). GL Assessment uses more sophisticated distractors based on common calculation errors: carrying errors, forgetting to add zero for tens row, wrong times-table facts, place-value mistakes. Evenly-spaced distractors can be gamed by estimation alone.
suggestedFix: For at least 30% of questions, replace generic evenly-spaced distractors with error-based distractors. For example, for 46×27=1,242: include 1,142 (forgetting to carry), 1,382 (wrong times-table 46×7=282 instead of 322), 920 (only computing 46×20), 1,272 (adding tens incorrectly). This better simulates GL paper design.
subject: maths

title: No inverse/missing-number multiplication questions
description: All 228 questions ask children to find the product. GL papers also test inverse multiplication: 'A number multiplied by 34 gives 1,156. What is the number?' or '? × 48 = 3,360'. This is a distinct skill (using division to check/find factors) that appears in GL assessments.
suggestedFix: Add 10-15 inverse multiplication questions at D2/D3 difficulty. These test deeper understanding and are commonly seen in GL papers.
subject: maths

title: Two questions have identical answer sets
description: Q75 (75×86=6,450) and Q78 (86×75=6,450) have identical options: 6,400/6,430/6,450/6,480/6,530. They also have the same correct answer 6,450 at index 2. The questions use the same two numbers (75 and 86) just in reverse order, making them effectively duplicate questions.
suggestedFix: Replace one of these questions with a different multiplication. The commutativity is not being tested here -- both are straightforward word problems.
subject: maths

title: No multi-step problems combining multiplication with other operations
description: GL papers frequently embed multiplication within multi-step problems: 'A school buys 24 boxes of pencils at £3 each and 18 boxes of pens at £5 each. What is the total cost?' None of the 228 questions require a second operation after multiplication.
suggestedFix: Add 5-10 multi-step questions at D2/D3 that combine multiplication with addition, subtraction, or comparison. These are common GL formats.
subject: maths

title: Word problem contexts become repetitive and adult-oriented
description: Many higher-numbered questions (Q50-Q125) use adult/industrial contexts: warehouse shipping, factory production, power stations, logistics companies, wind farms, solar panels, bottling plants, crisp factories. While these are valid contexts, they're less engaging for a 10-year-old than school, sports, pets, or family scenarios. GL papers tend to use child-relevant contexts.
suggestedFix: Replace 15-20 adult/industrial contexts with child-friendly scenarios: birthday parties, school events, pocket money, sports teams, holiday activities, cooking/baking, nature/animals. Keep some variety but ensure at least 60% of contexts are relatable to a 10-year-old.
subject: maths

title: 24 × 3 is times-table level, not long multiplication
description: Q126: 'A packet of crisps has 24 crisps inside. How many crisps are in 3 packets?' The answer 72 is a basic times-table fact (24×3). While labelled D1, this doesn't test long multiplication skills at all. Similarly Q135 (12×8=96) and Q143 (7×12=84) are pure times-table recall.
suggestedFix: Either reclassify as 'times tables' or replace with a proper D1 long multiplication question like 23×14 or 31×12 that requires the column/grid method but uses small, friendly numbers.
subject: maths

title: Explanations use only one method -- should occasionally show alternatives
description: Questions 1-125 all explain using the partitioning/compensation strategy (e.g. '46 × 27: (46 × 20 = 920) + (46 × 7 = 322) = 1,242'). While this is a valid and efficient mental method, GL expects children to know the column method. The explanations never demonstrate the formal column method layout, and never mention checking by estimation. Children may not connect the explanation method to what they're learning in the column-method and grid-method sections (Q193-Q222).
suggestedFix: For a subset of questions (especially D1 and early D2), show the column method layout in the explanation, or at least add 'You could also use the column method' as a note. Consider varying explanation methods: some partitioning, some grid, some column.
subject: maths

title: Unrealistic context: 99-metre running track
description: Q108: 'A running track is 99 metres around. If an athlete runs 79 laps, how many metres does she run?' A 99-metre track is not a standard track size (standard is 200m or 400m). While the maths is fine, using an unrealistic number may confuse children who know about real running tracks. The 99 was likely chosen for the compensation method (99×79 = 100×79 - 79), but the context is jarring.
suggestedFix: Change to a more natural context: 'A corridor is 99 metres long' or 'A garden path is 99 metres'. Alternatively use a realistic track (200m or 400m) with a different number of laps.
subject: maths

title: Q170 correct answer is wrong — should be -31, not -26
description: Sequence: 12, 7, 2, -3, -8, ... (subtract 5 each time). Term 6: -8 - 5 = -13. Term 7: -13 - 5 = -18. Sum of 6th and 7th terms: -13 + (-18) = -31. The question lists correct answer as '-26' (index 1) but -31 (index 0) is actually correct. The explanation text also contains the error: it states 'Sum: -13 + (-18) = -31' which is mathematically correct but then contradicts the marked answer of -26.
suggestedFix: Change correct index from 1 to 0. The correct answer is -31. The explanation already shows the correct working (-13 + -18 = -31) so only the correct index needs updating.
subject: maths

title: 62 questions mapped to 'master-crossing-zero' which has no lessons
description: The sub-concept 'master-crossing-zero' appears in the mappings with 62 questions (Q1, Q4, Q12, Q13, Q18, Q21, Q23, Q29, Q32, Q33, Q40, Q41, Q50, and many more) but '[No lesson found]' is shown for all of them. These are questions where the child must add to a negative and cross zero to get a positive result (e.g. -12 + 17 = 5). This is one of the most common GL negative number question patterns and currently has zero teaching support.
suggestedFix: Create 2 lessons for 'master-crossing-zero': (1) a step-by-step lesson showing how to bridge through zero when adding a large positive to a negative, using the 'go to zero, then keep going' method; (2) a spot-the-mistake lesson addressing the common error of subtracting instead of adding, or getting the wrong sign. These should follow the existing template patterns with NumberLine visuals.
subject: maths

title: No negative multiplication or division questions despite GL testing this at D3
description: GL Assessment includes questions involving multiplication and division with negative numbers at D3 difficulty (e.g. -3 x 4 = -12, -20 ÷ 5 = -4, and the trickier -4 x -3 = 12). The topic has zero questions covering this skill. While this is more of a Year 6/KS3 bridge topic, GL does test simple cases and the current bank misses this entirely.
suggestedFix: Add 8-10 questions: 3 at D2 (negative x positive, e.g. -5 x 3), 5 at D3 (negative x negative giving positive, negative ÷ positive, word problems requiring multiplication of negatives). Include real-world contexts like temperature drops over multiple hours.
subject: maths

title: D2 overweight at 53% and D3 underweight at 18% vs GL targets
description: Current distribution: D1:52 (29%), D2:95 (53%), D3:32 (18%). GL target is approximately 30:40:30. The D2 count of 95 is 13 percentage points above target, while D3 at 32 is 12 percentage points below target. This imbalance means the middle ground is over-served while advanced pupils lack sufficient challenge. The D2 bloat is mainly from repetitive submarine/temperature/bank problems using the same arithmetic pattern (negative + positive or positive - larger positive).
suggestedFix: Reclassify 10-15 of the more complex D2 questions as D3 where justified (e.g. Q36, Q89 'closest to zero' questions requiring absolute value thinking, Q177 Ben Nevis altitude problem). Add 15-20 new D3 questions covering multiplication/division, coordinates, and multi-step compound problems. Target: D1 ~52, D2 ~75-80, D3 ~50-55.
subject: maths

title: Q59 and Q109 rated D3 but are simple 'furthest from zero' comparisons — should be D2
description: Q59 asks 'Which is furthest from zero? -16, 9, -5, 18, -22' and Q109 asks the same type with different numbers. These only require comparing absolute values of 5 given numbers — the same cognitive demand as 'closest to zero' questions (Q19, Q36, Q89) which are rated D2. There is no multi-step reasoning, no computation, and no tricky concept. Rating these D3 inflates the D3 count without providing genuine D3 challenge.
suggestedFix: Reclassify Q59 and Q109 from D3 to D2. Replace with genuinely harder D3 questions requiring multi-step negative number reasoning.
subject: maths

title: ~25 near-identical 'temperature rises by X°C' D2 questions with minimal variation
description: Questions Q1, Q4, Q23, Q33, Q50, Q60, Q67, Q83, Q95, Q139, and more all follow the exact same pattern: 'Temperature is -N°C. It rises/warms by M°C. What is the new temperature?' The arithmetic is always negative + positive. While some repetition builds fluency, having ~25 questions with the same structure at D2 is excessive. The context varies (cities, countries) but the mathematical demand is identical.
suggestedFix: Reduce to ~12 temperature-rise questions at D2. Replace removed questions with more varied D2/D3 types: temperature change over multiple time periods, comparing temperatures across multiple cities, finding which city has a specific temperature after a change.
subject: maths

title: ~15 near-identical submarine/diver depth questions at D2
description: Questions Q6, Q16, Q29, Q37, Q58, Q66, Q72, Q79, Q80, Q101, Q102, Q118, and more all follow: 'A submarine/diver is at -N metres. It rises/dives M metres. What is the new depth?' The arithmetic alternates between negative + positive and negative - positive. Context substitutes include whales, dolphins, robots, and potholers, but the mathematical structure is identical each time.
suggestedFix: Reduce to ~8 depth questions. Replace with more varied marine/underground problems: multi-stage journeys (dive then rise then dive), comparing depths of two submarines, calculating how far apart two divers are, finding the depth after a fractional change.
subject: maths

title: ~12 near-identical 'spends more than they have' bank balance questions
description: Questions Q3, Q24, Q38, Q51, Q62, Q65, Q81, Q86, Q99, Q106, Q115, Q150 all follow: 'X has £A. They spend/buy £B (where B > A). What is their balance?' The arithmetic is always positive - larger positive = negative. While this is a valid real-world context, the pattern is over-represented at both D1 and D2.
suggestedFix: Reduce to ~6 simple overspend questions. Replace with more varied money problems: multiple transactions, comparing two people's balances, working backwards from a final balance, finding how much more is needed to clear a debt.
subject: maths

title: Same-side difference questions (Q55, Q90, Q161) mapped to 'difference-across-zero' but lesson only teaches opposite-side technique
description: Q55 (difference between -20 and -8 = 12), Q90 (difference between -30 and -12 = 18), and Q161 (difference between -13 and -4 = 9) are all same-side-of-zero differences. They are mapped to 'Finding the Difference Across Zero' but the lesson only teaches the 'add distances from zero' technique, which only works when numbers are on opposite sides. For same-side differences, the correct method is to subtract the smaller absolute value from the larger, or equivalently count the gap. A child following the lesson's taught method on Q55 would calculate 20 + 8 = 28 (wrong).
suggestedFix: Either: (1) create a separate sub-concept 'difference-between-two-negatives' with its own lesson explaining the subtraction/counting method, or (2) extend the 'difference-across-zero' lesson to include a second section covering same-side differences with the rule 'same side = subtract, opposite sides = add'.
subject: maths

title: Q44 options lack °C units while question asks about temperature
description: Q44 asks 'The temperature in York is 2°C. It drops by 14°C overnight. What is the new temperature?' but the options are '16, 12, -12, -16, 14' without °C units. Most other temperature questions include °C in the options. This inconsistency could momentarily confuse a child, though it does not affect the answer.
suggestedFix: Add °C to all options: '16°C, 12°C, -12°C, -16°C, 14°C'.
subject: maths

title: Multiple temperature questions have options without °C units
description: Questions Q44, Q70, Q77, Q92, Q103, and Q110 all ask about temperature changes but present options as plain numbers (e.g. '-12', '17') rather than with °C. Other temperature questions in the set (Q1, Q4, Q8, Q10, Q23, Q39, Q49, etc.) correctly include °C in options. This inconsistency is a minor quality issue.
suggestedFix: Add °C to all options in Q44, Q70, Q77, Q92, Q103, and Q110 for consistency.
subject: maths

title: Multiple money questions have options without £ symbol
description: Questions Q63, Q65, Q81, Q86, Q99, and Q115 ask about bank balances or spending but present options as plain numbers (e.g. '-22', '28') rather than with £ or -£ prefix. Other money questions (Q3, Q24, Q62, Q87, Q106, Q107) correctly include £ in options. This inconsistency is minor but worth standardising.
suggestedFix: Add £ to all options in the affected questions. Use '-£22' format for negative balances to match the style of Q3, Q24 etc.
subject: maths

title: No questions on negative coordinates despite GL testing this
description: GL Assessment tests negative coordinates in the maths paper — reading and plotting points in all four quadrants (e.g. 'What are the coordinates of point P?' where P is at (-3, 2)). The negative numbers topic has zero coordinate questions. While coordinates could sit in a geometry topic, the negative number skill is the primary challenge and this is a natural home for 5-8 such questions.
suggestedFix: Add 5-8 coordinate questions: 2 at D1 (read a point with one negative coordinate), 3 at D2 (read points in third quadrant, find midpoint of two points with negatives), 2 at D3 (reflect a shape across an axis into negative coordinates, find missing vertex of a shape given 3 vertices with negatives).
subject: maths

title: Several D1 temperature-drop questions involve larger numbers than typical D1
description: Questions Q103 (14 - 42), Q110 (8 - 33), Q92 (5 - 24) are rated D1 but involve subtracting numbers in the 20s-40s from single-digit numbers. While the concept is simple (positive minus larger positive), the arithmetic with larger numbers pushes these towards D2. Compare with Q28 (3 - 11) and Q56 (4 - 19) which are also D1 but use more age-appropriate numbers. The core skill is identical but GL typically uses smaller, friendlier numbers at D1.
suggestedFix: Consider reclassifying Q103, Q110, and Q92 to D2, or reducing the numbers to keep them at genuine D1 level (e.g. 14 - 42 could become 4 - 12).
subject: maths

title: 8 near-identical 'express as %' questions all giving 30% with same options
description: Questions Q76, Q82, Q99, Q106, Q112, Q120, Q125, Q132 all follow the exact same pattern: 'What percentage is X out of Y?' where X/Y = 3/10 = 30%. All 8 have identical option sets (25%, 28%, 30%, 32%, 35%) and identical difficulty (D1). The only variation is the magnitude of the numbers (72/240, 54/180, 108/360, 135/450, 144/480, 168/560, 192/640, 216/720). This provides zero variety — a child sees the same question 8 times. These should be diversified to test different fractions (1/4=25%, 2/5=40%, 3/5=60%, etc.) with varied option sets.
suggestedFix: Replace 6 of the 8 with 'express as percentage' questions that yield different answers (25%, 40%, 60%, 75%, etc.) using different fraction ratios, varied contexts, and different option sets. Keep 2 that give 30% for practice but change their options.
subject: maths

title: 29 questions mapped to orphan sub-concept with no lesson
description: 29 questions (16% of the bank) are mapped to 'master-building-block-percentages' which has lessonTeachSummary '[No lesson found]'. These are all 'find X% of Y' questions using the building-block method (e.g. 'What is 35% of 420?', 'What is 8% of £650?'). A child doing focused learning will get these questions without any preceding lesson teaching the method.
suggestedFix: Either (a) create lessons for the 'master-building-block-percentages' sub-concept, or (b) remap these questions to the existing 'building-percentages' sub-concept which already teaches the 10%/1%/5% building-block method.
subject: maths

title: Q86 rated D3 but is a standard complement + find-percentage problem
description: Q86: 'A school has 900 pupils. If 42% passed a swimming test, how many did not pass?' is rated D3 (hard). However, this is a straightforward two-step problem: 100%-42%=58%, then 58% of 900=522. The numbers are clean (no decimals). Per GL criteria, D3 requires deeper conceptual understanding (reverse percentages, successive changes, compound problems). This is a standard D2 complement problem with larger numbers.
suggestedFix: Change difficulty to D2.
subject: maths

title: Q150 and Q152 rated D3 but are standard single-step discount problems
description: Q150 ('£320 reduced by 35%') and Q152 ('£260 reduced by 15%') are both rated D3. These are standard percentage decrease problems requiring find-the-percentage then subtract — exactly what the GL research describes as D2. The percentages (35%, 15%) are not unusual, and the numbers are clean. Q150 is identical in structure to Q37 (D2: '£80 reduced by 35%') and Q5 (D2: '£300 reduced by 15%'). The only difference is larger numbers, which doesn't warrant a difficulty jump.
suggestedFix: Change both to D2.
subject: maths

title: Q147 rated D3 but is a straightforward percentage decrease
