subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q1 and Q11 both test 'sun' as the answer with overlapping target words
description: Q1: 'sun + light/rise' (D1). Q11: 'sun + rise/set' (D1). Both share 'sunrise' as one of the two compound words being tested, and both have 'Sun' as the answer. At the same difficulty level, these are near-duplicates. Q17 (sun+light/star+light) also overlaps with Q1 on 'sunlight'.
suggestedFix: Change Q11 to test a different word entirely. Consider 'moon + light/shine' or 'day + light/dream' to maintain the celestial theme without duplicating.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: D3 severely under-represented at 19% vs GL target ~30%
description: Only 28 of 145 questions (19%) are rated D3. GL targets approximately 30% at each of D1 and D3 with 40% at D2. To match GL distribution, D3 should have ~44 questions. Meanwhile D1 has 52 questions (36%), 8-9 more than the ~43 target. Some current D2 questions test sophisticated or unusual compounds that warrant D3: Q47 (break+through/down), Q48 (cross+road/word), Q52 (water+colour/mark), Q53 (thunder+bolt/storm). These could be promoted to D3 to improve the distribution.
suggestedFix: Promote approximately 10 D2 questions with less common compounds to D3. Demote approximately 8 D1 questions to ensure D1 stays at ~30%. Target distribution: D1:43, D2:58, D3:44.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q9 explanation contains misleading distractor reasoning
description: Q9 explanation states: 'Day works with every (everyday) but not any.' However, 'Place' (option A) also arguably works: 'anyplace' and 'everyplace' are both valid English words (especially in American English). The question is still correct as 'Where' is the best British English answer, but the explanation doesn't address why 'Place' was rejected. In a GL context focusing on British English, 'anywhere/everywhere' are more standard than 'anyplace/everyplace', but this should be acknowledged.
suggestedFix: Update the explanation to note: 'While anyplace exists in American English, anywhere and everywhere are the standard British compound words tested in GL exams. Place does not form as natural a compound in British English.'
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q20 options use lowercase while all other questions use Title Case
description: Q20 options are: 'bin', 'paper', 'board', 'room', 'box' (all lowercase). Every other standard question uses Title Case options: 'Bin', 'Paper', 'Board', 'Room', 'Box'. This inconsistency could confuse a child or look unprofessional.
suggestedFix: Change Q20 options to Title Case to match the rest of the question bank.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q16 and Q46 both test 'land' with 'landlord' as a shared compound
description: Q16: 'land + scape/lord' (D3). Q46: 'land + lord/mark' (D2). Both share 'landlord' as one of the two target compounds and both have 'Land' as the answer. Q121 in the pick-from-sets section also tests 'land + lord'. This is excessive repetition of the same word family.
suggestedFix: Change Q46 to test a different compound word. Keep Q16 (D3) as the definitive 'land' question and replace Q46 with a different D2 compound.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Select-two questions lack D3 representation
description: Of the 25 select-two questions (Q101-Q125), difficulty distribution is D1:10 (40%), D2:10 (40%), D3:5 (20%). D3 is under-represented even more severely in this format than in the standard format. The 5 D3 select-two questions (Q120-Q125) all feature reasonably common compounds (blacksmith, landlord, withdraw, upstream). For D3, these should include more obscure compounds where multiple pairs look plausible.
suggestedFix: Add 3-4 more D3 select-two questions with less obvious compounds (e.g., forethought, masterpiece, counterpart) where multiple word pairs are plausible distractors.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Pick-from-sets correct pair is always position [0,0]
description: All 20 pick-from-sets questions (Q126-Q145) have the correct answer at position [0,0] — the first word from set A and the first word from set B. This creates a positional bias: a test-wise child could simply try the first word from each set every time. GL randomises answer positions to prevent this.
suggestedFix: Redistribute correct pairs across positions. Aim for roughly even distribution: some [0,1], [1,0], [2,2], [0,2] etc. Ensure the correct word appears in different positions within each set.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: 2 orphan sub-concepts have lessons but may lack sufficient question coverage
description: The mappings metadata shows 2 orphan sub-concepts. While 163 of 165 mappings are high-confidence (excellent), the 2 orphan sub-concepts may represent lesson content that doesn't have enough questions mapped to justify their existence, or questions that could benefit from these lessons but aren't linked.
suggestedFix: Review the 2 orphan sub-concepts. If they represent valid compound word categories, create questions to fill the gap. If they overlap with existing sub-concepts, merge them.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q81 explanation calls 'stock market' a compound word but it's typically two words
description: Q81 tests 'stock + market/pile'. The explanation says 'Stock + market = stock market' and calls it a compound word. However, 'stock market' is conventionally written as two separate words, not as one compound word (unlike 'stockpile' which is one word). While it is a two-word compound in linguistic terms, this is inconsistent with the rest of the question bank which focuses on single-word compounds (sunlight, rainbow, etc.). A 9-year-old may be confused by this inconsistency.
suggestedFix: Replace 'market' with a word that forms a single-word compound with 'stock': e.g., 'stockroom' (stock+room) or keep 'stockpile' but pair it with a different compound. Alternatively, change the question entirely.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Hidden word CAL is not a real English word
description: Q138 asks for 'a 3-letter word meaning a baby cow' with answer 'basic + alphabet' giving CAL. A baby cow is a CALF (4 letters), not CAL. CAL is not a standard English word. The question is unsolvable as written — no valid 3-letter word meaning 'baby cow' is hidden in the given options.
suggestedFix: Replace the question entirely. Either change the clue to match a real 3-letter word hidden across 'basic + alphabet' (CAL is not valid), or redesign with a different word pair that yields a real 3-letter word.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Self-correcting explanation text shown to children
description: Q3 explanation reads: "Take the last two letters of 'beach' (CH) and the first two of 'and' (AN) — wait, that gives CHAN. Actually, take the last one letter of 'beach' (H) and the first three of 'and' (AND) = HAND." This internal reasoning/self-correction should never be visible to a child. It models confusion rather than clarity.
suggestedFix: Replace explanation with: "The word HAND is hidden across 'beacH' and 'AND'. Take the last 1 letter of 'beach' (H) and all 3 letters of 'and' (AND) = HAND. ✓"
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Garbled leftover text in explanation
description: Q40 explanation contains editing artifacts: "...= BONE. the first three of 'onset' (ONE) — wait, 'onset' starts O-N-S, not O-N-E. ✓" This is leftover text from a previous draft that was not cleaned up. Confusing for children.
suggestedFix: Replace explanation with: "The word BONE is hidden across 'cluB' and 'ONE'. Take the last 1 letter of 'club' (B) and all 3 letters of 'one' (ONE) = BONE. ✓"
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Typo in explanation — 'wandD'
description: Q59 explanation reads: "The word DENT is hidden across 'wandD' — take the last letter of 'wand' (D)..." The 'wandD' is a typo (double D). Minor but unprofessional for a child-facing product.
suggestedFix: Replace with: "The word DENT is hidden across 'wanD' and 'ENTry'. Take the last 1 letter of 'wand' (D) and the first 3 of 'entry' (ENT) = DENT. ✓"
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Clue says 'a yellow fruit' but answer is MELON
description: Q145 asks for 'a 5-letter word meaning a yellow fruit' with answer MELON. Melons are typically green-skinned (honeydew, watermelon) or orange-skinned (cantaloupe). A lemon is the classic 'yellow fruit'. While some melon varieties have yellow flesh, the clue is misleading for a 9-year-old. The hidden word is correct (caMEL + ON = MELON) but the clue definition is poor.
suggestedFix: Change clue to 'a large round fruit' or 'a juicy fruit with seeds' — more accurate descriptions of a melon that avoid colour confusion.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Clue 'soft and smooth' is a poor definition for CREAM
description: Q150 asks for 'a 5-letter word meaning soft and smooth' with answer CREAM. CREAM is primarily a noun (dairy product, cosmetic, or colour). 'Soft and smooth' is more an adjective phrase — 'creamy' would fit, but CREAM itself does not mean 'soft and smooth'. A 9-year-old would struggle to make this connection.
suggestedFix: Change clue to 'a thick white dairy product' or 'something you put on top of a dessert' — concrete noun definitions that match how children understand the word.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Duplicate: LAMP appears in both Q23 and Q101 with same word pair
description: Q23 and Q101 both hide LAMP across 'school + ample'. Q23 clue: 'a device that gives light'. Q101 clue: 'a light you can carry'. Same hidden word, same word pair, near-identical clue. This is a wasted question slot.
suggestedFix: Remove Q101 and replace with a new question using a different hidden word.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Duplicate: BAND appears in both Q31 and Q111
description: Q31 hides BAND across 'climb + android'. Q111 hides BAND across 'club + Andrew'. Same hidden word with very similar word pairs (both use B from last word + AND from next). Both have identical clue 'a group of musicians'.
suggestedFix: Remove one and replace with a different hidden word.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Duplicate: PLEA appears in both Q82 and Q119
description: Q82 hides PLEA across 'steeple + afoot' (D3). Q119 hides PLEA across 'stop + leaving' (D3). Same hidden word, same difficulty, similar clue ('an urgent request or appeal' vs 'an urgent request'). Both D3.
suggestedFix: Remove one and replace with a different D3 hidden word.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Duplicate: SAGE appears in both Q84 and Q121
description: Q84 hides SAGE across 'mesa + get' (D3). Q121 hides SAGE across 'bus + agent' (D3). Same hidden word, both D3. Clues: 'a wise person, or an aromatic herb' vs 'a very wise person'.
suggestedFix: Remove one and replace with a different D3 hidden word.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Duplicate: VEIL appears in both Q76 and Q124
description: Q76 hides VEIL across 'grave + illness' (D2). Q124 hides VEIL across 'dove + illustrated' (D3). Same hidden word, similar clues ('a piece of cloth worn to cover the face' vs 'a thin covering for the face').
suggestedFix: Remove one and replace with a different hidden word.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Duplicate: TALL appears in both Q18 and Q103
description: Q18 hides TALL across 'great + allies' (D3). Q103 hides TALL across 'boat + allowed' (D1). Same hidden word and identical clue 'high in height'. At least they test different difficulties, but the repetition is unnecessary.
suggestedFix: Remove one (preferably Q103 as a D1 TALL is very similar in approach) and replace with a different hidden word.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Split type distribution inverted vs GL weighting
description: 95 questions are 1+3 splits (61%), 45 are 2+2 splits (29%), and 15 are 3+1 splits (10%). The Oracle states that 2+2 is the most common GL split type, yet it only accounts for 29% of questions. The 1+3 split dominates at 61%, which does not reflect actual GL exam weighting. Children will over-practise 1+3 and under-practise the most common split type.
suggestedFix: Rebalance to approximately 2+2: 50%, 1+3: 30%, 3+1: 20%. This means converting or replacing ~30 questions from 1+3 to 2+2 split types, and adding ~15 more 3+1 splits.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: 6 lesson sub-concepts have no questions mapped to them
description: The sub-concepts 'within-word', 'clue-first', 'three-letter-hidden', 'five-letter-hidden', 'backwards-hidden', and 'double-meaning' are all orphaned — they have lessons teaching these skills but zero quiz questions testing them. Children learn about backwards reading and within-word finding but never practise these in the actual quiz. Note: 'clue-first' is a strategy sub-concept (no questions needed), but the others should have questions if they're taught.
suggestedFix: For 'three-letter-hidden' and 'five-letter-hidden': these ARE covered by existing questions (Q126-139 for 3-letter, Q141-155 for 5-letter) — the mapping just categorises them by split type rather than word length. Consider adding a secondary tag. For 'backwards-hidden' and 'within-word': either create dedicated questions or mark these lessons as strategy-only (no direct question practice expected). For 'double-meaning': same treatment.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: D2 questions underrepresented vs GL target
description: Current distribution is D1:52(33.5%), D2:52(33.5%), D3:51(32.9%). GL targets approximately D1:30%, D2:40%, D3:30%. D2 is about 10 questions short of the ideal 62 questions (40% of 155). The even three-way split doesn't reflect GL's emphasis on medium-difficulty questions.
suggestedFix: When replacing duplicates or rebalancing splits, aim to add the new questions at D2 difficulty. Target: D1:~47, D2:~62, D3:~46.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: DENT appears in both Q59 and Q166
description: Q59 hides DENT across 'wand + entry' (D2). Q166 hides DENT across 'garden + tools' (D2). Same hidden word, same difficulty. Not as egregious as other duplicates because the word pairs and split types differ (1+3 vs 3+1).
suggestedFix: Lower priority than other duplicates since the split types differ, providing different practice. Consider replacing one if reducing total duplicates.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Within-word lesson contradicts question format
description: The 'Hidden Inside One Word' lesson teaches children to find words hidden entirely within a single word (e.g., LANE inside 'planet'). However, all 155 questions use the 'select-two' format requiring children to pick TWO words. A within-word answer would only need ONE word selected. This creates a disconnect between what is taught and what is tested.
suggestedFix: Either: (a) add a question variant that allows single-word selection for within-word hiding, or (b) reframe the lesson as a supporting strategy ('check inside single words too — but in the exam, the answer always spans two words') to avoid confusion.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Question is trivially circular — answer is stated in the question
description: Q8 asks 'If CAT is coded as DBU, what is the code for CAT?' The answer (DBU) is literally given in the question stem. This tests nothing — a child can copy the answer without understanding any shift rules. This would never appear in a real GL paper.
suggestedFix: Change to a genuine encode/decode question. Either ask 'If CAT is coded as DBU, what is the code for DOG?' or 'If CAT is coded as DBU, what word is coded as GPS?' Both test the +1 shift properly.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Option A contains accented character MBOÉ instead of MBOE
description: Q14 options include 'MBOÉ' as option A and 'MBOE' as option B. The correct answer is MBOE (option B, index 1). The accented É in option A is a rendering/encoding artefact that should not appear — it could confuse children or cause display issues. All options should use plain uppercase ASCII letters only.
suggestedFix: Replace 'MBOÉ' with a proper distractor like 'NCOE' or 'MBOF'. Ensure no accented characters exist in any letter code options.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: First 44 questions over-rely on +1/-1 shifts — excessive repetition
description: Q1-Q44 are almost entirely +1 or -1 shifts on 3-letter words. Many are near-duplicates in structure: Q9 (DOG→CNF, encode SUN), Q11 (SUN→RTM, encode PEN), Q23 (CAT→BZS, encode SUN), Q25 (SUN→RTM, encode CUP), Q28 (BUS→ATR, encode RUN). The same words (CAT, DOG, SUN, PEN, HAT) appear repeatedly. This creates a monotonous experience and doesn't build skills progressively. GL papers use only 6 letter code questions per section — having 44 near-identical questions wastes bank space.
suggestedFix: Retire 15-20 of the most similar Q1-Q44 questions. Keep the best representatives of each format: 3-4 'identify the rule' questions, 3-4 encode questions, 3-4 decode questions. Redistribute those slots to D2 questions (+2/-2 shifts, 4-letter words) to bring D2 closer to the GL 40% target.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Wrap-around direction described incorrectly in explanation
description: Q88 explanation states 'Z→C (wraps past A)' for decoding. When moving forward +3, Z wraps past Z (i.e., Z→A→B→C), not past A. The phrasing 'wraps past A' implies going backward past A, which would give a different result. The answer (TRICK) is correct, but the explanation language could mislead a child about the wrap-around direction.
suggestedFix: Change to 'Z→C (wraps forward past Z: Z→A→B→C)' to accurately describe the forward wrap-around.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Correct answer is RUN but option label says 'RUN' at index 2 while correctAnswer field says RUN at correct:2
description: Q29 asks 'If DOG is coded as EPH, what word is coded as SVO?' Decoding with -1: S→R, V→U, O→N = RUN. The correct index is 2, and options are [RUM, RUG, RUN, RUT, TUN]. Index 2 = RUN. However, the correctAnswer field says 'RUN' — this is correct. No actual error, but worth noting the distractor 'RUG' also decoded to index 1 when the explanation could clarify why RUG is wrong (V→U is correct not V→U, O→G would need O→G which doesn't work).
suggestedFix: No fix needed — answer verified as correct. Distractors are well-designed near-misses.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Correct index points to wrong option — answer is IODPH but correct:1 points to IODPH which matches
description: Q77 correct:1 points to options[1] = 'IODPH'. Let me verify: CHARM→FKDUP is +3. FLAME +3: F→I, L→O, A→D, M→P, E→H = IODPH. Options[0]='IODQH', options[1]='IODPH'. Correct answer IODPH is at index 1. Verified correct.
suggestedFix: No fix needed — verified correct.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Wrap-around explanation uses incorrect direction phrasing
description: Q96 decodes PLAHU with shift -3. Explanation says 'A→X (wraps past A)'. When going backward 3 from A: A→Z→Y→X. The correct phrasing is 'wraps backward past A to Z, then continues'. The answer MIXER is correct (P-3=M, L-3=I, A-3=X, H-3=E, U-3=R). The issue is only in the explanation wording.
suggestedFix: Change explanation to 'A→X (wraps backward: A→Z→Y→X)' for clarity.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Variable shift question tip references +1 shift which is misleading
description: Q99 uses shifts +1,+2,+3,+1 (a non-uniform pattern). The tip says '+1 is the most common GL code — practise until it's automatic!' This tip is misleading for a variable-shift question because it draws attention to +1 when the whole point is that shifts vary per position. A child might be confused by the +1 tip when the pattern includes +2 and +3 shifts.
suggestedFix: Change tip to: 'When the shifts change per position, write the pattern above the letters. Look for +1,+2,+3 or repeating sequences.'
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Questions state the rule in the stem — reduces cognitive demand below D1
description: Q101-Q108 all include the shift rule in parentheses: 'each letter moved forward by 1', 'each letter moved back by 1', 'each letter moved forward by 2'. In real GL papers, the child must discover the rule from the example pair. Stating the rule explicitly makes these trivially easy — even below genuine D1 difficulty. These could mislead children into thinking the rule will always be given.
suggestedFix: Remove the parenthetical rule statement from these 8 questions. The example pair (e.g., 'FISH is coded as GJTI') is sufficient for the child to work out +1. If these are meant as introductory scaffolding questions, label them as 'practice' rather than mixing them into the regular D1 bank.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Identical tips repeated across 100+ questions
description: The tip 'Always check every letter — don't assume the shift from just the first one' appears on approximately 95 of the first 100 questions. While the advice is sound, the extreme repetition reduces its impact. Tips on later questions (Q101+) vary more ('When the shift changes each position, write the pattern above the letters'). The earlier block could benefit from varied tips.
suggestedFix: Rotate between 4-5 different tips across the first 100 questions: (1) the check-every-letter tip, (2) 'Use EJOTY to find letter positions quickly', (3) 'Write both the original and shifted letters side by side', (4) 'Watch for wrap-around near Z and A', (5) 'If stuck, try the most common shifts first: +1, +2, -1, -2'.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Two orphan sub-concepts have lessons but no mapped questions
description: The mapping metadata shows 2 orphan sub-concepts — these have lessons created but no questions directly mapped to them. This means children learn a concept in a micro-lesson but never practise it in quiz questions, reducing learning transfer.
suggestedFix: Review the 2 orphan sub-concepts and either map existing questions to them or create new questions that exercise those specific skills.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q18 option A 'FOOD' is a strong distractor but explanation doesn't address it
description: Q18 decodes IROG with -3 shift. I→F, R→O, O→L, G→D = FOLD. Option A is 'FOOD' which a child might select if they miscounted one letter. The explanation says 'The word is FOLD' but doesn't explain why FOOD is wrong (the third letter O doesn't decode to O with -3, it decodes to L). Better explanations address why the strongest distractor fails.
suggestedFix: Add a note like: 'O→L (not O), so the third letter is L, making FOLD not FOOD.'
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q2 rated D3 but uses simple +1 constant shift — more appropriate for D2
description: Q2 asks 'If COLD is coded as DPME, what is WARM coded as?' This is a straightforward +1 constant shift on a 4-letter word. Per the Oracle spec, D2 is 'Constant +2/-2 (sometimes +3), 4-letter words, child determines rule from example'. A simple +1 on a 4-letter word falls between D1 (3-letter, +1) and D2 (4-letter, +2). This should be D2 at most, not D3. D3 requires larger shifts, variable shifts, wrap-around, or mirror codes.
suggestedFix: Recalibrate Q2 from D3 to D2. Review other early D3 questions (Q4, Q5, Q6) that also use simple +1/+3 constant shifts to ensure they genuinely warrant D3 classification.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q34 decoding verified — FOX is correct
description: Q34: JAM coded as KBN is +1 shift. GPY decoded with -1: G→F, P→O, Y→X = FOX. Options: [FIG, FOX, FLY, FUN, FAR]. correctAnswer 'FOX' is at index 1, correct field is 2. Wait — index 2 = 'FLY'. But correctAnswer says 'FOX' which is at index 1. The correct field says 2 (0-indexed). Index 0=FIG, 1=FOX? No, let me recount: options are ['FIG', 'FLY', 'FOX', 'FUN', 'FAR']. Index 0=FIG, 1=FLY, 2=FOX, 3=FUN, 4=FAR. correct:2 points to FOX. correctAnswer is 'FOX'. Verified correct — my initial concern was unfounded.
suggestedFix: No fix needed — answer verified as correct.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Massive question duplication — 30+ questions are exact or near-exact duplicates
description: The question bank contains extensive duplication. Exact duplicates include: Q1/Q7 (WHEAT+EAR), Q3/Q12 (BRAID+OWL), Q4/Q9 (PLATE+AN), Q5/Q10 (SPARK+OAK), Q6/Q11 (BLOOM+OAT), Q2/Q13 (SPINE+OAR). Near-duplicates with the same base word pair include: Q31/Q45/Q79 (OAR+SCOLD), Q32/Q46 (AGE+SCOLD), Q33/Q47 (OAK+SCOLD), Q34/Q52/Q81 (AGE+PRICE), Q35/Q53 (LAY+PRICE), Q36/Q54 (ACE+PRICE), Q37/Q59/Q82 (OAR+STALE), Q38/Q60 (AGE+STALE), Q39/Q61 (OAK+STALE), Q40/Q66/Q84 (OWL+FLAME), Q41/Q67/Q85 (ATE+FLAME), Q42/Q68 (ACE+FLAME), Q43/Q73/Q86 (OAR+STERN), Q44/Q74 (AGE+STERN). At least 40 questions should be replaced with unique word pairs.
recommendation: Replace duplicates with genuinely unique word pairs. Each question should use a distinct combination of source and receiving words. Good replacements would include: BRIDGE+AIR (B->RIDGE+BAIR? no... try CREEK+OAR->REEK+COAR? no), follow GL patterns with verified word pairs.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q105 CARD+CAR creates a nonsensical circular swap
description: Q105 asks to move a letter from CARD to CAR. The answer given is D: CARD becomes CAR, and CAR becomes CARD. This is logically absurd — it just swaps the words back and forth. The question doesn't test any meaningful skill. Similarly, Q116 (CART+CAT, move R: CART->CAT, CAT->CART) and Q117 (CAST+CAT, move S: CAST->CAT, CAT->CAST) and Q122 (HALT+HAT, move L: HALT->HAT, HAT->HALT) are all circular swaps.
recommendation: Replace these questions entirely. A letter move should create two genuinely NEW words, not swap the original pair back.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Extreme difficulty imbalance: 66% D2 vs GL target of 40%
description: Current distribution is D1:24 (19%), D2:84 (66%), D3:20 (16%). GL target is approximately D1:30%, D2:40%, D3:30%. D2 is over-represented by 26 percentage points. This means children get insufficient practice at both the easy confidence-building level (D1) and the challenging exam-discriminating level (D3). The D2 block from Q31-Q128 is particularly repetitive with minor variations on the same word combinations.
recommendation: Target approximately D1:38, D2:52, D3:38 for 128 questions. Add more D1 questions with short common words and obvious first-letter removals. Add more D3 questions with 6-7 letter words, non-obvious letter positions, less common vocabulary, and right-to-left direction.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q22 PLIGHT+OUR rated D3 but is a simple first-letter removal
description: PLIGHT -> LIGHT is a straightforward first-letter removal. OUR -> POUR is a common word. Both resulting words are high-frequency. This should be D1 or at most D2. The only slightly harder element is PLIGHT being 6 letters, but the P removal is the most obvious move. Per Oracle specs, D3 requires '6-7 letter words, non-obvious letter position, less common vocabulary'.
recommendation: Re-rate to D1 (or D2 at most). Reserve D3 for questions where the letter position is genuinely non-obvious or the resulting words are uncommon.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q15 mapped to 'Moving the First Letter' but answer is middle letter R
description: Q15 (SHORE+INK, answer R) is mapped to sub-concept 'remove-first-letter' with high confidence. However, R is not the first letter of SHORE — S is. The R is the 4th letter (middle). This should be mapped to 'remove-middle-letter'. The mapping algorithm appears to have blindly assigned all questions with a remove-from-word-1 pattern to 'first letter' regardless of actual position.
recommendation: Re-map to 'remove-middle-letter' sub-concept. Audit all other mappings to check position accuracy.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Over-reliance on tiny receiving words (OAR, OAK, AGE, ATE, EAR, OWL)
description: A huge proportion of questions use 2-3 letter receiving words like OAR (appears 15+ times), OAK (5+ times), AGE (8+ times), EAR (6+ times), ATE (4+ times), OWL (3+ times). Real GL papers use more varied and longer receiving words. This creates a pattern where children learn 'if the small word starts with a vowel, add a consonant to the front' rather than genuinely developing the remove-and-check skill.
recommendation: Diversify receiving words. Use 4-5 letter words as receivers too. Include examples where the moved letter goes to the middle or end of the receiving word, not just the start.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Repetitive base word reuse — SCOLD appears in 9 questions, FLAME in 8, STALE in 8
description: Rather than creating unique word pairs, the bank systematically pairs 4-5 base words (SCOLD, PRICE, FLAME, STALE, STERN, STORE) with many different small receiving words. This produces Q31-51 (SCOLD variants), Q34-58 (PRICE variants), Q59-72 (FLAME/STALE variants). A child seeing SCOLD for the 9th time learns nothing new about letter-move strategy.
recommendation: Each question should use a unique source word. There are thousands of valid English words for letter-move puzzles — no need to reuse.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Several D3 questions are actually D2 difficulty
description: Q37-Q39 and Q43-Q44 are rated D3 but involve straightforward first-letter removals from common 5-letter words (STALE->TALE, STERN->TERN). While TERN is slightly less common, the operation itself is simple. Per Oracle specs, D3 should involve 'non-obvious letter position' or 'right-to-left direction' or 'multiple letters appear to work'. Q37 (OAR+STALE) has the same answer and mechanism as D2 question Q59.
recommendation: Re-rate to D2 or replace with genuinely D3 questions that require middle-letter removal or less common vocabulary.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Tips are repetitive and not difficulty-appropriate
description: The same 6-7 tips rotate mechanically across all questions regardless of difficulty: 'Start with consonants at the start and end', 'Try both directions', 'Use the remove and check method', 'Say the new words out loud', 'Check BOTH resulting words', 'Work systematically'. D3 questions should have tips specific to their difficulty (e.g. 'For longer words, try removing letters from the middle' or 'When the first and last letters don't work, the answer is often a less obvious middle letter').
recommendation: Tailor tips to difficulty level. D1 tips should be basic strategy. D2 tips should address common mistakes. D3 tips should address the specific challenge of that question.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Question format inconsistency between Q1-Q30 and Q31-Q128
description: Q1-Q30 present as 'WORD1  WORD2' (left-to-right). Q31-Q128 present as 'SMALL_WORD BIGGER_WORD' (right-to-left or mixed). Q104-Q128 switch to yet another pattern with shorter words on both sides. This inconsistency creates three different question 'styles' within the same topic, which could confuse students about what the question is asking.
recommendation: Standardise the presentation format. If both directions are tested, randomise the word order rather than creating blocks of one direction.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Options are just the individual letters of one word — no GL-style distractors
description: Every question lists the 5 letters of one of the two words as the options. This means options never include plausible-but-wrong letters from the other word, or letters that would create one valid word but not two. GL-style distractors should include letters that create a valid word from one side but not both, testing whether children check BOTH resulting words.
recommendation: Include at least 1-2 distractor letters that partially work (create one valid word but not the other). This tests the critical skill of checking both results.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: 6 of 9 sub-concepts are orphaned (have lessons but no questions mapped)
description: The mapping file shows 6 orphanSubConcepts. This means lessons exist for skills that no questions actually test, or the mapping failed to connect them. Children learn techniques in lessons that they never get to practise in quizzes, reducing teaching efficacy.
recommendation: Either map existing questions to these sub-concepts or create new questions that specifically test each lesson's learning goal.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Answer position distribution may be uneven across the large D2 block
description: In the Q31-Q72 block, the correct answer appears to be systematically varied (0,1,2,3,4 cycling) but the letter being moved often stays the same (S for SCOLD, P for PRICE, F/L for FLAME). GL distributes answer positions evenly to prevent pattern-guessing. The mechanical cycling here is transparent.
recommendation: When replacing duplicate questions, ensure answer positions are genuinely randomised rather than mechanically cycled.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Some D1 questions use words a 9-year-old might not know (TARE, COWL, SEAR)
description: Q19 creates TARE (a weed/weight allowance) which is uncommon for Year 5. Q14 creates COWL (a hood/chimney cap). Q21 creates SEAR (to burn). While these are real words, D1 should use 'high-frequency words' per Oracle specs. A child who doesn't know TARE or COWL may think their answer is wrong even when correct.
recommendation: Replace with word pairs producing more common D1 results. E.g. SPEAK+OAR -> PEAK+SOAR (from Oracle example).
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Explanations do not define unfamiliar resulting words at D2/D3
description: When the letter move creates less common words (TERN, TRIPE, TARE, COWL, SEAR, BOAR), the explanation should briefly define them so children learn vocabulary alongside the technique. Some early questions do this (Q29 explains 'tripe = stomach lining used as food') but most D2/D3 questions do not.
recommendation: Add brief definitions for any resulting word that a Year 5 child might not know. This is both educationally valuable and builds confidence.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Lessons are good but could explicitly teach the right-to-left strategy
description: The lessons cover first/last/middle letter removal well, but the right-to-left direction (moving a letter from word 2 to word 1) is only mentioned in passing ('Try both directions'). Given that ~40% of questions use right-to-left movement, a dedicated lesson on this direction would be valuable.
recommendation: Consider adding a specific lesson sub-concept for right-to-left moves with worked examples showing why you should try both directions systematically.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q1 and Q9 are identical questions — both ask A, C, E, G, ___
description: Q1 (D2) asks 'What comes next in this letter series? A, C, E, G, ___' with answer I. Q9 (D1) asks the exact same question with the exact same answer. The options differ slightly (Q1: H,I,J,K,L; Q9: H,I,J,K,L — actually identical options too). Having the same question twice wastes a slot and could appear in the same quiz session. Q1 is rated D2 and Q9 is rated D1, which itself is inconsistent — the same question can't be two different difficulties.
suggestedFix: Remove Q9 entirely (keep Q1 which has the better difficulty rating of D2 — a simple skip-one pattern is arguably low-D2). Alternatively, change Q9 to a different D1 pattern like 'A, B, C, D, ___' or 'B, D, F, H, ___' (though the latter already exists as Q3 and Q10).
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q3 and Q10 are identical questions — both ask B, D, F, H, ___
description: Q3 (D1) asks 'What comes next in this letter series? B, D, F, H, ___' with answer J. Q10 (D1) asks the exact same question with the same answer. Both are rated D1. Options are slightly different (Q3: I,J,K,L,M; Q10: I,K,L,J,M) but the question and answer are identical. This is a direct duplicate.
suggestedFix: Remove Q10. Replace with a different D1 single-letter series such as 'C, E, G, I, ___' (K) or 'D, F, H, J, ___' (L).
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q7 is a near-duplicate of Q1/Q9 — same pattern with one extra term
description: Q7 asks 'A, C, E, G, I, ___' (K). Q1/Q9 ask 'A, C, E, G, ___' (I). These test the identical skip-one-forward pattern. Q7 merely adds one more term. Having three questions on A,C,E,G... is excessive. The answer K is correct (verified: +2 pattern).
suggestedFix: Keep Q7 (the longest version) and remove Q1 and Q9, or keep Q1 and remove Q7 and Q9. One representative question per pattern is sufficient.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: ZZ, YY, XX, WW, ___ appears 4 times across the bank
description: Q8 (D2), Q25 (D1), Q107 (D1), and Q130 (D2) all test the 'doubled letter going back 1' pattern with ZZ, YY, XX, WW, ___. Q8 and Q25 use exactly the same series (4 terms); Q107 uses 5 terms (ZZ through VV); Q130 uses 4 terms. All have answer VV (or UU for Q107). Having 4 questions testing the same pattern is excessive even across different quiz sessions.
suggestedFix: Keep Q25 (D1) and Q107 (D1, longer version). Remove Q8 and Q130. Replace with different patterns — e.g., 'AA, CC, EE, GG, ___' (II) for a skip-one doubled pattern, or 'ZY, XW, VU, TS, ___' (RQ) which is already covered but less repetitive.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Repeating constant series AB,AB,AB,AB,___ is trivially easy — below D1 level
description: Q104 asks 'What comes next? AB, AB, AB, AB, ___' with answer AB. This requires no pattern recognition whatsoever — any child can see it repeats. This would never appear in a GL paper. It's below the cognitive threshold of any real test question and wastes a D1 slot that could test a genuine pattern.
suggestedFix: Replace with a genuine D1 letter pair pattern such as 'CE, DF, EG, FH, ___' (GI) — both letters +1, with a constant gap of 2.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: A1,B2,C3,D4,___ appears as both Q26 and Q106
description: Q26 (D1) asks 'What comes next? A1, B2, C3, D4, ___' with answer E5. Q106 (D1) asks 'What comes next? A1, B2, C3, D4, E5, ___' with answer F6. These are the same letter+number pattern with different series lengths. Both are D1. While Q106 has one more term, the pattern is identical.
suggestedFix: Keep Q106 (longer, slightly harder) and remove Q26, or vice versa. One representative per pattern is sufficient.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q1 rated D2 but pattern is D1 — simple skip-one forward
description: Q1 asks 'A, C, E, G, ___' with answer I. This is a +2 constant pattern on single letters — the Oracle spec puts this squarely at D1 ('Same simple rule, small steps +1/+2'). Rating it D2 inflates the difficulty. Q9 (identical question) is rated D1, confirming the inconsistency.
suggestedFix: Recalibrate Q1 from D2 to D1. It's a simple skip-one pattern that any child who knows the alphabet can spot.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q6 rated D3 but is a simple both-forward +1/+1 pair pattern — should be D1
description: Q6 asks 'AC, BD, CE, DF, ___' with answer EG. Both letters advance by +1 each step. Per the Oracle spec, D1 is 'Same simple rule for both letters, small steps +1/+2, same direction, no wrap-around'. This is exactly that. D3 requires asymmetric steps, wrap-around, convergent patterns, or accelerating gaps. Q6 is the definition of D1.
suggestedFix: Recalibrate Q6 from D3 to D1.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: No wrap-around series questions despite Oracle spec listing it as D3 type
description: The Oracle spec lists 'Wrap-around — sequences crossing Z→A boundary' as a D3 pattern type for letter pair series. No questions in the 132-question bank test this pattern. A child could encounter a wrap-around series in a GL paper (e.g., XZ, ZB, BD, DF, ___) and be unprepared.
suggestedFix: Add 3-5 D3 wrap-around questions. Examples: 'XZ, ZB, BD, DF, ___' (FH — both +2 with wrap), or 'WZ, YB, AD, CF, ___' (EH — both +2 forward with wrap at Z/A boundary).
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q5 rated D1 but uses increasing gaps — more appropriate for D2 or D3
description: Q5 asks 'A, B, D, G, ___' with answer K. The gaps increase: +1, +2, +3, +4. Per the Oracle spec, D3 includes 'accelerating gaps'. This is genuinely harder than simple constant-step patterns. Rating it D1 undersells the cognitive demand. A child must notice the gaps are increasing and predict the next gap (+4), which requires second-order pattern recognition.
suggestedFix: Recalibrate Q5 from D1 to D2 (or even D3 if the child must discover the pattern without hints). Increasing-gap patterns are explicitly listed as D3 in the Oracle spec.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: BODMAS contradiction between lessons and questions
description: The lesson 'Left to Right — NOT BODMAS!' explicitly teaches that BODMAS does not apply to letter sums. But Q12 (If A=4, B=7, C=2, what is A × B − C?) has the explanation 'Remember BODMAS — multiplication before subtraction' and the answer 26 (4×7=28, 28-2=26). Q14 (If A=3, B=6, C=9, what is (B × C) ÷ A?) also uses BODMAS. If a child follows the lesson's advice (left to right, no BODMAS), they would calculate Q12 as: A×B=28, then 28-C=26 — which happens to give the same answer because multiplication comes first anyway. But for Q14 with brackets, left-to-right would give B×C=54, ÷A=18 — same answer because brackets enforce order. The fundamental issue is that the lesson says 'BODMAS doesn't apply' but then questions require BODMAS and tips say 'Remember BODMAS'. A child will be confused about which rule to follow.
suggestedFix: Decide on one consistent rule: either (a) BODMAS always applies (mathematically correct), and update the lesson to remove the 'NOT BODMAS' claim, or (b) all letter sum questions are left-to-right only (no mixed × and + in the same expression), and remove Q12/Q14-style mixed-operation questions. Option (a) is recommended since GL does test BODMAS in this context.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: D1 under-represented at 22% (target 30%)
description: Only 28 of 128 questions (22%) are D1. The GL target is ~30% (38 questions). This means children encounter harder questions sooner than intended, which can be discouraging for a topic that already requires strong alphabet-position knowledge. The 10 missing D1 questions would provide more practice at the foundational 'calculate word value' skill before progressing to comparisons and mixed operations.
suggestedFix: Add 10 D1 questions: 5 simple 3-letter word values (using early-alphabet letters A-M for smaller numbers), 3 'what number does letter X represent' questions, and 2 simple A+B+C addition problems. This brings D1 to 38/138 (28%), close to target.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q5 rated D2 but is a simple D1 word value question
description: Q5: 'If A=1, B=2, C=3... what is the total value of the word ICE?' Answer: I=9, C=3, E=5 = 17. This is a straightforward 3-letter word value addition using early-alphabet letters — identical in structure to Q1 (CAT=24), Q2 (BED=11), Q3 (AGE=13), all of which are D1. There is no reason for ICE to be D2. The letters I, C, E are all in the first half of the alphabet.
suggestedFix: Downgrade to D1. This would also help address the D1 under-representation issue.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q9 rated D2 but PIG=32 is simple enough for D1
description: Q9: 'What is the total value of PIG?' P=16, I=9, G=7 = 32. While P is in the second half of the alphabet, the calculation is straightforward addition of three numbers. This is comparable to Q15 (SUN: S=19, U=21, N=14 = 54) which is rated D1. If SUN is D1 with values up to 21, then PIG with values up to 16 should also be D1.
suggestedFix: Downgrade to D1 for consistency with Q15 (SUN) and other D1 word-value questions.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q13 rated D3 but FACE=15 is simple 4-letter addition
description: Q13: 'What is the total value of FACE?' F=6, A=1, C=3, E=5 = 15. This is rated D3 but uses only early-alphabet letters (all ≤6) and requires straightforward addition. 4-letter word values are D2 in the Oracle spec. The sum (15) is actually smaller than many D1 questions. This should be D2 at most.
suggestedFix: Downgrade to D2. True D3 word value questions should use 5+ letter words with late-alphabet letters (e.g., YOUTH = 25+15+21+20+8 = 89).
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Tips reference BODMAS on pure addition questions
description: Multiple pure word-value addition questions include the tip 'Remember BODMAS! Multiplication before addition when there are mixed operations.' However, these questions contain no multiplication — they are pure addition (e.g., Q8 DOG: D+O+G, Q17 CUP: C+U+P). This tip is irrelevant and potentially confusing, especially given the BODMAS lesson contradiction already identified.
suggestedFix: Remove BODMAS tips from pure addition questions. Only show BODMAS-related tips on questions that actually involve mixed operations.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Tips reference double letters on questions without double letters
description: The tip 'Watch out for double letters — count them twice (BELL = B+E+L+L)!' appears on comparison questions where none of the option words contain double letters. For example, Q4 (FIG, HEN, APE, COW, DOG — none have doubles), Q20 (FIG — no doubles). While the general advice is sound, it's not relevant to the specific question.
suggestedFix: Only show the double-letter tip on questions where at least one option word contains a repeated letter (e.g., QUEEN with two E's in Q40).
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Pure addition questions mapped to 'Mixed Operations' sub-concept
description: Q5 (ICE: I+C+E=17) and Q8 (DOG: D+O+G=26) are pure addition questions but mapped to the 'Mixed Addition and Subtraction' sub-concept. These questions contain no subtraction or mixed operations. They should be mapped to 'Adding Letter Values' (simple-addition) instead.
suggestedFix: Remap Q5 and Q8 to the 'simple-addition' sub-concept (Adding Letter Values).
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Missing Oracle sub-type: same-value word pairs
description: The Oracle spec lists 'Same-value pairs — Which two words have the same value? (calculate all)' as a sub-type. This is not represented in the question bank. This sub-type tests efficient estimation and comparison — a different skill from simple calculation.
suggestedFix: Add 5-8 questions at D2/D3 asking 'Which two of these words have the same total value?' with 5 word options. Children must calculate all five and find the matching pair.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Missing Oracle sub-type: letter equations (find missing letter)
description: The Oracle spec lists 'Letter equations — B + ? = J (find missing letter)' and 'Complete equation — What letter makes C + ? = H?' These are not represented. These reverse-engineering questions test a different cognitive skill (working backwards from a target) compared to forward calculation.
suggestedFix: Add 5-8 questions at D2/D3 in the format 'If A=1, B=2... what letter completes the equation C + ? = H?' (answer: E, because 3 + 5 = 8).
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Missing Oracle sub-type: word difference
description: The Oracle spec lists 'Word difference — Difference between CAT and DOG?' as a sub-type. This requires calculating two word values and finding the difference — a two-step problem that is not currently tested.
suggestedFix: Add 3-5 questions at D2/D3 asking for the difference between two words' total values.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Rhyming synonym puzzles incorrectly mapped to 'Words with Multiple Meanings'
description: Q5 ('overweight feline' = Fat cat), Q11 ('large swine' = Big pig), Q17 ('angry employer' = Cross boss) are rhyming synonym puzzles — a distinct GL question type where the child must find two rhyming words that match a definition. These are mapped to the 'homonyms' sub-concept ('Words with Multiple Meanings'), which teaches about words like 'bank' having two meanings. The lesson content (about recognising multiple definitions from context) has nothing to do with solving rhyming synonym puzzles. A child who studies the homonyms lesson will not learn how to solve these questions.
suggestedFix: Create a new sub-concept 'Rhyming Synonym Puzzles' with a lesson teaching the strategy: (1) identify the definition words, (2) find a synonym for each, (3) check the synonyms rhyme. Map Q5, Q11, Q17, and any other rhyming pair questions to this new sub-concept.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q6 (3-person ordering) mapped to 'Ordering 4 People'
description: Q6: 'Tom is older than Sam. Sam is older than Jack. Who is the youngest?' involves only 3 people but is mapped to the 'four-person-ordering' sub-concept. The lesson for 4-person ordering teaches about finding the 'link name' that connects two clues — unnecessary for 3-person problems where the connection is always obvious. This should be mapped to a 3-person ordering sub-concept or the 'superlative-questions' sub-concept.
suggestedFix: Remap Q6 to 'superlative-questions' (Who Is Tallest / Shortest / Oldest?) which is the correct sub-concept for 3-person extreme-position questions.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: 10 questions have no lesson mapping
description: The mapping meta shows 10 unmapped questions out of 163. These questions are likely calendar/sequence questions (Q16 'What month comes two months after March?', Q20 'What day comes three days after Monday?'), arithmetic-logic hybrids (Q12 'Rose has twice as many as Lily'), or unusual question formats that don't fit existing sub-concepts. Unmapped questions mean the child gets no micro-lesson preparation before encountering them.
suggestedFix: Identify the 10 unmapped questions and either: (a) create new sub-concepts for genuine gaps (calendar reasoning, arithmetic-logic), or (b) map them to the closest existing sub-concept with a note about the imperfect fit.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: D3 over-represented at 37% (target 30%)
description: 61 of 163 questions (37%) are D3, which is 7% above the GL target of ~30%. Logic & Language is already conceptually challenging — over-weighting D3 means children spend disproportionate time on the hardest questions. The extra 12 D3 questions (61 vs target ~49) should be redistributed: some downgraded to D2, others genuinely belonging at D3.
suggestedFix: Review D3 questions and downgrade the following candidates to D2: (a) Q4 (4-person ordering with extreme position — standard D2), (b) Q5 (rhyming synonym 'fat cat' — common vocabulary, not D3 complexity), (c) Q14 (sentence rearrangement with 6 words — D2 per Oracle spec), (d) Q17 (rhyming synonym 'cross boss' — could be D2). Target reducing D3 to ~50 questions.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q4 rated D3 but is straightforward 4-person ordering
description: Q4: 'Dan has more sweets than Emma. Fay has fewer sweets than Emma but more than George. Who has the most sweets?' This creates the chain Dan > Emma > Fay > George and asks for the extreme position (most). Per the Oracle spec, 4-person ordering is D2, and asking for the extreme position (rather than middle) makes it easier. A true D3 ordering question would have 5 people or ask for a non-extreme position.
suggestedFix: Downgrade to D2.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q5 'fat cat' rhyming puzzle rated D3 but uses very common vocabulary
description: Q5: 'overweight feline = fat cat' is rated D3. Both 'fat' and 'cat' are Year 2-3 vocabulary. 'Feline' is the only challenging word, and it's very commonly known. The Oracle spec places common homonyms at D1 and less common ones at D2. While rhyming synonyms aren't exactly homonyms, the difficulty principle applies — common vocabulary should be D1/D2.
suggestedFix: Downgrade to D2. Reserve D3 for rhyming synonym puzzles using sophisticated vocabulary (e.g., 'a petite dwelling' = small hall, 'an angry employer' = cross boss).
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Some questions test arithmetic/calendar knowledge rather than logic
description: Q12 ('Rose has twice as many as Lily' — tests multiplication), Q16 ('What month comes two months after March?' — tests calendar knowledge), Q20 ('What day comes three days after Monday?' — tests day-of-week knowledge). These test factual/arithmetic knowledge rather than the logical reasoning skills that define the Logic & Language category. GL's Logic & Language section specifically tests deduction, ordering, syllogisms, and language-based reasoning.
suggestedFix: Either: (a) move these to a more appropriate topic (calendar questions could be in a general knowledge section), or (b) reframe to include genuine logical reasoning (e.g., 'If Monday is a school day and Freya has swimming every other school day starting Monday, does she swim on Wednesday?' — combines calendar with logic).
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Tips from wrong question types appear on unrelated questions
description: Ordering questions get syllogism tips and vice versa. For example, Q7 (syllogism: 'All dogs have four legs') gets the tip 'Watch out for direction words — fewer is opposite to more!' which is an ordering tip. Q11 (rhyming synonym puzzle) gets 'Focus only on what MUST be true' which is a syllogism tip. Q8 (sentence rearrangement) gets 'For 4-5 person rankings, write ALL names before picking your answer' — completely irrelevant to sentence unscrambling.
suggestedFix: Assign tips from pools specific to the question sub-type: ordering tips for ordering questions, syllogism tips for syllogism questions, sentence tips for sentence rearrangement questions, etc.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q22 'box' connecting 'bank' and 'match' has a weak explanation
description: Q22: 'Which word connects both bank and match?' Answer: Box. The explanation says 'Box connects with match (a matchbox) and bank (a cash box kept at a bank, or a safety deposit box).' The bank-box connection is tenuous — 'cash box' and 'safety deposit box' are not natural collocations of 'bank + box'. A stronger connection would be 'money box' (piggy bank). The GL format for double-meaning questions typically requires clear, unambiguous connections.
suggestedFix: Either strengthen the explanation to use 'money box' as the bank connection, or replace the question with a clearer double-meaning word. For example: 'Which word connects park and pupil?' → Bright (bright park, bright pupil).
subject: verbalreasoning

title: No logic grid / Explore the Facts questions
description: The Oracle spec lists 'Logic grid / Explore the Facts — multiple people + attributes, cross-reference to deduce' as a D3 sub-type. This is completely absent from the question bank. GL regularly includes 1-2 logic grid questions per paper where children must match 3-4 people to 3-4 attributes using elimination. These are high-value D3 questions.
suggestedFix: Add 5-8 logic grid questions at D3. Example: 'Ali, Ben, and Cara each like a different colour (red, blue, green) and a different sport (football, tennis, swimming). Ali likes red. The swimmer likes blue. Ben plays tennis. Which sport does Cara do?'
subject: verbalreasoning

title: 'Cannot tell' is rarely the correct answer
description: The Oracle spec emphasises that 'Cannot tell' / 'Cannot be determined' should be a valid correct answer for some questions, testing whether children understand the difference between 'could be true' and 'must be true'. While 'Cannot tell' appears as an option in many questions, it is almost never the correct answer in this bank. GL specifically tests this as a trap — children who feel they must commit to an answer get caught.
suggestedFix: Add 5-8 questions where 'Cannot tell' IS the correct answer. Example: 'Some children like swimming. Kai is a child. Does Kai like swimming? Cannot tell — some does not mean all.' These should be D2/D3.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: D1 over-relies on 3-person ordering with identical structure
description: The D1 block contains many 3-person ordering questions with near-identical structure: 'X is [comparison] than Y. Y is [comparison] than Z. Who is the [superlative]?' While the names, attributes, and comparison words vary, the structural template is identical across ~15 questions. A child quickly learns the pattern ('always pick the person mentioned last in the final clue') rather than developing genuine ordering skills.
suggestedFix: Vary D1 question structures more: include some where clues are in reverse order (bottom-up), some where the comparison direction changes ('Z is shorter than Y'), and some where two separate facts need combining rather than following a chain. Also intersperse D1 syllogisms and sentence rearrangement questions.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Many 'three-letter word' options are NOT real English words, violating GL format
description: The Oracle specification states: 'Three consecutive letters removed from a word. Those three letters must form a valid word on their own.' However, across Q16-Q100 (the prefix/suffix format), many correct answers are NOT real English three-letter words: DAR (Q74), MON (Q75), BUL (Q79), HAP (Q80), CUL (Q81), FAC (Q82), FLA (Q83), VIS (Q84), MES (Q85), PRA (Q86), OPE (Q88), CUR (Q91), FRE (Q92), POS (Q93), EFF (Q94), CEL (Q95), BEA (Q96), FRA (Q97), CHA (Q98), JUD (Q99), WAN (Q100), ANC (Q41), SUR (Q42), PER (Q43), SPE (Q46), ADV (Q47), LAN (Q48), DEP (Q45), SIL (Q27), RAB (Q26), PLA (Q23), STO (Q36), PUB (Q37), SEC (Q38), WEA (Q39), VOL (Q40). While some are arguable (PER, PUB, SUM, DIN, TIM are real words), many like DAR, BUL, CUL, FAC, FLA, VIS, MES, FRE, POS, EFF, CEL, BEA, FRA, CHA, JUD, ADV, SPE, DEP, STO, SEC, WEA, ANC, SUR are NOT standard English words. This fundamentally changes the question type from 'find the stolen word' to 'find the missing letters', which is a different (and easier) cognitive task.
recommendation: This is a design choice rather than a simple fix. Option A: Restrict to questions where the three letters genuinely form a word (CAR, PET, MAN, KIT, AGE, TEN, APT, APE, PAT, CAP, ILL, BAT, COT, etc.) — this matches the GL 'stolen word' format exactly. Option B: Accept these as a 'missing letters' variant (which is valid) but relabel the questions appropriately and don't claim the letters form a word. The current situation is the worst of both worlds: questions ASK for a 'three-letter word' but many answers are not words.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Mechanical filler distractors (TIP, MOB, HUB, JAR) appear across 60+ questions
description: Questions Q16-Q100 systematically use the same filler options: TIP, MOB, HUB, JAR appear in almost every question as wrong answers. These are transparently not related to the target word and offer no learning value. A child quickly learns to ignore any option containing TIP, MOB, HUB, or JAR, making the questions trivially easy regardless of the actual word. GL-style distractors should be plausible alternatives that test whether the child can distinguish similar letter combinations.
recommendation: Replace filler distractors with plausible alternatives. For Q16 (FORE___=FOREMAN), good distractors would be MAT (FOREMAT?), MAP (FOREMAP?), MEN (FOREMEN — real but different word), MAX (FOREMAX?). Each distractor should at least look like it could complete a word.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q101-Q125 block has answer always in position 0 (first option)
description: Every question from Q101 to Q125 has the correct answer as the first option (correct: 0). This is a massive pattern that makes these questions trivially exploitable. A child who notices this pattern can get 25 questions right by always picking A. GL papers distribute answer positions evenly.
recommendation: Randomise the answer position across all 5 positions for Q101-Q125. Each position should appear roughly 5 times across 25 questions.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: No sentence-context questions despite Oracle listing this as a sub-type
description: The Oracle specification lists sub-type 4 as: 'In sentence context: The children GATED → HER (gatHERed)'. This format appears on GL papers but is completely absent from the question bank. Sentence-context questions are harder because children must first identify the complete word from context, then find the three-letter word within it.
recommendation: Add 10-15 sentence-context questions across D2 and D3. Example: 'The ship sailed across the ___(?)___ER.' Answer: HAR (HARBOUR).
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Tips are repetitive and sometimes inappropriate for the question type
description: The same 7 tips cycle mechanically regardless of question type or difficulty. Several tips are mismatched: Q11 (B_AUTY, single letter) gets the tip 'Try common three-letter words first' — but it's asking for a single letter. Q16 (FORE_MAN) gets 'Double letters are common traps — NECESSARY has one C and two S's!' — irrelevant to prefix questions. Q50 (H_LP, single letter, D1) gets 'Check BOTH things — the complete word is real AND the stolen letters form a real word' — also irrelevant for single-letter questions.
recommendation: Match tips to question sub-type. Single-letter questions should get vowel/consonant tips. Three-letter-word questions should get 'sound it out' and 'check both parts' tips. D3 questions should get difficulty-specific tips.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Some D1 single-letter questions are too easy even for D1
description: Q49-Q55 ask for obvious vowels in extremely common 4-letter words: G_FT (GIFT), H_LP (HELP), G_LD (GOLD), J_MP (JUMP), N_ST (NEST), M_LK (MILK), S_FT (SOFT). These are Years 1-2 words that any child preparing for the 11+ would know instantly. While D1 should be accessible, these are so trivial they offer zero exam preparation value and waste quiz time.
recommendation: Keep 2-3 of these as warm-up but replace the rest with D1 questions that at least require a moment's thought — e.g. words with two plausible vowels (G_LD could be GOLD or GILD).
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q23 answer PLA is not a real English word
description: Q23 asks '_ _ _ NET' with answer PLA making PLANET. PLA is not a recognised English word. The question stem says 'Which three-letter word completes this longer word?' but PLA is not a word. This violates the question's own instructions. Other problematic examples: Q26 RAB (RABBIT), Q27 SIL (SILVER).
recommendation: Either change the question wording to 'Which three letters complete this longer word?' (removing the 'word' requirement) or replace with questions where the answer IS a real word.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q6 PASSAGE answer SAG — verify SAG is the only valid option
description: Q6 asks for PAS___E. The answer is SAG making PASSAGE. However, the question format shows PAS _ _ _ E which could also potentially be PASSIVE (SIV) or PASTIME (TIM). While SAG is correct for PASSAGE, the distractors (SAT, SAD, SAP, SAW) are all quite close and none make real words with the remaining letters, so the question works. No fix needed, but worth noting the importance of thorough option validation.
recommendation: No action needed — this is well-constructed. Noted as a verification checkpoint only.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: No lesson specifically teaching the 'word within a word' recognition skill
description: The GL format fundamentally tests whether children can see a real word hidden inside a longer word. The lessons teach vowel/consonant strategies and 'sounding out', but don't explicitly teach children to look for words-within-words as a pattern recognition skill. A lesson on 'word hunting' — finding smaller words inside bigger ones — would directly prepare for this question type.
recommendation: Add a lesson sub-concept specifically on 'word hunting' — practising finding hidden words in longer words without the gap format. This builds the core pattern recognition skill.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Three different question presentation formats without clear progression
description: Questions use three formats: (1) 'CH _ _ _ ER' with underscores (Q1-Q10), (2) '( _ _ _ ) LING' with brackets (Q74-Q100), (3) 'F ( _ _ _ ) YARD' with letter + brackets (Q101-Q125). While variety is fine, the formats aren't clearly explained to children and the switch between them could cause confusion. GL papers use a consistent format throughout.
recommendation: Standardise on one format (underscores are closest to GL) or add a brief intro screen when the format changes.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Some D3 explanations could be more educational
description: D3 questions use challenging words (PARAPET, SPECTACLE, ANCESTOR, SURRENDER) which are great for vocabulary building. However, explanations could do more to reinforce the vocabulary. Q9 (PARAPET) does explain 'a low wall along the edge of a roof or bridge' which is excellent. But Q41 (ANCESTOR) just says 'a person from whom one is descended' without giving a memorable example, and Q46 (SPECTACLE) gives a generic definition.
recommendation: Add age-appropriate example sentences or memorable context to D3 explanations. E.g. 'ANCESTOR — someone from your family who lived a long time ago, like your great-great-grandparents'.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: No questions test the 'end position' stolen word format
description: The Oracle lists 'Stolen word at end: FORE _ _ _ → MAN (FOREMAN)' as a sub-type. Q16 (FORE___=FOREMAN) does test this. However, most 'end position' questions in Q74-Q100 use the '( _ _ _ ) SUFFIX' format which is really a prefix search (find letters that go BEFORE the suffix). True end-position questions like 'BASKET___' where the answer goes at the end are underrepresented.
recommendation: Add questions in the format 'WORD _ _ _' where the three-letter word appears at the end (e.g. 'CHAR _ _ _' → IOT making CHARIOT, where IOT is not a word, so maybe 'KIT _ _ _' → TEN making KITTEN).
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q60 answer is -12, introducing negative numbers unexpectedly
description: Q60: '543, 432, 321, 210, 99, ___' with answer -12 (pattern is -111). This is rated D2 but the answer involves a negative number. While the VR Number Series Oracle spec does not explicitly exclude negatives, the GL Assessment typically does not require negative number answers in the VR paper. Negative numbers are a separate maths topic. A child who correctly identifies the -111 pattern might still get confused by the negative answer, which is a maths skill issue rather than a pattern recognition issue. Additionally, all five options are negative, which removes the 'is the answer negative?' decision from the problem — a real GL question would mix positive and negative options to test understanding.
suggestedFix: Either: (a) Replace with a series where -111 yields a small positive answer (e.g., 654, 543, 432, 321, 210, ___  → answer 99, which is already Q60's penultimate term — so use different starting numbers), or (b) move to D3 and include a mix of positive and negative options to genuinely test whether the child understands the answer should be negative.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Interleaved sequence questions Q26-Q30 rated D1 but are D2-level
description: Questions 26-30 use interleaved (alternating) sequences: 2,5,3,6,4,__ and 10,20,15,25,20,__ etc. These are rated D1 but require the child to recognise that two separate patterns are interleaved — a cognitively more demanding skill than simple constant difference. The Oracle spec places interleaved sequences at D2 minimum ('D2: Different rules per letter OR opposite directions'). While the sub-patterns within each interleaved pair are simple (+1, +5, +10), the act of separating them is what makes these harder than straight D1.
suggestedFix: Upgrade Q26-Q30 to D2. Replace them with 5 additional straight constant-difference D1 questions (perhaps using negative constant differences or less common step sizes like +3 or +9 from non-zero starting points).
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q71 rated D3 but is a straightforward interleaved +2/+2 pattern
description: Q71: '27, 37, 29, 39, 31, ___' is rated D3. Odd positions: 27, 29, 31 (+2). Even positions: 37, 39, 41 (+2). Both sub-sequences use the same simple +2 step. This is equivalent to Q26-Q30 (interleaved with simple steps), which are rated D1. A true D3 interleaved sequence should have mixed operations (one multiplies, one adds) or larger/varying step sizes.
suggestedFix: Downgrade to D2, or replace with a genuine D3 interleaved pattern — e.g., odd positions doubling (3, 6, 12, 24) while even positions subtract 5 (40, 35, 30, 25).
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q58 constant difference of +69 is unusually large for D2
description: Q58: '58, 127, 196, 265, 334, ___' with a constant difference of +69. While this is a valid constant-difference pattern, the arithmetic of adding 69 to 334 (=403) is significantly harder than typical D2 arithmetic. The pattern itself is D1-level (constant difference), but the computational burden pushes it beyond what a 9-year-old would comfortably do in exam conditions. GL tends to use smaller gaps for constant-difference D2 questions, reserving large numbers for patterns where the complexity is in recognising the pattern, not the arithmetic.
suggestedFix: Either downgrade the step size to something more manageable (e.g., +23 or +37) or move to D3 if large arithmetic is the intentional challenge.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: No questions test series starting with or crossing through negative numbers
description: The question bank has no series that start negative or cross from positive to negative (except Q60's answer). GL occasionally tests series like -8, -5, -2, 1, 4, ___ or 10, 5, 0, -5, ___. This is a genuine GL pattern type that tests whether children understand negative number ordering, which overlaps with the Negative Numbers maths topic.
suggestedFix: Add 3-5 questions at D2/D3 using series that include negative numbers. Example: -10, -6, -2, 2, 6, ___ (answer: 10, pattern +4).
subject: verbalreasoning

title: No questions test cube numbers or power sequences
description: The bank includes square numbers (Q41-Q45) but no cube numbers (1, 8, 27, 64, 125) or other power sequences. While cube numbers are less common in GL, they do appear occasionally at D3. Powers of 2 are well-covered through doubling/halving but not presented as 'powers' explicitly.
suggestedFix: Add 2-3 D3 questions testing cube number sequences or mixed power patterns.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Tips are recycled regardless of question context
description: Similar to Number Word Codes, tips are drawn from a small pool (~12) and assigned without regard to the question type. For example, Q5 (constant +7 pattern) gets 'Check the gap between the first two numbers, then verify it works for all pairs' which is appropriate, but Q46 (Fibonacci: 1,1,2,3,5,___) gets a tip about adding the last two numbers which is perfect. However, some questions get tips about recognising times tables when the pattern is clearly not a times table. The mismatch is less severe here than in Number Word Codes because the tips are more generally applicable.
suggestedFix: Group tips by pattern type and assign from the relevant group. Constant-difference tips for constant-difference questions, Fibonacci tips for Fibonacci questions, etc.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q8 correct index points to wrong option
description: Q8: 'If BIRD = 1234, what is the code for RIB?' Options: ['312', '321', '231', '123', '213']. correct=1 (index 1 = '321') and correctAnswer='321'. However, from BIRD=1234: B=1, I=2, R=3, D=4. RIB = R(3) I(2) B(1) = 321. The correct answer IS 321 which is at index 1. Wait — on re-check, correct=1 means options[1]='321', and correctAnswer='321'. But the explanation says 'From BIRD=1234: B=1, I=2, R=3, D=4. RIB = R(3) I(2) B(1) = 321' which is correct. However, the 'correct' field is 1, but options[0]='312' and options[1]='321'. Actually this IS correct: index 1 = '321'. But the explanation mentions 'the first digit must be 3' and says to 'cross out options that don't start with 3', while options[0]='312' also starts with 3 — the elimination tip is misleading in this context since two options start with 3.
suggestedFix: The answer itself is verified correct (321, index 1). However, the tip 'Use elimination — if you know the first digit must be 3, cross out options that don't start with 3' is misleading because option '312' also starts with 3. Replace this tip with one that's relevant to the question at hand.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Tip about double letters appears on questions with no double letters
description: The tip 'Start by finding double letters — if a word has OO and a code has the same digit twice, that's your entry point!' appears on at least 10 questions where neither the source word nor the target word contains any repeated letters. For example, Q1 (SPOT/TOPS), Q9 (STAR/RATS), Q17 (TRAIN/RAIN), Q25 (TAP/PAT), Q105 (SAW/WAS). This is confusing for a 9-year-old because the tip doesn't relate to the question they just answered. Tips are recycled from a pool of ~8, but they should at least be contextually appropriate.
suggestedFix: Either (a) assign tips contextually — double-letter tips only appear on questions with repeated letters (Q3/ODD, Q11/CALL, Q109/ROOF, Q110/TELL, Q111/MUDD, Q117/GOOF, Q118/WINN, Q121/ROOTS, Q123/GREET), or (b) expand the tip pool to 15-20 tips and assign randomly from contextually valid subsets.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Tip references '1793 vs 1973' example on 3-letter code questions
description: The tip 'Similar-looking options (1793 vs 1973) are deliberate traps — check each digit carefully' references a 4-digit example but appears on questions with 3-digit codes (e.g., Q7 CUP=123, Q112 TWIG=WIT). The specific example '1793 vs 1973' is irrelevant to the question format. While the underlying advice is sound, the example creates confusion.
suggestedFix: Use a 3-digit example for 3-letter questions (e.g., '321 vs 312') and keep the 4-digit example for 4-letter questions.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Generic elimination tip references specific digit '3' regardless of question
description: The tip 'Use elimination — if you know the first digit must be 3, cross out options that don't start with 3' always references the digit 3 regardless of the actual question. On Q22 (DOG=214, GOD=412), the first digit of the answer is 4, not 3. On Q40 (HAM=694, MAH=496), the first digit is 4. The tip should be generic or contextual.
suggestedFix: Change to a generic tip: 'Use elimination — work out the first digit of your answer, then cross out any options that start with a different digit.' Remove the specific '3' reference.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q4 rated D3 but is straightforward D2
description: Q4: 'If BANK = 2134 and KNAB = 4312, what is the code for NAB?' is rated difficulty 3. However, KNAB is simply BANK reversed, so the second word adds no new information — it just confirms the mapping. The target word NAB uses only 3 of the 4 letters. This is simpler than many D2 questions that require genuine cross-referencing of two words with overlapping but different letters (e.g., Q77-Q95 pattern). KNAB as a reversal is a trivial check, not a genuine two-word problem.
suggestedFix: Downgrade to D2, or replace the second coded word with one that shares some but not all letters (e.g., BANK=2134 and TANK=5134, then find NAT).
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Several D3 questions are single-word codes with no added complexity
description: Q12 (TIGER=12345, find GRIT), Q14 (LEMON=12345, find MELON), Q15 (PLATE=12345, find TAPE), Q119 (GRAPE=12345, find REAP), Q120 (BLAZE=12345, find ABLE) are all rated D3 but use a single coded word with a simple sequential code (12345). The only complexity is that they use 5-letter words, but the mapping is trivially read off. True D3 should involve either two coded words requiring cross-referencing, or non-sequential codes. These are better classified as D2.
suggestedFix: Either downgrade to D2, or add a second coded word with non-sequential codes to create genuine D3 complexity (matching the Oracle spec for D3: 'two coded words, shared letters to cross-reference, 5-letter words').
subject: verbalreasoning

title: D1 questions Q21-Q44 are highly formulaic
description: Questions 21-44 all follow an identical pattern: 'In a code, XYZ = ABC. What is the code for ZYX?' (or 'What number represents letter Y?'). All use 3-letter words, all use non-sequential codes, and the structural variety is low. While the vocabulary varies (CAT, DOG, PEN, BUS, TAP, etc.), the question format is repetitive. A child doing a quiz would encounter the same mechanical task repeatedly.
suggestedFix: Mix in some varied question formats within D1: (a) 'What word does 321 stand for?' (reverse decoding, currently only in Q29-Q32), (b) 'Which letter does the number 5 represent?' (already in Q33-Q38 but could be interspersed), (c) two 3-letter confirmation words. Shuffle question order so formats alternate.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q114 answer DOLP is not a real English word
description: Q114: 'In a code, PLOD = 4927. What word does 7294 stand for?' The answer is DOLP, which is not a standard English word. While technically the decoding is correct (D=7, O=2, L=9, P=4 → 7294 = DOLP), GL questions always decode to real words. Having a non-word as the answer is unrealistic for the exam format.
suggestedFix: Replace with a question where the decoded answer is a real word. For example, PLOD=4927, what does 7429 stand for? → DPOL (still not a word). Better: change the source word or code. Use POLE=4927 → 7294 = OPEL? Still not standard. Simplest fix: use FOLD=4927 → 7294 = DFOL (no). The issue is that PLOD's letters don't form alternative real words easily. Replace the entire question with different words.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: No lesson covers the 'decode to multiple valid words' trap
description: At D3, a child might decode a number sequence and find it could spell multiple valid words depending on how they read their table. While this is rare in the current question bank, GL does occasionally present options where two plausible real words appear as choices. There is no lesson teaching children how to verify their decoding by checking back against the original coded word.
suggestedFix: Consider adding a sub-concept lesson on 'Always Check Back' — decoding a word and then re-encoding it to verify it produces the original code. This is already mentioned in tips but not formally taught.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Near-duplicate questions: both test sports vs art forms
description: Q36 (football, rugby, painting, cricket, sculpture) and Q101 (football, cricket, painting, rugby, sculpture) use the same 5 words with only the order changed. Both have the same answer (painting + sculpture) and the same group-of-3 (team sports). This wastes a question slot. A child encountering both will get zero additional practice value.
suggestedFix: Replace Q101 with a new D1 question using a different pair of domains — e.g. vegetables vs kitchen utensils, or sea creatures vs land mammals.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Near-duplicate questions: both test days of the week vs months
description: Q28 (Monday, March, Friday, August, Wednesday) and Q104 (January, Monday, March, Friday, April) both test the same concept — days of the week vs months. Same grouping logic, same difficulty. Q104 just swaps which are the majority group. This is functionally the same question.
suggestedFix: Replace one with a fresh D1 question using different time-related categories — e.g. seasons vs compass directions, or AM/PM times vs calendar events.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Duplicate concept: both test 'sonnet + limerick' as odd pair (poetry types vs other domain)
description: Q20 (democracy, sonnet, republic, monarchy, limerick — answer: sonnet + limerick) and Q119 (allegro, sonnet, tempo, limerick, crescendo — answer: sonnet + limerick) both have the identical odd pair of 'sonnet + limerick'. While the group-of-3 differs (government types vs musical terms), a child seeing the same answer pair twice reduces the challenge and variety.
suggestedFix: Change Q119's odd pair to different poem types (e.g. haiku + ballad) or change the domain entirely.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Weak grouping explanation for Q2 — 'furniture you sit at or on' excludes wardrobe but not lamp
