description: Q2 options: table, chair, lamp, sofa, wardrobe. Answer: lamp + wardrobe. Explanation says 'Table, chair, and sofa are all furniture you sit at or on.' But a wardrobe is also furniture — the distinguishing feature is specifically that table/chair/sofa are seating-related furniture. The explanation should make this distinction sharper, as 'furniture you sit at or on' is the correct sub-category, but the phrasing could confuse a child who thinks 'but wardrobe is furniture too'.
suggestedFix: Revise explanation to: 'Table, chair, and sofa are all pieces of furniture designed for sitting. A lamp gives light and a wardrobe stores clothes — neither is for sitting on or at.'
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q1 rated D2 but involves sub-category reasoning that could be D2 or D3
description: Q1 (eagle, sparrow, bat, penguin, robin) is rated D2. The grouping of 'common flying birds' requires knowing that (a) a bat is a mammal, not a bird, and (b) a penguin is a bird that cannot fly. This double-layer reasoning (taxonomy + capability) is more complex than typical D2 category splits. Consider whether this belongs at D3, or at minimum acknowledge that the sub-category reasoning makes this harder than a straightforward split.
suggestedFix: This is a judgement call — the question is well-designed. If keeping at D2, ensure the lesson mapping acknowledges the sub-category complexity. The current mapping to 'red-herring-words' is reasonable but 'category-within-category' might be a better fit.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q9 (oak/daisy/willow/beech/tulip) rated D2 but is straightforward D1
description: Q9 asks to distinguish trees (oak, willow, beech) from flowers (daisy, tulip). Most 9-year-olds can instantly distinguish trees from flowers. This is a concrete, obvious category split more suited to D1 than D2.
suggestedFix: Reclassify from D2 to D1. This is a typical 'plants' sub-category question that children encounter in Year 3-4 science.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q40 explanation says caterpillar is an insect — technically debatable for a caterpillar
description: Q40 (caterpillar, frog, butterfly, toad, bee). Answer: frog + toad. Explanation: 'Caterpillar, butterfly, and bee are all insects.' A caterpillar is the larval stage of a butterfly (a lepidopteran), so calling it an 'insect' is taxonomically correct (it is an insect larva). However, the grouping works perfectly and the answer is unambiguously correct. This is a minor pedantic point but worth noting for scientific accuracy.
suggestedFix: No change needed to the answer. Optionally refine explanation to 'Caterpillar, butterfly, and bee are all insects (a caterpillar is a butterfly larva)' for extra educational value.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Only 10 tip texts rotated across all 125 questions, some contextually mismatched
description: All 125 questions use one of exactly 10 tip strings, rotated in sequence regardless of question content. For example, 'Watch for double meanings' appears on Q6 (instruments vs art supplies), Q14 (length vs weight units), Q22 (furniture vs clothing), Q30 (buildings vs transport) — none of which involve double meanings. Similarly, 'Tip: The two odd words do NOT need to relate to each other' appears on questions where the two odd words DO relate (e.g. Q11 cotton + silk, both fabrics). Tips should be either contextually relevant or omitted.
suggestedFix: Either (a) assign tips based on question characteristics (use 'Watch for double meanings' only when a word genuinely has multiple meanings, use 'sub-category' tip when all 5 seem related), or (b) use a smaller set of universally applicable tips and remove contextually misleading ones.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Double full stop in some tip text
description: Several questions have tip text ending with a double full stop: 'Tip: Name the link specifically. 'All trees' is better than 'all nature things'..' — note the two dots at the end. This appears on Q10, Q18, Q26, Q34, Q42, Q50, Q58, Q66, Q74, Q82, Q90 and more (every 8th question using this tip rotation).
suggestedFix: Remove the trailing extra full stop from the tip text. Change '..' to '.'.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: 1 orphan sub-concept noted in mapping metadata
description: The mapping metadata reports 1 orphan sub-concept. The 'red-herring-words' sub-concept has a lesson but some questions mapped to it (e.g. Q1) might be better served by 'category-within-category'. The lesson teaches avoiding first-instinct traps, which is valuable, but the mapping could be more precise for questions that specifically test sub-category reasoning vs distractor avoidance.
suggestedFix: Review the ~15 questions mapped to 'red-herring-words' and consider whether any are better mapped to 'category-within-category' or 'abstract-categories' for more targeted lesson delivery.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: No questions with vowel answers despite Oracle flagging this as a key D3 pattern
description: The Oracle specification explicitly states: 'D3 (Hard ~30%): vowel answers, strong distractors that work for one pair but fail on the other'. The lesson content teaches vowel answers (sub-concept 'vowel-shared' with worked examples like TE(?)RM = A → TEA+ARM). However, reviewing all 125 questions, none appear to have a vowel as the correct answer. Every answer is a consonant (T, S, N, R, P, B, D, G, W, M, H, K, L). This is a significant gap because vowel-answer questions are genuinely harder and appear on GL papers.
recommendation: Add 5-10 vowel-answer questions at D2-D3 difficulty. Examples from Oracle: SNO(?)ILE / GRA(?)ELL → B (already consonant, wrong example). Better: TE(?)RM / PE(?)ND → A (TEA+ARM, PEA+AND). Also: SE(?)CH / P(?)RT → A (SEA+ACH... no). Need careful construction: FE(?)TH / SP(?)CE → A (FEAT+ATH? no). The lesson already has TE(?)RM example which works.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: 5 of 8 sub-concepts are orphaned — lessons without mapped questions
description: The mapping file shows 5 orphanSubConcepts out of 8 total. This means the majority of lesson content isn't connected to practice questions. Children learn strategies in lessons that they can't directly apply in the subsequent quiz. Only 3 sub-concepts have questions mapped to them.
recommendation: Review each orphaned sub-concept and either: (a) map existing questions to it based on the skill required, or (b) create new questions that specifically target that sub-concept's learning goal.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Tips are repetitive — same 10 tips cycle across all 125 questions
description: Every question uses one of these tips on rotation: 'Start with the most restrictive fragment', 'Always check ALL FOUR words', 'Common answer letters are T, S, N, R', 'If no consonant works, try vowels', 'You only have 5 options', 'Both pairs must work with the SAME letter', 'Look for common word endings (-ANK, -OOL, -EST)', 'Short fragments like SK_ or QU_ are very restrictive', 'Short fragments are very restrictive', 'systematically try each one'. While these are all good tips, the mechanical rotation means children see the same advice repeatedly without progression.
recommendation: Vary tips by difficulty. D1: basic strategy tips. D2: refinement tips specific to the question's challenge. D3: advanced tips like 'Try less common consonants — B, G, K, W, H' or 'Longer fragments mean more letters to check — be systematic'. Also add tips that teach vocabulary ('HELM means the wheel or tiller of a ship').
subject: verbalreasoning

title: T is over-represented as the correct answer, especially in D1
description: In the D1 block (Q1-Q38), the letter T appears as the correct answer disproportionately often: Q1(T), Q7(T), Q13(T), Q20(T), Q21(T), Q22(T), Q23(T), Q24(T), Q28(T). That's 9 out of 38 D1 questions (24%) with answer T. While T is indeed the most common shared letter in English, this over-representation means children who always guess T will score well on D1 without learning the actual skill. GL papers use a wider distribution.
recommendation: Reduce T answers to ~15% of D1 (5-6 out of 38) and increase representation of less common letters like W, G, M, B at D1 level.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Some D1 questions have obviously wrong distractors
description: In several D1 questions, the 5 options include letters that clearly cannot start common words. For example, Q16 AD(?)IG / OD(?)OOR: options are D, B, T, N, S — all are plausible. This is good. But in some questions, the options are so obviously limited that the question becomes trivial. The distractor quality is generally acceptable but could be improved by including letters that work for ONE pair but not the other, testing the crucial 'check all four words' skill.
recommendation: Ensure at least one distractor in each question is a letter that completes ONE word pair successfully but fails on the other. This directly tests the most important exam skill: checking all four words.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: D3 questions could include more uncommon vocabulary to match Oracle specification
description: The Oracle states D3 should include 'Uncommon vocabulary (HELM, SNOB, BILE, SLEW)'. While D3 questions do use longer words (ORBIT, DISTURB, PLASTER, COUNCIL, GALLOP, BISHOP), the resulting words are mostly common English. Adding questions that create less familiar words (like HELM, DIRK, BILE) would better match GL difficulty and build vocabulary.
recommendation: Add 3-5 D3 questions that produce less common but valid words. Oracle examples: CAS(?)NEW / DIR(?)IDS → K (CASK+KNEW, DIRK+KIDS). SNO(?)ILE / GRA(?)ELL → B (SNOB+BILE, GRAB+BELL).
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Lesson interact screen uses visual props that may not match the quiz format
description: The lesson screens use 'shared-pair' mode LetterTiles with format like 'TE(?)RM' and 'P(?)GE'. However, the actual quiz questions present as 'CA ( ? ) EN' with spaces and parentheses. While the concept is the same, children may need a moment to connect the lesson format to the quiz format. Minor consistency issue.
recommendation: Consider aligning lesson visual format with quiz question format so children recognise the pattern immediately when they move from lesson to quiz.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: All 125 answers verified as correct — no wrong answers found
description: Systematic verification of all questions confirmed every answer creates 4 valid English words. Examples verified: Q1 CA(T)EN/BU(T)ANK → CAT+TEN+BUT+TANK ✓, Q89 ORBI(T)RADE/CHAR(T)IGER → ORBIT+TRADE+CHART+TIGER ✓, Q95 ABSOR(B)ENCH/SUPER(B)RAIN → ABSORB+BENCH+SUPERB+BRAIN ✓, Q100 COUNCI(L)OTUS/PENCI(L)UNCH → COUNCIL+LOTUS+PENCIL+LUNCH ✓. This is the strongest answer accuracy of the three topics reviewed.
recommendation: No action needed. This is a positive finding confirming answer quality.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Duplicate word 'challenge' appears in both setA and setB
description: Q97 (absolve + forgive): setA contains ['absolve', 'condemn', 'challenge'] and setB contains ['dispute', 'forgive', 'challenge']. The word 'challenge' appears in both sets. In GL pick-from-sets format, each word should appear in only one set. This is a data integrity issue that could confuse the rendering or the child.
suggestedFix: Replace one instance of 'challenge' with a different distractor. In setB, replace 'challenge' with 'forbid' or 'accuse' to maintain the semantic field.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: 'Soggy' and 'damp' presented as synonyms despite intensity difference
description: Q76 presents soggy + damp as the correct synonym pair, with explanation stating they 'both mean slightly wet'. However, 'soggy' means thoroughly soaked and unpleasant (much wetter), while 'damp' means slightly moist. These differ significantly in intensity — the same intensity trap the app's own lessons warn about (lesson 'common-synonyms-mistake' teaches that deafening is NOT a synonym of loud because it's too strong). By the app's own teaching, soggy is to damp as deafening is to loud. The explanation incorrectly defines 'soggy' as 'slightly wet' when it means 'very wet and soft'.
suggestedFix: Change the correct pair. Either: (a) replace 'soggy' with 'moist' in setA so the pair is moist + damp (true synonyms at the same intensity), or (b) replace 'damp' with 'waterlogged' in setB to pair soggy + waterlogged (both strong-intensity wet).
subject: verbalreasoning

title: 'Valuable' appears in both setA and setB of Q51
description: Q51 (genuine + authentic): setA contains ['genuine', 'fake', 'valuable'] and setB contains ['valuable', 'authentic', 'rare']. The word 'valuable' appears in both sets. This is the same structural issue as Q97. In GL format each word appears in only one group.
suggestedFix: Replace 'valuable' in one set with a different word. In setA, replace with 'precious' or 'original' to maintain variety.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q4 (diligent + hardworking) rated D3 but 'hardworking' is very common
description: Q4 pairs 'diligent' with 'hardworking' and is rated D3. While 'diligent' is a D3-level word, the answer word 'hardworking' is extremely common and obvious once a child knows what 'diligent' means. The distractors (lazy, clumsy, graceful, careless) are from completely different semantic fields, making elimination trivial. Per GL D3 criteria, distractors should be semantically related and highly plausible. This is borderline D2/D3.
suggestedFix: Either upgrade distractors to be more plausible (e.g. replace 'clumsy' and 'careless' with 'ambitious' and 'studious' which are closer to the semantic field of hard work) or reclassify as D2.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: 8 D1 questions have weak distractors from completely unrelated fields
description: Questions Q36-Q44 (D1) use distractors that are entirely from different semantic domains — physical actions vs body sounds (Q35: laugh/giggle with sneeze/yawn/cough/hiccup), physical objects vs actions (Q42: gift/present with stone/path/bridge/cloud). While D1 distractors should be clearly wrong, GL still places them in adjacent semantic fields to test basic discrimination. Having all distractors from completely unrelated domains makes these too easy even for D1.
suggestedFix: Replace some unrelated distractors with words from the same broad category. E.g., for Q42 (gift/present), use 'reward', 'parcel', 'treat', 'souvenir' instead of 'stone', 'path', 'bridge', 'cloud'.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q13 explanation mentions words not in the question options
description: Q13 (laborious + arduous) explanation says: 'Popular' means well-liked, 'straightforward' means easy, 'famous' means well known. But the actual setA is ['laborious', 'enjoyable', 'frequent'] and setB is ['sudden', 'arduous', 'straightforward']. 'Popular' and 'famous' are not in either set. The explanation should only describe words actually present in the question.
suggestedFix: Update explanation to describe the actual words: 'Enjoyable' means pleasant, 'frequent' means happening often, 'sudden' means quick and unexpected, 'straightforward' means easy.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q35 explanation says giggle is a 'lighter laugh' which undermines synonym claim
description: Q35 (laugh + giggle) explanation states: 'A giggle is a lighter, quieter laugh.' This explicitly acknowledges an intensity difference — the same issue the app warns about in the 'spot-the-mistake' lesson (deafening vs loud). If a giggle is 'lighter' than a laugh, they differ in intensity, which contradicts the synonym teaching. The explanation inadvertently teaches the child to accept intensity differences in synonyms.
suggestedFix: Either (a) rephrase the explanation to say 'Laugh and giggle both mean to make sounds showing amusement or fun' without mentioning the intensity difference, or (b) replace the pair with laugh + chuckle which are closer in intensity.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Only 1 lesson for context-dependent synonyms despite being a key GL skill
description: The 'Same Word, Different Synonyms' sub-concept has only 1 lesson but covers a critical GL skill: polysemy (words with multiple meanings needing different synonyms depending on context). GL tests this heavily in comprehension passages. The single lesson teaches 'light' (brightness vs weight) but doesn't practise other common polysemy examples (bank, bat, run, match, point, etc.). No questions in the bank explicitly test context-dependent synonym selection.
suggestedFix: Add a second lesson with more examples of polysemous words, and consider creating questions that provide sentence context to test context-dependent synonym selection.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: No noun synonym pairs tested
description: The question bank covers adjective synonyms (big/large, ancient/old), verb synonyms (conceal/hide, flourish/thrive), and formal-informal pairs extensively, but pure noun synonym pairs are underrepresented. Only a few questions test noun pairs (gift/present, error/mistake, centre/middle). GL tests nouns like voyage/journey, pupil/student, automobile/car, liberty/freedom.
suggestedFix: Add more noun synonym pairs at each difficulty level, especially abstract nouns (liberty/freedom, courage/bravery, sorrow/grief) which appear at D2/D3 in GL.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: 'Silent' and 'quiet' are not exact synonyms
description: Q29 pairs 'silent' with 'quiet' as synonyms. 'Silent' means making no sound at all (absolute), while 'quiet' means making little sound (relative). A silent room has zero noise; a quiet room has low noise. This is a gradient difference, not a perfect synonym match. While acceptable at D1 level where approximate matches are common, it's worth noting for precision.
suggestedFix: No change required — acceptable at D1. If desired for precision, change 'quiet' to 'noiseless' or 'soundless' for a tighter match, or change the pair to 'silent + hushed'.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: correctPair position shows slight bias toward [0,1]
description: Across the 125 questions, the correct answer pair position shows a pattern: the correct word in setA is very frequently at position 0, and the correct word in setB is frequently at position 1. While GL distributes answer positions roughly evenly, having a detectable pattern allows children to develop position-based guessing strategies rather than vocabulary skills.
suggestedFix: Audit correctPair distribution and shuffle word positions within sets to ensure even distribution across all 9 possible position combinations ([0,0], [0,1], [0,2], [1,0], [1,1], [1,2], [2,0], [2,1], [2,2]).
subject: verbalreasoning

title: 1 orphan sub-concept has questions but limited lesson coverage
description: The mappings show 1 orphan sub-concept. The 'hard-vocabulary' sub-concept maps to D3 questions like 'laborious + arduous', 'gregarious + sociable', 'clandestine + secret'. While these questions have mapped lessons teaching elimination strategy, the lessons don't actually teach the specific vocabulary words children need. The lesson for 'hard-vocabulary' uses 'diligent' as its example but doesn't systematically teach the Latin/Greek roots (mal-, bene-, greg-, etc.) that would help children decode unfamiliar D3 words.
suggestedFix: Add root-word teaching to the hard-vocabulary lessons. For example, teach that 'greg-' means flock/group (gregarious), 'mal-' means bad (malevolent), 'bene-' means good (benevolent). This matches the Oracle's recommendation for vocabulary teaching (see Key Prefixes & Roots section).
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q78 and Q103 are identical questions with identical answers
description: Both questions ask '___ is to cow as lamb is to ___' with the answer 'calf + sheep'. Q78 (D1) and Q103 (D1) are exact duplicates — same question text, same answer, same difficulty. The only difference is one distractor word (bull vs barn in setA). This wastes a question slot in a 125-question bank.
suggestedFix: Delete Q103 and replace with a different young-adult pair at D1. Consider 'piglet + pig', 'duckling + duck', or 'kid + goat'.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q11 and Q122 test identical answer pair 'conductor + team'
description: Q11 (D3) asks '___ is to orchestra as captain is to ___' with answer 'conductor + team'. Q122 (D3) asks the identical question '___ is to orchestra as captain is to ___' with the same answer. These are exact duplicates at the same difficulty level. Different distractor words (musician/violin/ship/army vs violin/concert/ship/badge) but the core question and answer are identical.
suggestedFix: Delete Q122 and replace with a different leader-to-group pair at D3. Consider 'headteacher + school', 'principal + institution', or 'admiral + fleet'.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q34 and Q112 test identical answer pair 'leaf + flower'
description: Q34 (D1) and Q112 (D2) both ask '___ is to tree as petal is to ___' with the answer 'leaf + flower'. The question text is identical. The only difference is the distractor words in the sets and the difficulty rating (D1 vs D2). Having the same question text and answer twice is wasteful.
suggestedFix: Delete Q112 and replace with a different part-whole pair at D2. Consider 'pixel + screen', 'carriage + train' (but Q42 already uses this), or 'stanza + poem'.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q80 and Q123 test the same caterpillar/butterfly + tadpole/frog pairing
description: Q80 (D2) asks '___ is to frog as caterpillar is to ___' (tadpole + butterfly). Q123 (D3) asks '___ is to caterpillar as frog is to ___' (butterfly + tadpole). While the direction is reversed, these test identical knowledge — a child who knows the metamorphosis pairs will get both right or both wrong. The reversal does not genuinely increase difficulty.
suggestedFix: Replace Q123 with a different young-adult pair at D3. Consider 'leveret + hare', 'kit + fox', or 'squab + pigeon' — these use less common baby animal names appropriate for D3.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q35: a key is NOT part of a lock — it operates a lock
description: Q35 asks '___ is to clock as key is to ___' with answer 'hand + lock'. The explanation says 'A hand is part of a clock, just as a key is part of a lock.' The first pair is correct (a hand IS part of a clock), but a key is NOT part of a lock — it is a separate object that operates/opens a lock. This is a tool-to-object relationship, not part-to-whole. A child who correctly understands part-whole would be confused because the two pairs use different relationship types, making the analogy structurally flawed.
suggestedFix: Change the answer to maintain a consistent part-whole relationship. Options: (1) Change setB to include 'lock' as a distractor and 'keyboard' as the answer (a key is part of a keyboard), which matches 'hand is part of a clock'. (2) Keep 'lock' but change the explanation to acknowledge it's a tool-function relationship, and reclassify both pairs. Option 1 is cleaner.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q105: an actor does not 'create' a film — they perform in it
description: Q105 asks '___ is to book as actor is to ___' with answer 'author + film'. The explanation says 'An author creates a book, just as an actor performs in a film. Both are creators in their field.' This is contradictory — the explanation itself admits the relationship is 'creates' vs 'performs in', which are different relationships. An author creates a book (creator-creation), but an actor performs in a film (performer-medium). This is mapped to 'Creator to Creation' which only matches the first pair.
suggestedFix: Replace with a genuine creator-creation pair. Change to '___ is to book as director is to ___' (author + film) since a director creates a film. Or change to '___ is to book as composer is to ___' (author + symphony).
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q56: a telescope magnifies, but it primarily allows viewing distant objects — 'observe' is equally valid
description: Q56 asks '___ is to measure as telescope is to ___' with answer 'ruler + magnify'. While a telescope does magnify, its primary function is to observe distant objects. The word 'magnify' more precisely describes a microscope's function. The explanation says 'a telescope is used to magnify' but most would say 'a telescope is used to observe' or 'to view distant objects'. Note that Q62 already uses 'microscope + enlarge' which is the proper magnification pairing.
suggestedFix: Change setB to include 'observe' instead of 'magnify', and update the answer to 'ruler + observe'. This creates a cleaner parallel: a ruler is a tool used to measure, a telescope is a tool used to observe.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q12 (bark/dog :: meow/cat) mapped to Part-Whole but is actually object-characteristic/animal-sound
description: Q12 asks '___ is to dog as meow is to ___' (bark + cat). This is an animal-to-sound relationship: a dog makes a bark, a cat makes a meow. It is mapped to 'Part to Whole' but bark is not PART of a dog — it is the SOUND a dog makes. The lesson teaches 'a page is part of a book' which is not the same relationship type at all.
suggestedFix: Remap to a more appropriate sub-concept. If no animal-sound sub-concept exists, create one or map to the closest existing concept. Alternatively, map to 'Category to Example' or create a dedicated 'Object to Characteristic' lesson.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q2 (glove/hand :: helmet/head) mapped to Part-Whole but is actually object-function (protection)
description: Q2 asks '___ is to hand as helmet is to ___' (glove + head). The explanation says 'A glove covers and protects a hand, just as a helmet covers and protects a head.' This is an object-to-function/protection relationship, not part-whole. A glove is not PART of a hand. The Part-Whole lesson teaches 'a page is part of a book' which is a completely different relationship.
suggestedFix: Remap to 'Tool to User' (closest existing match, as these are protective items matched to what they protect) or create a new sub-concept for 'Object to Function' relationships.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Male-female pairs (Q15, Q118) mapped to Synonym & Antonym Pairs — no matching lesson exists
description: Q15 (nephew/uncle :: niece/aunt) and Q118 (queen/king :: duchess/duke) are male-to-female equivalent relationships. They are mapped to 'Synonym & Antonym Pairs' which teaches about words meaning the same or opposite. But nephew is not a synonym or antonym of uncle — it's the male counterpart relationship. The lesson content about hot/cold opposites does not prepare the child for this relationship type.
suggestedFix: Either create a dedicated 'Male to Female' sub-concept (it's one of the Oracle's 22 listed relationship types), or acknowledge in the Synonym-Antonym lesson that gender pairs follow a similar pattern. The Oracle explicitly lists 'Male-Female' as relationship type #11.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Degree/intensity questions (Q63-Q70) mapped to Synonym & Antonym Pairs — lesson doesn't teach this
description: Eight questions testing degree/intensity relationships (warm/boiling, whisper/shout, annoyed/furious, hungry/starving, drizzle/downpour, nervous/terrified, old/ancient, flurry/blizzard) are all mapped to 'Synonym & Antonym Pairs'. The lesson teaches about synonyms and antonyms (same/opposite meaning) but degree/intensity is a different relationship type — it's about matching the SCALE of increase, not identifying synonyms or antonyms. The Oracle lists 'Degree/intensity (mild to extreme)' as relationship type #3, distinct from synonyms (#1) and antonyms (#2).
suggestedFix: Create a dedicated 'Degree & Intensity' sub-concept and lesson. This is a common GL relationship type that deserves its own teaching — children need to understand matching the jump in intensity (mild→extreme must pair with mild→extreme, not mild→moderate).
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Place-activity questions (Q88-Q92) mapped to Category to Example — not the best fit
description: Q88 (school/learn), Q89 (pool/swim), Q90 (church/worship), Q91 (theatre/act), Q92 (laboratory/experiment) are place-to-activity relationships. They are mapped to 'Category to Example' which teaches 'rose is a type of flower'. A school is not a TYPE of learning — it is a PLACE where learning happens. While the mapping works at a stretch, the lesson content doesn't teach this relationship.
suggestedFix: Consider creating a 'Place to Activity' sub-concept, or expand the Category to Example lesson to mention place-activity as a variant pattern.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Leader-to-group questions mapped to 'Tool to User' — misleading lesson alignment
description: Q4 (doctor/patient :: teacher/pupil) and Q11/Q122 (conductor/orchestra :: captain/team) are leader/professional-to-group relationships. They are mapped to 'Tool to User' which teaches 'a writer uses a pen'. A doctor is not a TOOL, and a conductor is not a TOOL. The lesson says 'This pattern links a tool or instrument to the person who uses it' — but these questions link a professional to who they serve/lead. A child studying the Tool-User lesson would not learn to solve these questions.
suggestedFix: Create a 'Professional to Domain' or 'Leader to Group' sub-concept, or expand the Tool-User lesson to explicitly cover professional-to-role relationships alongside tool-to-user ones.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Tip about degree relationships appears on questions that have nothing to do with degree/intensity
description: The tip 'For degree relationships (warm to hot), match the INTENSITY jump' appears on 11 questions including part-whole (Q8: wheel/car, Q40: bone/skeleton), young-adult (Q80: tadpole/frog), cause-effect (Q96: moisture/rust), and tool-user (Q72: photographer/camera) questions. These tips cycle through a fixed rotation regardless of question type, which is confusing — a child doing a part-whole question is told to think about intensity jumps.
suggestedFix: Match tips to question relationship types. Part-whole questions should have part-whole tips, cause-effect should have cause-effect tips, etc. At minimum, make the tips relevant to the broad category or use a generic analogy-solving tip.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q50: 'raptor' is an unusual category term — 'bird of prey' is the standard GL-level vocabulary
description: Q50 asks '___ is to reptile as eagle is to ___' with answer 'snake + raptor'. While 'raptor' is technically correct (it means bird of prey), this is uncommon vocabulary for a 9-10 year old and not standard GL terminology. Most children would expect 'bird' as the category for eagle (which is the more direct parallel to 'snake is to reptile'). The word 'raptor' may be associated with dinosaurs (Jurassic Park) more than birds for this age group. 'Bird' is the equivalent level of category to 'reptile'.
suggestedFix: Change the answer to 'snake + bird' and update setB to include 'bird' instead of or alongside 'raptor'. This creates a cleaner parallel: snake is a type of reptile, eagle is a type of bird. Both are member-to-category at the same hierarchical level.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q51: 'leafy vegetable' as a set option is a two-word phrase — inconsistent with single-word format
description: Q51 has 'leafy vegetable' as an option in setB. All other options across all 125 questions are single words. A two-word phrase stands out and effectively gives away the answer because it's the only option that functions as a category label. The pick-from-sets format works best with single-word options.
suggestedFix: Replace 'leafy vegetable' with 'vegetable' (simpler, single word, maintains the same relationship). Update the explanation accordingly.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q74: 'multimeter' is specialist vocabulary unlikely to be known by most 9-10 year olds
description: Q74 asks '___ is to microscope as electrician is to ___' with answer 'scientist + multimeter'. While 'multimeter' is the correct specialist tool, it is very unlikely to be in the vocabulary of a 9-10 year old, even at D2 difficulty. Most GL questions at D2 use words children would have encountered through reading or school. This is rated D2 but the vocabulary is arguably D3 or beyond.
suggestedFix: Either upgrade to D3 difficulty, or replace with a more recognisable tool. Consider 'electrician + wire stripper' (still specialist but more intuitive) or change the profession entirely: '___ is to microscope as builder is to ___' (scientist + hammer).
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Several GL-tested relationship types have zero or minimal coverage
description: The Oracle lists 22 relationship types for verbal analogies. Of these, the following have zero questions: country associations (e.g. France/French, Japan/yen), container-contents (e.g. wallet/money, vase/flowers), symbol/representation (e.g. dove/peace, crown/royalty), sequence/order (e.g. Monday/Tuesday, spring/summer), grammatical form changes (e.g. run/running, mouse/mice). Collective nouns have only 2 questions (Q9: flock/sheep, Q109: bird/flock). These are all types that appear in GL papers and the gap means children won't practise them.
suggestedFix: Add 5-10 questions per missing relationship type, distributed across D1-D3. Priority order: (1) collective nouns (very common in GL), (2) country associations, (3) container-contents, (4) sequence/order. This would require expanding from 125 to ~145-155 questions.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q88 explanation is confused, self-contradictory, and rambling
description: Q88 explanation starts 'The pattern removes the first letter to leave a 3-letter word: S-EAT→EAT? The question says (SEAT, ATE).' Then proceeds to question itself: 'Let me recheck. A-T-E are the 2nd, 4th, 3rd letters of SEAT.' The explanation internally debates multiple interpretations, admits confusion ('Hmm'), and eventually settles on 'remove the first letter' which gives EAT not ATE as stated in the question. This is a stream-of-consciousness debugging log, not a child-facing explanation. The answer RIP is correct (TRIP with T removed = RIP), but the explanation is unacceptable for a 9-year-old.
suggestedFix: Replace entire explanation with: 'The pattern removes the first letter: SEAT→EAT, CLAM→LAM, TRIP→RIP. Note: the first pair in the question shows (SEAT, ATE) which appears to be a typo — it should be (SEAT, EAT). The rule is simply: remove the first letter. ✓' Also fix the question stem if possible — change (SEAT, ATE) to (SEAT, EAT) for consistency, or update to use a different example pair.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q95 uses an inconsistent rule across the two example pairs — makes pattern uncrackable
description: Q95 states: 'GRAPE → DRAPE, GLOW → BLOW. What does GRAIN become?' GRAPE→DRAPE changes G to D. GLOW→BLOW changes G to B. These are different substitutions (G→D vs G→B), so there is no consistent 'rule' a child can discover and apply. The explanation itself acknowledges this inconsistency: 'GRAPE→DRAPE changes G to D, and GLOW→BLOW changes G to B. Hmm, those are different.' It then handwaves to 'the answer BRAIN fits replacing G with B, matching GLOW→BLOW'. A GL paper would never present an inconsistent rule — the child must be able to derive one unambiguous pattern from the examples.
suggestedFix: Fix the example pairs to use a consistent rule. Either: (1) 'GRAPE → DRAPE, GLOW → DLOW... no, DLOW isn't a word. Try: (2) 'BLOW → GLOW, BRAIN → GRAIN. What does BLACK become?' → GLACK (not a word). Better: (3) 'GRAPE → DRAPE, GRIN → DRIN... not a word.' The issue is that changing G to a specific letter only works when the result is a real word. Best fix: replace entirely with a consistent pair like 'CLAP → FLAP, COLD → FOLD. What does CASE become?' (FASE — not a word). Actually, use: 'BAKE → CAKE, BOLD → COLD. What does BURN become?' (CURL — hmm). Finding consistent letter-swap pairs where all three results are real words is hard. Consider: 'BARN → YARN, BELL → YELL. What does BEST become?' → YEST (not great). Alternative: 'HEAT → SEAT, HILL → SILL. What does HIT become?' → SIT. This works.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q100 explanation is a confused debugging log — not suitable for children
description: Q100 presents 'SHORE → HORSE, PLUM → LUMP. What does CAFE become?' The explanation is a 400-word internal debate analysing different transformation hypotheses, acknowledging failures ('That's not HORSE', 'AFEC isn't a word'), and never arriving at a clear rule. It concludes with 'The simplest common pattern is that they are anagrams'. The answer FACE is correct (CAFE rearranged), but: (1) SHORE→HORSE and PLUM→LUMP follow different rearrangement patterns (not the same positional mapping), and (2) the explanation is completely inappropriate for a child. This is the same issue as Q88 — stream-of-consciousness reasoning left in the explanation field.
suggestedFix: Replace explanation with a clear, concise version: 'The pattern rearranges the letters to make a new word (an anagram): SHORE→HORSE, PLUM→LUMP, CAFE→FACE. Each output uses all the same letters in a different order. ✓' However, the question itself is problematic because the rearrangement rule is not systematic (different positional mappings for each pair). Consider replacing with a question that has a clear, consistent transformation rule.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q97 explanation is a debugging log with incorrect analysis
description: Q97 presents 'PEACH → CHEAP, NIGHT → THING. What does ANGEL become?' The explanation is another stream-of-consciousness analysis: 'the last two letters (C,H) move to the front... That gives CHPEA, not CHEAP. Actually, looking more carefully...' It concludes 'Both are anagrams where the letters cycle: positions shift. PEACH→CHEAP takes position 4,5,2,3,1. NIGHT→THING takes position 5,4,2,1,3. These are different mappings, so these are simply anagrams.' The answer GLEAN is correct, but the explanation is unusable for a child.
suggestedFix: Replace with: 'The pattern rearranges all the letters: PEACH→CHEAP, NIGHT→THING, ANGEL→GLEAN. Each word is an anagram of the input. ✓' Also review whether anagram questions belong in this topic — the Oracle spec for Word Code Analogies focuses on systematic letter operations, not arbitrary rearrangements. Anagrams might fit better in a dedicated anagram/word puzzle category.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q1 rule description is misleading — 'changing the first letter' is ambiguous
description: Q1 says 'big (dig) → cat (__). The word in brackets is made by changing the first letter.' The explanation says 'changing the first letter b to d gives dig. Following the same pattern for cat: we need to change c to b (the letter before it) giving bat.' But the rule 'change first letter' doesn't specify HOW to change it. b→d is +2 in the alphabet, but c→b is -1. If the child applies +2 (like the example), cat→eat. If they apply -1, cat→bat. The explanation claims the answer is bat, but the rule as described is ambiguous. A proper analogy should have one unambiguous interpretation.
suggestedFix: Rewrite Q1 to make the transformation rule clear and consistent. Either: (1) state the specific change: 'big(dig): the first letter moved forward by 2. Apply the same to cat → eat' or (2) use a consistent letter-change: 'big(pig): the first letter changed to P. Apply to cat → pat.' The current version teaches sloppy pattern recognition.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q4 example pair (SEAT, ATE) doesn't match the stated rule
description: Q4 presents '(SEAT, ATE) (CLAM, LAM) (TRIP, ___)'. The explanation says 'remove the first letter, take the last 3 letters'. But SEAT minus S = EAT, not ATE. The pair (SEAT, ATE) is wrong — it should be (SEAT, EAT). (CLAM, LAM) works: C removed = LAM. (TRIP, RIP) works: T removed = RIP. The answer RIP is correct for 'remove first letter', but the first example pair contradicts the rule. Q88 repeats this same incorrect pair.
suggestedFix: Change (SEAT, ATE) to (SEAT, EAT) in both Q4 and Q88. Or use a different word pair where removing the first letter works cleanly, e.g., (PLATE, LATE) or (BRAIN, RAIN).
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q66 answer 'lade' — unusual word for a 9-year-old at D1
description: Q66 asks '(plate, late) (train, rain) (blade, ___)'. The rule is remove first two letters: plate→late, train→rain, blade→lade. The answer 'lade' is technically correct but is an archaic/rare word meaning 'to load'. Most 9-year-olds would not know this word. For a D1 question, all answers should be common, recognisable words. This creates confusion — a child might doubt their answer because 'lade' doesn't look right, even though the pattern is clear.
suggestedFix: Replace with a word where removing the first two letters gives a common word. Options: (blame, lame), (plane, lane), (block, lock), (glove, love). Example: '(plate, late) (train, rain) (plane, ___)' → 'lane'.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Letter-shift and letter-pair-series questions do not belong in Word Code Analogies
description: Several questions test skills that belong in Letter Codes or Letter Pair Series, not Word Code Analogies: Q3 ('If each letter is moved forward by 1, what does FISH become?') is a letter code question. Q9 (A1,B2,C3,D4,___) is a letter-number series. Q13 (ZA,YB,XC,WD,___) is a letter pair series. Q16 (AB,CD,EF,GH,___) is a letter pair series. Q20 (AAB,BBC,CCD,DDE,___) is a letter series. Q111 (AZ,BY,CX,DW,___) is a letter pair series. Q115 (Z1,X2,V3,T4,___) is a letter-number series. These questions test alphabet position shifts or sequential patterns, not word transformations. Placing them here confuses the topic boundary and undermines lesson mapping (these questions map poorly to word-transformation lessons).
suggestedFix: Move these 7 questions to their correct topics: Q3 → Letter Codes, Q9/Q13/Q16/Q111/Q115 → Letter Pair Series, Q20 → Letter Pair Series. This would also help explain the high rate of low-confidence mappings (45 out of 125).
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q92 uses a pattern that is not uniquely determinable from the examples
description: Q92 states 'PAN → PLAN, TEN → THEN. What does BAN become?' The rule is described as 'inserts a consonant after the first letter'. But PAN→PLAN inserts L, and TEN→THEN inserts H. There's no systematic way to predict which consonant gets inserted. The answer BRAN inserts R. A child cannot deduce 'insert R' from seeing 'insert L' and 'insert H' — these are arbitrary. In GL papers, the rule must be deterministic and consistently applicable.
suggestedFix: Replace with a consistent insertion pattern. Example: 'PAN → PLAN, LAY → PLAY. What does ACE become?' → PLACE (always insert PL before the word). Or use a consistent suffix: 'BAN → BAND, TEN → TEND. What does MEN become?' → MEND (always add D).
subject: verbalreasoning

title: 45 low-confidence mappings (36%) — highest rate of any reviewed topic
description: The mapping file shows 48 high, 32 medium, and 45 low confidence mappings. The 45 low-confidence mappings indicate that over a third of questions don't map well to the existing lesson sub-concepts. This is likely caused by the topic mixing two distinct question types (word transformations and number codes) and including misclassified letter-series/letter-code questions. Poor mapping means children get lessons that don't prepare them for the questions they'll face.
suggestedFix: After resolving VR-WCA-008 (moving misclassified questions to correct topics) and potentially splitting this topic, re-run the mapping process. The remaining word-transformation questions should map much more cleanly to the letter-swap, letter-removal, and letter-replacement sub-concepts.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: D1 count (31 questions, 25%) is below GL target of 30%
description: Only 25% of questions are rated D1 versus the GL target of approximately 30%. This means fewer easy confidence-building questions. D1 questions should use single obvious operations on short words — the current D1 set covers this well but needs more volume.
suggestedFix: Add 6-8 D1 questions covering simple patterns: remove first letter (3-letter words), add -s for plural, add -er for comparative, simple 3-letter reversal. This would bring D1 to ~30%.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Q94 explanation claims a vowel sequence pattern (A→E→I) that isn't reliable
description: Q94 presents '(BEAT, BEET) (MAST, MIST) (BAND, ___)'. The explanation claims: 'Following the vowel sequence A→E→I, the next change is A→E. BAND→BEND.' But A→E→I is not a sequence being 'followed' — the first pair changes A to E, the second changes A to I. If there were a third pair, the pattern A→E, A→I would suggest A→O (next vowel). But the answer is BEND (A→E again). The explanation invents a non-existent sequential pattern. The actual rule is simpler: the second letter (a vowel) changes. BEAT→BEET (A→E), MAST→MIST (A→I). Both change 'a' in position 2 to a different vowel. For BAND, changing A to E gives BEND. This works but the 'A→E→I sequence' reasoning in the explanation is misleading.
suggestedFix: Rewrite explanation: 'The middle vowel changes: BEAT→BEET (A→E), MAST→MIST (A→I). Each pair changes the A to a different vowel. BAND with A changed to E = BEND. ✓' Remove the false 'vowel sequence' claim.
subject: verbalreasoning

title: Two orphan sub-concepts have lessons but no mapped questions
description: The mapping metadata shows 2 orphan sub-concepts. These have lessons but no questions map to them. Children learn skills in micro-lessons but never practise them in quizzes.
suggestedFix: Review the 2 orphan sub-concepts. Either map existing questions to them or create new questions. After resolving the topic-splitting question (VR-WCA-008), this may resolve naturally.
subject: verbalreasoning
